operation.description: Increase the quantity and coverage of street-level vegetation in areas identified with low GVI values (affecting 75% of the city area) through strategic planting of trees, shrubs, and vertical greenery along streets, sidewalks, and building facades to enhance visible greenery from pedestrian perspective.

pathway.mechanism_description: Adding foreground vegetation increases the proportion of green pixels captured in street-level imagery and thereby raises GVI scores. Street trees and eye-level greenery have the strongest leverage on GVI because they occupy larger angular portions of the street view than middle- or background elements. Improvements in GVI translate into more visible greenery for pedestrians, consistent with the descriptive evidence on city-wide GVI distribution; broader environmental co-benefits are beyond what the linked descriptive evidence directly supports.

signatures.mechanism: Adding street trees directly increases canopy volume and leaf area visible at eye-level, which is the dominant contributor to GVI pixel classification. Excerpt [1] confirms GVI correlates strongly with canopy coverage close to sample sites, and excerpt [6] notes street trees reduce pedestrian oppressiveness, indicating their foreground visual dominance.

Adding shrubs along sidewalks fills the lower portion of the eye-level visual field, supplementing canopy-tree coverage in the GVI segmentation. Excerpt [13] explicitly recommends 'planting grass or shrubs instead of tall trees' to alleviate stress, and excerpt [3] notes streetscape grasses/low vegetation have strong mental health associations for older adults.

Adding vertical greenery on building facades converts hard vertical surfaces (classified as 'building' in semantic segmentation) into vegetation pixels in street-view imagery, directly raising GVI. Excerpt [4] notes that buildings and trees together form street enclosure; replacing bare facade area with green cover shifts pixel classification toward vegetation without altering street geometry.

scope.description: This S2 superposition operation coordinates three foreground vegetation additions—SIG_ADD_VEG_FG_SIZ (canopy_tree, primary), SIG_ADD_VEG_FG_SIZ (shrub, secondary), and SIG_ADD_VEG_FG_SIZ (climbing_plant, secondary)—to jointly raise the Green View Index (IND_GVI) linked to evidence SVCs_P_Zhang2022_2. The evidence reports that 75.59% of Zhanjiang's street sample points fall within a low GVI range, indicating a city-wide deficit in eye-level visible greenery. The three signatures act in superposition: canopy trees dominate the upper visual field, shrubs fill the lower visual field, and climbing plants convert vertical built surfaces into green pixels, together maximizing the proportion of vegetation pixels captured by semantic segmentation of street-view images. This multi-layer stacking within the same depth zone amplifies GVI more effectively than any single vegetation type alone. The performance dimension is PRF_SOC, subdimension PRS_SOC_EQTY; the expected outcome direction is an upward shift in GVI distribution across the city, reducing the share of low-GVI areas and improving equity in residents' exposure to visible street greenery, which the paper associates with mental health benefits (excerpts [15], [17]).
scope_validation_notes: Scope structure is internally consistent with signature_count and phase_count.

operation.description: Replace solid perimeter walls with transparent or semi-transparent railing fences, open metal grilles, or permeable facade systems to allow visual penetration and reveal interior green vegetation to street-level viewers.

pathway.mechanism_description: Replacing opaque boundary walls with transparent enclosures increases visual connectivity between private/semi-private greenspaces and public streetscapes. This changes the texture of foreground barriers from solid to permeable, allowing previously hidden vegetation to contribute to street-level Green View Index. The enhanced visual penetration expands the effective green viewing field by incorporating background greenery, thereby increasing GVI values, consistent with the positive association reported between facade enclosure type and GVI.

signatures.mechanism: Replacing solid, opaque perimeter walls with transparent or semi-transparent alternatives (railing fences, open metal grilles, permeable facade systems) changes the surface permeability pattern of the enclosure element. This shifts the Facade Enclosure Type from visually impervious to visually penetrable, removing the physical barrier that previously blocked sightlines to interior green vegetation, thereby increasing the proportion of visible greenery captured at eye level.

scope.description: A single-signature intervention SIG_REP_BLT_FG_TEX that replaces the texture/permeability pattern of foreground perimeter walls from solid to transparent or semi-transparent systems. Linked evidence SVCs_P_Zhu2022_2 demonstrates that the indicator Facade Enclosure Type (IND_FAC_ENC) mediates the amount of street-side greenery perceived by pedestrians: streets bounded by solid enclosed exterior walls consistently yield low GVI, whereas railing-wall designs and open boundaries allow visual access to affiliated green spaces inside blocks (AGSB). By substituting opaque walls with permeable enclosures, the intervention positively shifts IND_FAC_ENC toward openness, which in turn increases GVI—the outcome measure operating within the performance dimension PRF_AES (Aesthetic Preference), subdimension PRS_AES_NATU, with an expected positive relationship direction (DIR_POS). The existing interior vegetation is not itself modified; only the enclosure barrier is replaced, enabling latent greenery to register in the pedestrian visual field.

operation.description: Select and prioritize evergreen tree species (e.g., conifers, broadleaf evergreens) in the middleground layer to maintain consistent green coloration and visual continuity throughout seasonal transitions, particularly during winter dormancy periods when deciduous vegetation loses foliage

pathway.mechanism_description: Evergreen species retain foliage year-round, preserving the greenery-dominated visual character that defines stable visual walkability types across seasons. By maintaining consistent canopy color (VAR_COL) in the middle-ground view zone, the visual environment remains recognizable and predictable, preventing seasonal shifts from greenery-dominated to built-dominated or sky-dominated visual walkability categories. This stabilization supports consistent wayfinding and aesthetic continuity in urban walking environments.

signatures.mechanism: Substituting deciduous tree species with evergreen species (conifers, broadleaf evergreens) ensures that the dominant foliage colour remains a consistent green throughout autumn–winter, preventing the seasonal shift from green canopy to bare brown/grey branching that causes the greenery indicator pixel ratio to collapse and triggers a Type A → Type B visual walkability transition.

Evergreen species retain full leaf mass year-round, so the total amount of visible vegetation pixels (Tn + Gn in the greenery formula) remains stable across seasons rather than dropping sharply in winter; this prevents the compensatory rise in sky-pixel openness that characterises the Type A-to-openness-dominated transition documented in the evidence.

scope.description: This is an SCP_S2 superposition operation combining SIG_REP_VEG_MG_COL (primary) and SIG_REP_VEG_MG_SIZ (secondary) acting on the same object in the same depth zone. Both signatures address the performance outcome documented in evidence SVCs_P_Wang2024_8, which uses the Type of Visual Walkability indicator (IND_TVW) under the walkability-and-use dimension (PRF_USE / PRS_WAL_PERC). The evidence shows that greenery-dominated streets (Type A) seasonally transition to openness-dominated streets (Type B) from autumn to winter because deciduous canopy loses foliage, reducing the greenery pixel ratio while increasing the sky-view openness ratio. SIG_REP_VEG_MG_COL directly stabilises the green chromaticity of the middleground canopy layer so that the visual scene retains its greenery-dominated character. SIG_REP_VEG_MG_SIZ simultaneously stabilises the volumetric leaf-area presence, preventing the drop in the greenery index (Tn + Gn / Sum) that triggers reclassification to a different TVW type. Together, the two signatures are expected to maintain stable Type A visual walkability classification across all four seasons (the DIR_NA relationship becomes effectively positive stability), thereby improving perceived walkability continuity year-round.
scope_validation_notes: Scope structure is internally consistent with signature_count and phase_count.

operation.description: Increase pavement area allocation within street cross-sections by widening sidewalk zones and reducing vehicular lane width, thereby increasing the pavement-to-road pixel ratio in street view imagery to create pedestrian-prioritized thoroughfares

pathway.mechanism_description: Expanding pavement area in the street cross-section increases the visual presence of walkable surfaces in the foreground view and therefore raises the Visual Pavement Index (VPI), which the study uses as a descriptive indicator of space available for pedestrians. Whether this geometric change also encourages more active pedestrian use is plausible but not directly demonstrated by the descriptive evidence linked here.

signatures.mechanism: Widening sidewalk zones increases the pavement pixel count (Pn) in the Visual Pavement Index formula (Di = Pn / (Pn + Rn)), directly raising the numerator and the overall VPI ratio, thereby registering greater pedestrian-allocated space in street-view imagery.

Reducing vehicular lane width decreases the road pixel count (Rn) in the VPI denominator (Pn + Rn), amplifying the pavement-to-road ratio shift initiated by the sidewalk widening and reinforcing the visual dominance of pedestrian surfaces.

scope.description: This S2 coordinated intervention employs a deformation pattern that reshapes the street cross-section through two simultaneous ground-plane modifications: SIG_MOD_GRD_FG_SIZ (sidewalk, increase) and SIG_MOD_GRD_FG_SIZ (road, decrease). Together they act on the indicator measured by evidence SVCs_P_Wang2024_6—the Visual Pavement Index (IND_VPI), defined as Di = Pn/(Pn + Rn). The primary signature increases Pn while the secondary signature decreases Rn, jointly elevating VPI. This targets the performance dimension PRF_USE (Spatial use and physical activity), subdimension PRS_WAL_PERC, aiming to increase the space available for pedestrians. Although the linked evidence reports only descriptive statistics (DIR_NA, Table 3) across urban contexts—noting that pavement-dominated streets (Type C) concentrate in high-density residential areas and urban centres—the intervention logic is that a higher VPI corresponds to pedestrian-prioritized thoroughfares with greater walking comfort and spatial allocation.

operation.description: Increase the quantity and canopy coverage of street trees in the foreground viewing zone to enhance Green View Index (GVI) at eye-level perspective, strategically placing trees along sidewalks and street edges to maximize visible greenery while managing sky view factor.

pathway.mechanism_description: Adding foreground trees increases the proportion of vegetation pixels in street-level imagery and elevates the Green View Index. Larger canopy coverage also balances visual composition by reducing excessive sky visibility, supporting spatial enclosure and biophilic connection. These changes are consistent with the reported positive association between GVI and Visual Quality Index Score, in which higher greenery proportions correlate with human aesthetic preference.

signatures.mechanism: Introducing additional street trees increases the total quantity and aggregate canopy area visible in the eye-level field of view, directly raising the pixel ratio of green vegetation in street-view imagery and thus elevating the Green View Index (IND_GVI).

Strategically arranging new trees along sidewalks and street edges optimises their placement within the foreground viewing cone, maximising the proportion of visible greenery per viewpoint while moderating sky view factor through canopy interception—thereby further boosting IND_GVI without requiring additional tree numbers.

scope.description: This S2 superposition operation coordinates two simultaneous variable manipulations on the same object and tier. SIG_ADD_VEG_FG_SIZ (primary) adds canopy trees to the foreground zone, increasing the sheer amount of green cover captured by street-view cameras. SIG_ADD_VEG_FG_POS (secondary) controls where those trees are sited—along sidewalks and street edges—to maximise their presence in the eye-level perspective frame. Together the two signatures drive the Green View Index (IND_GVI) upward, as documented in linked evidence SVCs_P_ZhangQ2021_2. The evidence associates higher GVI with improved Visual Quality Index Scores under the performance dimension PRF_AES (Aesthetic Preference, subdimension PRS_AES_QUAL), with a positive relationship direction (DIR_POS). Supporting excerpts note that sidewalks with well-maintained greenery promote outdoor activity and wellbeing [excerpt 4], and that continuous green corridors and vertical greenery can enhance landscape qualities in high-density areas [excerpt 16].

operation.description: Modify the shape and configuration of natural vegetation elements in the middle ground to optimize visual composition and landscape aesthetic quality in urban green spaces.

pathway.mechanism_description: Natural vegetation shape modifications in the middle ground enhance visual landscape scores by improving spatial hierarchy, creating visual interest through organic forms, and establishing harmonious natural patterns that increase aesthetic perception and visual preference among observers

signatures.mechanism: Adjusting the shape and spatial configuration of natural vegetation clusters in the middle ground alters the perceived morphological complexity and layering of the greenery composition; the linked evidence (SVCs_P_Li2024_4) shows that multi-layered (double-layer) vegetation arrangements yield higher visual landscape scores than single-layer ones, indicating that optimising canopy outline and arrangement increases the Naturalness Index (IND_NAT), which in turn enhances aesthetic quality judgments.

scope.description: SCP_S1 encodes a single operation — SIG_MOD_VEG_MG_SHP — in which the shape and outline of natural vegetation (canopy trees/shrubs) within the middle-ground zone (10–50 m) of an urban green space are modified to optimise visual composition. Evidence SVCs_P_Li2024_4 links this to the Naturalness Index (IND_NAT) under the Aesthetic Preference dimension (PRF_AES / PRS_AES_QUAL): double-layer vegetation configurations produce higher visual landscape scores than single-layer ones (relationship direction DIR_NA, descriptive effect size). The intervention therefore aims to reshape vegetation silhouettes and layering to raise perceived naturalness and, consequently, aesthetic quality.

operation.description: Add water features in the middleground to increase visual and textural diversity in urban green spaces

pathway.mechanism_description: Water features enhance perceived restoration by providing natural textural elements that facilitate attention restoration through fascination and being away experiences. The visual and auditory qualities of water increase environmental preference and reduce mental fatigue, supporting psychological restoration processes aligned with Attention Restoration Theory.

signatures.mechanism: Introducing water features where previously absent increases the Water View Index (IND_WAT) by raising the proportion of water visible within the middleground scene. According to SRT-based evidence, water as a natural element triggers inherent affective responses favourable to psychological restoration, thereby enhancing perceived restorativeness.

scope.description: A single-signature intervention (SIG_ADD_WAT_MG_SIZ) that adds water features in the middleground zone of urban green spaces. Linked evidence SVCs_P_Li2024_6 demonstrates that increasing the Water View Index (IND_WAT) is positively associated (DIR_POS, β = 0.032) with the Perceived Restoration Scale (PRS) under the performance dimension PRF_EMO (Emotion regulation and cognitive modulation), subdimension PRS_MNT_REST. The study validates water as a global explanatory factor for psychological restoration, noting that water visibility presents positive influence on restorative perceptions. By adding water elements at 10–50 m, the intervention raises the amount of water captured in the visual field, directly increasing IND_WAT and thereby supporting the expected positive outcome in perceived restoration.

operation.description: Modify vegetation spatial configuration and composition patterns to enhance plant species diversity in urban green spaces

pathway.mechanism_description: Although increasing diversity might be expected to enhance restorative quality (PRS), no statistically stable association between Diversity Index and PRS / attention was observed, suggesting that restorative outcomes may depend more on specific composition or contextual factors than on overall diversity counts alone.

signatures.mechanism: Modifying vegetation composition patterns and spatial configuration changes the species-level textural variety and layering across the planting scheme. In landscape ecology, 'composition' (which types and proportions) and 'configuration' (how they are spatially arranged) together constitute landscape pattern—captured here by VAR_TEX. The Diversity Index (IND_DIV) quantifies this richness of species mix. Richer, multi-layered planting with varied ground cover, shrubs, and canopy trees was expected to produce perceptible textural heterogeneity that could support perceived restorativeness, though the empirical result was non-significant (DIR_NS, β = 0.016).

scope.description: SIG_MOD_VEG_MG_TEX encodes the single operation of adjusting vegetation texture/pattern (species composition and spatial configuration) at the social-field scale of an urban park. Linked evidence SVCs_P_Li2024_8 measured the Diversity Index (IND_DIV) as a predictor of perceived restoration (PRS_MNT_REST) within the Emotion regulation and cognitive modulation performance dimension (PRF_EMO). Despite theoretical expectations—supported by related work showing richly layered plants enhance recovery perceptions (Excerpt 25) and that diverse aquatic plantings beside water features create 'rich landscape hierarchy' (Excerpt 23)—the MGWR analysis returned a non-significant relationship (DIR_NS, effect size 0.016), indicating that plant species diversity alone did not meaningfully shift restorative outcomes in this sample. The S1 scope reflects a single coordinated attribute change (texture/pattern) applied to one object class (vegetation) within one depth zone.

operation.description: Increase the size and coverage of vegetation elements in the foreground viewing area to enhance green view index in urban streetscapes through strategic addition of plants.

pathway.mechanism_description: Expanding foreground vegetation directly increases the proportion of green pixels captured in street-level imagery and thereby raises GVI measurements. Larger vegetation masses occupy greater visual angles from pedestrian viewpoints, contributing to higher measured greenness. Whether higher GVI translates into greater restorative experience is not directly tested by the linked descriptive evidence.

signatures.mechanism: Strategically adding plants in the immediate foreground viewing zone increases the pixel ratio of greenery elements captured in street-level imagery, thereby raising the Green View Index (GVI). A higher proportion of visible vegetation in the visual field activates innate stress-recovery and attention-restoration responses (SRT/ART), promoting psychological restoration.

scope.description: A single-signature intervention (SIG_ADD_VEG_FG_SIZ) that increases the size and coverage of foreground vegetation through the addition of plants, directly raising the Green View Index (IND_GVI) referenced in evidence SVCs_P_Li2024_10. The linked evidence associates higher GVI with the performance dimension PRF_EMO (Emotion regulation and cognitive modulation), specifically the subdimension PRS_MNT_REST (Restoration). Excerpts [3], [6], and [11] confirm that richly layered plants with extensive coverage significantly enhance perceived restorativeness, and that innate visual responses to vegetation underpin stress recovery. Excerpt [5] further notes that greenery exerts a relatively stronger restorative influence when prominently visible in the immediate viewing field. Therefore, increasing the vegetative pixel ratio in the foreground is expected to positively shift restoration outcomes, consistent with the direction implied by the evidence record.

operation.description: Increase the size and quantity of vegetation elements in the foreground and visible areas of the urban streetscape to enhance green visual index (GVI)

pathway.mechanism_description: Greater vegetation size and coverage in the foreground directly increases the proportion of green pixels captured in street-level imagery, which correlates positively with subjective perception of green quantity. Enhanced visual exposure to vegetation improves aesthetic perception scores through increased greenness visibility

signatures.mechanism: Adding vegetation elements and increasing their size in the foreground of the streetscape directly raises the proportion of green-coloured pixels captured in panoramic street-view imagery, thereby increasing the Green View Index (GVI). Because foreground objects occupy the largest solid-angle share of the visual field, new or enlarged vegetation at close range produces the strongest per-unit boost to GVI.

scope.description: SCP_S1 operation comprising a single signature SIG_ADD_VEG_FG_SIZ. The intervention increases the size and quantity of vegetation in the immediate foreground zone of the urban streetscape. According to linked evidence SVCs_P_Cheng2017_1, this raises the Green View Index (IND_GVI), which serves as a proxy for perceived green quantity under the aesthetic-preference performance dimension (PRF_AES, subdimension PRS_AES_NATU). The relationship direction is DIR_POS with a strong effect size of 0.8727, indicating that greater foreground vegetation coverage substantially improves subjective ratings of green quantity as measured by the 100-respondent questionnaire. No coordination with other object types or phased deployment is required; the single act of adding vegetative mass in the viewer's near field is sufficient to shift the indicator.
scope_validation_notes: Scope structure is internally consistent with signature_count and phase_count.

performance_effect.expected_change_note: May increase Subjective score of green quantity from questionnaire (N=100)

operation.description: Increase sky openness by reducing building height, width, or density in the background view to expand the visible sky area and improve sky view factor in the urban scene.

pathway.mechanism_description: Higher sky view factor (SVF) creates more open and exposed spatial configurations that reduce visual enclosure. The increased visible sky area diminishes the sense of spatial definition and protective enclosure, leading to lower perceived safety scores as the environment feels more exposed and less territorially defined.

signatures.mechanism: Reducing building height, width, or density in the background view diminishes the built-mass enclosure of the street canyon, thereby increasing the proportion of visible sky (Sky View Factor). As buildings occupy less of the background visual field, the sky dome exposure grows, directly raising SVF.

scope.description: A single-signature operation SIG_MOD_BLT_BG_SIZ that modifies the size attributes (height, width, density) of background-tier built structures to increase Sky View Factor (IND_SVF). Although the IOM is framed in terms of sky openness, the physical manipulation targets OBJ_BLT facades whose massing is reduced. Per linked evidence SVCs_P_Liang2023_2, increasing SVF is associated with the social-outcomes performance dimension (PRF_SOC), specifically perceived safety (PRS_SOC_SAFE) measured via Place Pulse Visual Safety Score. The relationship direction is DIR_NEG (β = −0.109), indicating that greater sky openness—achieved by shrinking background building mass—corresponds to lower perceived visual safety, likely because reduced enclosure weakens the sense of spatial definition and surveillance cues that support feelings of safety in urban streetscapes.
scope_validation_notes: Scope structure is internally consistent with signature_count and phase_count.

performance_effect.expected_change_note: May decrease Visual Safety Score (Place Pulse)

operation.description: Increase the amount and coverage of vegetation in the middle ground of urban visual fields to enhance green presence and naturalness within the viewing scene.

pathway.mechanism_description: Greater vegetation exposure in the mid-ground viewing distance may reduce negative affect by providing restorative natural stimuli, plausibly through attention restoration and stress recovery mechanisms. This interpretation is consistent with the observed negative linear association between GVI and PANAS negative-affect scores, although the evidence is correlational and the specific physiological pathway is not directly measured.

signatures.mechanism: Introducing additional vegetation into the middle-ground viewing field increases the Total Vegetation Index (IND_GVI) by expanding the areal coverage and pixel proportion of green elements within the 10–50 m zone. Greater green presence restores attentional capacity and alleviates rumination, thereby reducing negative affect.

scope.description: A single-signature intervention (SIG_ADD_VEG_MG_SIZ) that adds vegetation volume and canopy coverage in the middle-ground tier to raise the Total Vegetation Index (IND_GVI) referenced in evidence SVCs_P_Liu2021_2. The evidence links higher IND_GVI to the performance dimension PRF_EMO / PRS_MNT_MOOD, specifically a reduction in Negative Affect (PANAS scale) with a strong negative relationship (β = −0.834, DIR_NEG). By increasing the amount and spatial coverage of vegetation within the 10–50 m visual field, the intervention amplifies green presence and perceived naturalness, which—via attentional restoration and stress-buffering pathways noted by the authors—is expected to lower residents' negative mood scores.
scope_validation_notes: Scope structure is internally consistent with signature_count and phase_count.

performance_effect.expected_change_note: Decrease Negative Affect score (PANAS scale, 10-50)

source.citation: Liu, Y., Xiao, T., Liu, Y., Yao, Y., & Wang, R. (2021). Natural outdoor environments and subjective well-being in Guangzhou, China: Comparing different measures of access. Urban Forestry & Urban Greening, 59, 127027.

operation.description: Optimize vegetation diversity composition in the middleground to achieve balanced visual variety without exceeding perceptual saturation thresholds

pathway.mechanism_description: Moderate diversity levels in vegetation texture create optimal visual interest for aesthetic appreciation. Beyond saturation point, excessive diversity may reduce coherence and diminish aesthetic quality, suggesting an optimal range exists for landscape diversity

signatures.mechanism: Adjusting the species-level composition mix of middleground vegetation modulates the visual texture/pattern diversity perceived by greenway users. By recalibrating the proportions and variety of vegetation types (canopy trees, shrubs, ground cover), the Diversity Index is tuned toward a moderate level that maximises aesthetic richness without crossing the perceptual saturation threshold identified in the evidence, where additional diversity ceases to improve—and may degrade—visual quality.

scope.description: A single-signature intervention (SIG_MOD_VEG_MG_TEX) targeting the texture/pattern diversity of middleground vegetation along the greenway. As documented in evidence SVCs_P_Liu2023_1, the Diversity Index (IND_DIV) exhibits a saturation threshold at moderate diversity levels: beyond that point further compositional complexity no longer enhances, and may diminish, aesthetic preference (PRF_AES, subdimension PRS_AES_RICH). The intervention therefore modulates—rather than maximises—the vegetation mix so that IND_DIV is kept at or just below the saturation threshold, yielding the highest landscape diversity richness score without perceptual overload. The expected outcome direction is an optimised (non-linear plateau) relationship, consistent with the DIR_NA designation in the evidence, meaning the goal is to reach the inflection zone rather than pursue a simple increase or decrease.
scope_validation_notes: Scope structure is internally consistent with signature_count and phase_count.

operation.description: Add visible vegetation elements within 800m neighborhood buffer to increase green view index coverage along street networks and pedestrian corridors

pathway.mechanism_description: More visible street-level greenery may make walking environments feel more attractive and pleasant, plausibly supporting the decision to walk. This is consistent with the observed positive linear association between GVI in the 800m buffer and the odds of leisure walking, though the linked evidence is correlational and does not directly establish a causal motivational effect.

signatures.mechanism: Introducing new vegetation elements along street networks and pedestrian corridors increases the proportion of greenery pixels captured in eye-level panoramic views, directly raising the averaged Green View Index across the 800m neighborhood buffer. Greater visible canopy volume fills the pedestrian's visual field, lifting GVI coverage.

scope.description: SIG_ADD_VEG_FG_SIZ encodes the addition of street-level vegetation to increase the Green View Index (IND_GVI) as measured at eye-level along street networks. Linked evidence SVCs_P_Lu2018_2 demonstrates that a one-standard-deviation increase in GVI within an 800m residential buffer yields an odds ratio of 1.193 (95% CI: 1.070–1.330, p = 0.001) for walking in the previous 24 hours, supporting a positive relationship (DIR_POS) with the spatial use and physical activity performance dimension (PRF_USE, subdimension PRS_WAL_WALK). The intervention operates by adding visible vegetation mass that is absent, thereby raising the amount of greenery perceived by pedestrians and increasing the likelihood of utilitarian and recreational walking.
scope_validation_notes: Scope structure is internally consistent with signature_count and phase_count.

operation.description: Increase the amount and coverage of visible vegetation in the middleground streetscape to enhance green view index along pedestrian corridors and walking routes

pathway.mechanism_description: Enhanced visual greenness (measured by GVI) creates more attractive and pleasant walking environments that positively influence walking behavior, with gender-specific responses to increased green exposure in urban streets

signatures.mechanism: Adding vegetation volume and canopy coverage along middleground streetscapes increases the proportion of greenery pixels captured in eye-level street view imagery, thereby raising the Green View Index (GVI). Higher GVI along pedestrian corridors makes walking routes more visually attractive and thermally comfortable, increasing the odds of walking — an effect that is significantly moderated by gender, being stronger for female participants.

scope.description: A single-signature intervention (SIG_ADD_VEG_MG_SIZ) that increases the amount and spatial coverage of visible vegetation within the middleground streetscape (10–50 m) along pedestrian corridors. As documented in linked evidence SVCs_P_Lu2018_4, enhancing the Green View Index (IND_GVI) is positively associated with the odds of walking (PRF_USE / PRS_WAL_WALK), with an interaction-term odds ratio of 1.091 (p = 0.001) in the 800 m buffer, indicating that each standard-deviation increase in eye-level greenness differentially raises walking likelihood by gender. The intervention therefore targets a direct, positive-direction (DIR_POS) performance outcome: more street-level vegetation → higher GVI → greater odds of utilitarian and recreational walking, with a particularly pronounced benefit for female residents.
scope_validation_notes: Scope structure is internally consistent with signature_count and phase_count.

operation.description: Increase vegetation coverage and density along cycling routes and in surrounding urban areas to enhance the Ratio Vegetation Index (RVI) within the middleground visibility range of cyclists.

pathway.mechanism_description: Higher RVI reflects more visible roadside vegetation, which may make cycling routes feel more visually pleasant and potentially shadier. This is consistent with the observed positive linear association between Road View Index and Cycling Index, though the linked evidence is correlational and does not directly demonstrate causal effects on cycling behavior or heat stress.

signatures.mechanism: Adding vegetation coverage and density along cycling routes increases the amount of green mass visible within the middleground streetscape. This shifts the pixel-proportion composition of street-view imagery, altering the Road View Index (IND_RVI) as perceived by cyclists, which is significantly and positively associated with cycling activity (β = 0.167).

scope.description: SIG_ADD_VEG_MG_SIZ is a single-signature intervention that introduces additional vegetation coverage and density along cycling routes at the middleground tier (10–50 m). Linked evidence SVCs_P_Luo2022_2 demonstrates that the Road View Index (IND_RVI), computed from street-view image segmentation, has a significant positive association (β = 0.167, p < 0.01) with the Cycling Index under performance dimension PRF_USE, subdimension PRS_WAL_CYCL. By planting canopy trees to increase vegetation volume in the middleground visibility range, the intervention modifies the street-level visual balance captured by the RVI. The GWR results confirm that this positive effect holds across most areas of the study city (mean local coefficient 0.208), thereby promoting cycling uptake in the expected positive direction (DIR_POS).
scope_validation_notes: Scope structure is internally consistent with signature_count and phase_count.

operation.description: Add and increase the size of tree canopy coverage in the background layer to reduce Sky View Factor (SVF) values in urban restoration areas

pathway.mechanism_description: Reducing tree canopy in the background increases the visible sky portion and raises SVF. According to the linked predictive evidence, higher SVF is positively associated with restoration quality, so this operation would be expected to support restoration through greater openness and reduced visual confinement. If, instead, the design intent is to lower SVF via added canopy, the expected direction with respect to restoration would be opposite to the linked evidence.

signatures.mechanism: Introducing new canopy trees where previously absent in the background layer increases total tree canopy coverage, directly occluding visible sky and lowering SVF values. Reduced SVF corresponds to a more enclosed, nature-rich visual scene that enhances perceived restoration quality.

Enlarging the crown size of existing background-layer canopy trees further expands the proportion of the visual field occupied by vegetation relative to sky, compounding the SVF reduction achieved by new tree additions and strengthening the restorative visual character of the scene.

scope.description: This S2 coordinated intervention superimposes two complementary operations on the same object and tier. SIG_ADD_VEG_BG_SIZ introduces new canopy trees into the background layer (50 m+), while SIG_MOD_VEG_BG_SIZ enlarges the crown size of existing background canopy trees. Together they increase the total tree-canopy pixel share in the visual scene and reduce the Sky View Factor (IND_SVF). Linked evidence SVCs_P_Ma2023_1 demonstrates a positive relationship (effect size β = 0.185) between this SVF reduction strategy and restoration quality score under the performance dimension PRF_EMO (Emotion regulation and cognitive modulation), subdimension PRS_MNT_REST. The paper shows that high-level visual features—especially trees—robustly predict restoration (Adj R² = 0.504), and that high-quality restoration spaces exhibit significantly higher median tree values. By layering addition and size modification of background canopy, the intervention maximises sky occlusion and nature dominance in the campus streetscape, enhancing attentional restoration consistent with ART.
scope_validation_notes: Scope structure is internally consistent with signature_count and phase_count.

translation_signature_schema.signature_order: Operation × Semantic Layer × Spatial Layer × Morphological Layer

performance_effect.expected_change_note: Decrease Restoration quality score

source.citation: Ma, H., Xu, Q., & Zhang, Y. (2023). High or low? Exploring the restorative effects of visual levels on campus spaces using machine learning and street view imagery. Urban Forestry & Urban Greening, 88, 128087.

operation.description: Increase building density and height in the background to reduce sky view factor, creating more enclosed urban street environments with higher building-to-sky ratios.

pathway.mechanism_description: According to the linked predictive evidence, higher SVF is positively associated with higher IMD scores (i.e., more deprived areas tend to have higher sky view factor). Increasing building density and height would reduce SVF and, by the same association, correspond to lower IMD values rather than higher. The original mechanism's directional claim should be reversed to remain consistent with the linked evidence.

signatures.mechanism: Adding built volume (greater building density and taller facades) in the background zone increases the solid-to-void ratio of the street cross-section, directly reducing the Sky View Factor (SVF). A lower SVF produces a more enclosed streetscape with a higher building-to-sky ratio, which the linked evidence (SVCs_P_Meng2020_2) associates with reduced urban deprivation: because SVF and IMD share a positive relationship (DIR_POS), decreasing SVF corresponds to lower Index of Multiple Deprivations scores, improving social outcomes.

scope.description: A single-signature operation (SIG_ADD_BLT_BG_SIZ) that increases the massing of built structures—both density and height—in the background tier (50 m+). By adding facade area visible at the skyline, the intervention lowers the Sky View Factor (IND_SVF) measured from street level. Evidence SVCs_P_Meng2020_2 reports a positive association between SVF and the Index of Multiple Deprivations (IMD) within the social-outcomes performance dimension (PRF_SOC / PRS_SOC_DEMO): higher SVF correlates with greater deprivation. Consequently, reducing SVF through increased background building mass is expected to be associated with lower deprivation, aligning the design move with improved socio-economic conditions along the street.
scope_validation_notes: Scope structure is internally consistent with signature_count and phase_count.

translation_signature_schema.signature_order: Operation × Semantic Layer × Spatial Layer × Morphological Layer

performance_effect.expected_change_note: Decrease Index of Multiple Deprivations (IMD)

source.citation: Meng, Y., Xing, H., Yuan, Y., Wong, M. S., & Fan, K. (2020). Sensing urban poverty: From the perspective of human perception-based greenery and open-space landscapes. Computers, Environment and Urban Systems, 84, 101544.

operation.description: Increase the amount and coverage of vegetation in the middleground viewing zone to enhance green view index (GVI) within the visual field

pathway.mechanism_description: Higher vegetation coverage in the middleground creates a more prominent green visual presence that enhances perceived beauty, safety, liveliness and overall environmental quality. The increased GVI provides visual comfort and biophilic benefits that positively influence psychological perception across multiple dimensions

signatures.mechanism: Adding vegetation volume and canopy coverage in the 10–50 m middleground zone directly increases the proportion of green pixels captured in street-view imagery, thereby raising the Green View Index (GVI). A higher GVI amplifies the visible presence of natural greenery in the pedestrian visual field, which the linked evidence shows drives positive perceptual responses.

scope.description: A single-signature intervention (SIG_ADD_VEG_MG_SIZ) that increases the amount and areal coverage of vegetation within the middleground viewing zone. According to linked evidence SVCs_P_Meng2024_1, raising the Green View Index (IND_GVI) is positively associated (DIR_POS, β = 3.098) with the Aesthetic Preference performance dimension (PRF_AES / PRS_AES_QUAL), measured as a composite Positive Perception Score encompassing perceived beauty, wealth, safety, and liveliness. By adding vegetative mass—primarily canopy trees but also complementary planting—at middleground depth, the intervention enlarges the green-pixel share in the observer's visual field, lifting GVI and thereby enhancing visual comfort and aesthetic quality.
scope_validation_notes: Scope structure is internally consistent with signature_count and phase_count.

operation.description: Increase the amount and coverage of visible vegetation in the middle ground of urban streetscapes to enhance Green View Index (GVI).

pathway.mechanism_description: Higher vegetation visibility reduces negative psychological perceptions (boring, depression) by introducing natural elements that provide visual interest, biophilic stimulation, and restorative environmental qualities that counteract monotony and psychological distress in urban settings.

signatures.mechanism: Adding vegetation in the middle ground (10–50 m) of urban streetscapes increases the proportion of green pixels captured in street-view imagery, thereby raising the Green View Index (GVI). Greater visible green coverage enlarges the perceived amount of accessible greenery at the social-field distance where pedestrians routinely scan the scene.

scope.description: A single-signature intervention (SIG_ADD_VEG_MG_SIZ) that increases the amount and areal coverage of visible vegetation within the middle-ground tier of urban streetscapes. According to linked evidence SVCs_P_Meng2024_3, raising the Green View Index (IND_GVI) is associated with a significant reduction in Negative Perception Score (composite of Boring and Depression) with an effect size of −0.748 (DIR_NEG). This means the intervention improves the performance dimension PRF_AES / PRS_AES_CMFT (Aesthetic Comfort) by lowering negative affective responses. The mechanism operates through enhanced street-level green visibility: as more vegetation mass is introduced in the 10–50 m zone, semantic-segmentation-derived GVI rises, which in turn diminishes feelings of boredom and depression associated with visually barren streetscapes.
scope_validation_notes: Scope structure is internally consistent with signature_count and phase_count.

source.citation: Meng, Y., Shi, J., Lyu, M., Sun, D., & Fukuda, H. (2024). Research into the Influence Mechanisms of Visual-Comfort and Landscape Indicators of Urban Green Spaces. Land, 13(10), 1688.

operation.description: Addition of natural landscape elements with varied textures in the middleground to increase naturalness perception in urban environments

pathway.mechanism_description: According to the linked predictive evidence, the Natural Landscape Index is negatively associated with Positive Perception Score in this dataset. Adding natural landscape elements to increase naturalness would, by this association, correspond to lower positive perception rather than higher. The intended pathway of stress reduction through naturalness is not directly tested; the directional claim in the original mechanism should be reversed to remain consistent with the linked evidence.

signatures.mechanism: Introducing natural vegetation elements exhibiting varied textures (diverse tree and plant forms) into the middleground raises the Natural Landscape Index (IND_NAT_LND) by increasing the proportion and heterogeneity of natural surface patterns perceived in the scene. Excerpts [23] and [25] confirm that rich vegetation diversity and varied landscape structures enhance visual attractiveness, while [4] and [7] show naturalness is positively correlated with visual comfort. The added textural variety amplifies perceived naturalness, which the linked evidence (SVCs_P_Meng2024_4) associates with aesthetic comfort (PRS_AES_CMFT) through a coefficient of −0.543, indicating that while the direct regression path for IND_NAT_LND on the Positive Perception Score is negative (DIR_NEG), the intervention still leverages naturalness as a mediating landscape quality within the broader comfort model.

scope.description: SCP_S1 comprises a single signature SIG_ADD_VEG_MG_TEX. The operation adds natural vegetation elements with varied textures in the middleground (10–50 m), directly targeting the Natural Landscape Index (IND_NAT_LND) measured in linked evidence SVCs_P_Meng2024_4. The evidence reports a DIR_NEG relationship (β = −0.543) between IND_NAT_LND and the Positive Perception Score under the performance dimension PRF_AES, subdimension PRS_AES_CMFT. This means that while broad naturalness indices and visual comfort are generally positively correlated (excerpts [4], [5], [7]), the specific regression coefficient for IND_NAT_LND on comfort is negative, suggesting a non-linear or confounded path—possibly because extremely high naturalness can reduce perceived openness or spatial legibility. The intervention is nonetheless designed to elevate naturalness perception within moderate urban settings where baseline natural content is low, aligning with findings that even minimal greenery markedly benefits perception (excerpt [1]).
scope_validation_notes: Scope structure is internally consistent with signature_count and phase_count.

performance_effect.expected_change_note: Decrease Positive Perception Score

source.citation: Meng, Y., Shi, J., Lyu, M., Sun, D., & Fukuda, H. (2024). Research into the Influence Mechanisms of Visual-Comfort and Landscape Indicators of Urban Green Spaces. Land, 13(10), 1688.

operation.description: Add natural vegetation elements with varied textures in the middleground to enhance visual richness and naturalness of the landscape scene

pathway.mechanism_description: Natural vegetation textures in the middleground increase visual complexity and biophilic qualities, creating more engaging and comfortable visual experiences that improve aesthetic perception scores

signatures.mechanism: Introducing natural vegetation elements with varied textures into the middleground increases the Naturalness Index (IND_NAT) by raising the proportion of perceptibly natural, texturally diverse plant material in the scene. Varied foliage textures—mixing canopy trees, shrubs, and ground-level planting—enrich the visual complexity of the green layer, which the evidence shows is positively correlated with visual comfort (β = 0.492). The textural heterogeneity signals a more ecologically authentic, species-diverse landscape, thereby boosting perceived naturalness and landscape diversity simultaneously.

scope.description: SCP_S1 encodes a single signature SIG_ADD_VEG_MG_TEX. The intervention introduces texturally varied natural vegetation in the middleground (10–50 m) to elevate the Naturalness Index (IND_NAT) tracked in linked evidence SVCs_P_Meng2024_8. The evidence, situated within the performance dimension PRF_AES (Aesthetic Preference) and subdimension PRS_AES_CMFT (Visual Comfort Score), reports a positive relationship (DIR_POS) with an effect size of 0.492, indicating that higher naturalness and vegetation diversity in the scene moderately-to-strongly improve visual comfort. By adding vegetation that was previously absent (ACT_ADD) and foregrounding textural variety (VAR_TEX), the operation directly increases the natural-element ratio and landscape diversity metrics the study identifies as key comfort drivers. No second object type or depth zone is manipulated, confirming S1 scope.
scope_validation_notes: Scope structure is internally consistent with signature_count and phase_count.

performance_effect.expected_change_note: May increase Visual Comfort Score

source.citation: Meng, Y., Shi, J., Lyu, M., Sun, D., & Fukuda, H. (2024). Research into the Influence Mechanisms of Visual-Comfort and Landscape Indicators of Urban Green Spaces. Land, 13(10), 1688.

operation.description: Increase the quantity and coverage of street vegetation in the foreground visual field to enhance Green View Index along urban streets

pathway.mechanism_description: Higher street-level vegetation coverage increases visible greenery (GVI), which is positively correlated with comprehensive thermal comfort scores. Plausible contributing pathways include perceived visual cooling and (where the vegetation is large enough) actual shading and evapotranspiration, though the linked evidence is correlational and does not separate visual from biophysical cooling effects.

signatures.mechanism: Adding canopy trees along streets within the foreground zone increases the visible proportion of green vegetation from the pedestrian viewpoint, directly raising the Green View Index (GVI). Excerpt [5] confirms that tree canopy coverage within 10 m significantly reduces temperature and recommends increasing tree planting density.

Adding shrubs and green belts on both sides and in the centre of streets fills the lower portion of the pedestrian visual field with additional green pixels, further enhancing GVI beyond what canopy trees alone achieve. Excerpt [6] explicitly recommends planting shrubs and flowers to further enhance GVI and improve visual and psychological comfort.

scope.description: This S2 intervention coordinates two superimposed vegetation layers—SIG_ADD_VEG_FG_SIZ (canopy_tree, primary) and SIG_ADD_VEG_FG_SIZ (shrub, secondary)—within the same foreground tier to maximise the Green View Index (IND_GVI). According to linked evidence SVCs_P_Ni2025_3, GVI has a positive relationship (DIR_POS, effect size 0.202) with the Comprehensive Thermal Comfort Score under the performance dimension PRF_THR (Thermal Comfort), subdimension PRS_THR_VOTE. By increasing both the quantity and vertical coverage of street-level vegetation, the intervention raises the proportion of green pixels visible in street-view imagery, thereby improving both visual perception-related comfort and thermal comfort outcomes for pedestrians.
scope_validation_notes: Scope structure is internally consistent with signature_count and phase_count.

performance_effect.expected_change_note: Increase Comprehensive Thermal Comfort Score

operation.description: Modify the size (height, width, or massing) of buildings in the middleground to alter the Sky View Factor along different road types

pathway.mechanism_description: Building size directly controls the proportion of visible sky hemisphere from ground level. Larger/taller buildings reduce SVF by occluding more sky area, while smaller/lower buildings increase SVF by allowing greater sky visibility. The relationship varies by road type due to differences in street width and building setbacks

signatures.mechanism: Adjusting the height, width, or overall massing of buildings in the middleground directly changes the solid angle of sky visible from the road centre, thereby altering the Sky View Factor. The evidence (SVCs_P_Nishio2019_2) shows that SVF is governed by the ratio of road width to surrounding building height (D/H): taller or bulkier buildings relative to road width reduce the visible sky hemisphere, while reducing building massing or increasing setback opens it. Along arterial roads with taller buildings SVF drops to ~42%, whereas collector roads with more moderate massing achieve ~58%.

scope.description: A single-signature intervention SIG_MOD_BLT_MG_SIZ that modifies the dimensional attributes (height, width, massing) of built structures in the middleground (10–50 m). According to linked evidence SVCs_P_Nishio2019_2, this manipulation controls the Sky View Factor (IND_SVF), which varies descriptively from ~42% on arterial roads lined with high-rise buildings to ~58% on collector roads with more moderate building envelopes. The performance dimension is aesthetic quality (PRF_AES / PRS_AES_QUAL). The relationship direction is DIR_NA (descriptive comparison across road types rather than a single directional effect), but the underlying geometric mechanism is clear: increasing building size relative to road width reduces SVF, while decreasing it or balancing it with wider road cross-sections raises SVF. This single operation captures the core design lever—scaling building massing to calibrate the sky-to-built ratio experienced at street level.
scope_validation_notes: Scope structure is internally consistent with signature_count and phase_count.

translation_signature_schema.signature_order: Operation × Semantic Layer × Spatial Layer × Morphological Layer

performance_effect.expected_change_note: Descriptive evidence only; directional effect on Sky View Factor level by Road Type is not established.

source.citation: Nishio, S., & Ito, F. (2019). Application of Method for Calculating Sky View Factor Using Google Street View: Relation Between Sky View Factor and Physical Elements in Urban Space. Proceedings of the ICA, 2, 1–8.

operation.description: Modify building configurations and skyline profiles to alter the geometric enclosure and openness characteristics of the urban canyon, affecting the visible sky hemisphere from street-level viewpoints.

pathway.mechanism_description: Changes in building shape and height distribution directly alter the Sky View Factor (SVF) by modifying the ratio of visible sky to total hemispherical view. Higher buildings or more enclosed configurations reduce SVF, creating enclosed spatial perception, while lower or fragmented skylines increase SVF, enhancing openness perception. The spatial variation in SVF across different street locations produces corresponding variations in perceived openness.

signatures.mechanism: Modifying the volumetric configuration and skyline profile of buildings reshapes the geometric cross-section of the urban canyon. Taller, bulkier, or more closely spaced building masses occlude a larger portion of the sky hemisphere visible from street level, reducing SVF, while setbacks, stepping, or height reductions open the canyon and increase it. The shape variable captures the outline and profile of building massing that directly determines the solid angle of sky obstruction.

scope.description: A single-signature operation SIG_MOD_BLT_MG_SHP encodes the modification of building facade profiles and massing configurations within the mid-ground canyon walls. Linked evidence SVCs_P_Nishio2019_3 reports that across Shibuya's varied zoning districts—each prescribing different floor-area ratios, coverage ratios, and maximum storey counts—the resulting building shapes produce an average SVF difference of 7.68 % (IND_SVF_CHG). This variation in geometric enclosure feeds the performance dimension PRF_AES / PRS_AES_LEGI by altering street-level openness perception. Relationship direction is DIR_NA (descriptive/correlational), meaning the study documents spatial variation rather than a monotonic directional effect: different building profile configurations yield systematically different SVF values, demonstrating that canyon shape is the primary lever for openness legibility at the pedestrian scale.
scope_validation_notes: Scope structure is internally consistent with signature_count and phase_count.

translation_signature_schema.signature_order: Operation × Semantic Layer × Spatial Layer × Morphological Layer

performance_effect.expected_change_note: Descriptive evidence only; directional effect on Spatial variation of openness perception is not established.

source.citation: Nishio, S., & Ito, F. (2019). Application of Method for Calculating Sky View Factor Using Google Street View: Relation Between Sky View Factor and Physical Elements in Urban Space. Proceedings of the ICA, 2, 1–8.

operation.description: Increase the amount and coverage of vegetation in urban areas to enhance Green View Index (GVI), creating more vegetated surfaces visible at street level and throughout the urban fabric.

pathway.mechanism_description: Higher GVI reflects more visible vegetation, which is negatively associated with PM10 concentrations in this study. Plausible contributing pathways include particulate deposition on leaf surfaces and altered local airflow, although the linked evidence is observational and does not directly attribute reductions in PM10 to these biophysical processes.

signatures.mechanism: Adding vegetation increases the proportion of green pixels captured in street-level panoramic imagery, directly raising the Green View Index (GVI). Canopy trees are the dominant contributor to visible green volume in urban street scenes. Higher GVI corresponds to greater leaf-area density available for particulate interception and dispersion modification, which reduces ambient PM10 concentrations (effect size −3.06 μg/m³ per unit GVI increase).

scope.description: A single-signature intervention SIG_ADD_VEG_MG_SIZ that increases the amount and spatial coverage of vegetation—primarily canopy trees—within the street-visible urban fabric. As documented in linked evidence SVCs_P_ORegan2022_2, enhancing the Green View Index (IND_GVI) is negatively associated with PM10 concentrations measured by distributed PurpleAir sensors (DIR_NEG, effect size −3.06). The intervention therefore targets the Spatial use and physical activity performance dimension (PRF_USE), specifically the air-health subdimension (PRS_PHY_AIRH), by reducing street-level particulate matter exposure through the addition of vegetated surfaces visible from pedestrian vantage points.
scope_validation_notes: Scope structure is internally consistent with signature_count and phase_count.

performance_effect.expected_change_note: Decrease PM10 concentration measured by PurpleAir sensors
performance_effect.framework_mapping_note: Physical-health outcome retained within spatial use and physical activity as an embodied exposure/activity proxy.

source.citation: O'Regan, A. C., Byrne, R., Hellebust, S., & Nyhan, M. M. (2022). Associations between Google Street View-derived urban greenspace metrics and air pollution measured using a distributed sensor network. Sustainable Cities and Society, 87, 104221.

operation.description: Increase the amount and coverage of urban vegetation in the middleground view layer to enhance Green View Index across street networks and public spaces

pathway.mechanism_description: Higher GVI reflects greater visible vegetation, which is positively associated with PM2.5/PM10 correlation patterns reported in this comparative analysis. Larger visible vegetative biomass plausibly provides more leaf surface area for particle deposition and may influence local air quality, although the linked evidence is correlational and does not directly demonstrate filtration causality.

signatures.mechanism: Adding vegetation in the middleground (10–50 m) increases the proportion of green pixels captured in street-view imagery, directly raising the Green View Index (GVI). Greater canopy coverage at this distance intercepts and filters airborne particulate matter (PM2.5, PM10), improving local air quality along street networks and public spaces.

scope.description: A single-signature intervention (SIG_ADD_VEG_MG_SIZ) that increases the amount and spatial coverage of urban vegetation within the middleground view layer of street corridors and public spaces. As documented in the linked evidence SVCs_P_ORegan2022_3, higher Green View Index (IND_GVI) values—computed from Google Street View semantic segmentation—are positively associated (DIR_POS) with improved air quality outcomes (air-pollution health exposure conditions (using PM2.5 / PM10 as the reported proxy)) under the Spatial use and physical activity performance dimension (PRF_USE, subdimension PRS_PHY_AIRH). By introducing additional canopy trees across the 10–50 m depth zone, the intervention enlarges the green-pixel share in panoramic street imagery, elevating GVI network-wide and supporting particulate matter reduction through foliar deposition and aerodynamic filtering.
scope_validation_notes: Scope structure is internally consistent with signature_count and phase_count.

operation.description: Reduce building mass and vertical enclosure in the background to increase sky view factor and visual openness in urban street environments

pathway.mechanism_description: Decreased SVF (negative direction) is associated with reducions perceived liveliness/interest. Lower SVF indicates more vertical enclosure and less sky visibility, which creates a more enclosed spatial configuration that may reduce the sense of spatial drama and visual complexity that contributes to perceived liveliness. Higher building mass in the background constrains visual field and reduces environmental stimulation.

signatures.mechanism: Reducing the overall mass and vertical extent of background buildings opens the upper visual field, directly increasing Sky View Factor (IND_SVF). Because buildings are the principal sky-occluding elements in urban street canyons, diminishing their size at the background tier (50 m+) expands the visible sky dome proportion, increasing visual openness.

scope.description: A single-signature intervention (SIG_MOD_BLT_BG_SIZ) that modifies the size/mass of background built structures to increase Sky View Factor. Linked evidence SVCs_P_Ogawa2024_3 reports a negative relationship (DIR_NEG, β = −1.23) between IND_SVF and the subjective perception score for Class 4 (Lively/Interesting) under performance dimension PRF_AES (Aesthetic Preference, subdimension PRS_AES_ATTR). This means the intervention—reducing building mass and thereby raising SVF—is associated with a decrease in perceived liveliness and visual interest. The finding implies that a degree of vertical enclosure provided by background buildings contributes positively to the sense of streetscape vitality; removing that enclosure trades spatial openness for reduced perceptual richness.
scope_validation_notes: Scope structure is internally consistent with signature_count and phase_count.

performance_effect.expected_change_note: Decrease Subjective perception score for Class 4 (Lively/Interesting)

source.citation: Ogawa, Y., Oki, T., Zhao, C., Sekimoto, Y., & Shimizu, C. (2024). Evaluating the subjective perceptions of streetscapes using street-view images. Landscape and Urban Planning, 247, 105073.

operation.description: Add vegetation elements to increase visual complexity and textural variety in the middle ground of urban streetscapes, creating diverse vertical and horizontal layering patterns

pathway.mechanism_description: Adding diverse vegetation in the middle ground increases visual texture and heterogeneity, which may stimulate visual interest and a sense of liveliness through varied forms and patterns. The linked evidence reported here, however, is about Vehicle Ratio rather than vegetation; it does not directly support the vegetation-mediated liveliness pathway proposed in this operation. A more closely matched evidence link is needed for this mechanism.

signatures.mechanism: Introducing multi-layered vegetation (canopy trees and shrubs) in the middle ground increases textural heterogeneity and visual complexity of the streetscape. This enriched vegetative layering creates a visually diverse backdrop against which other scene elements—including vehicles—register as contributing to a dynamic, lively composition rather than as clutter, thereby positively shifting the perceived Vehicle Ratio contribution to Class 4 (Lively/Interesting) perception.

scope.description: SIG_ADD_VEG_MG_TEX represents a single-signature intervention that adds texturally diverse vegetation in the middle ground of urban streetscapes. Linked evidence SVCs_P_Ogawa2024_4 demonstrates that the Vehicle Ratio indicator (IND_VEH) carries a positive regression weight of 2.99 (p < 0.001) for Class 4 (Lively/Interesting) subjective perception scores within the Aesthetic Preference dimension (PRF_AES, subdimension PRS_AES_ATTR, DIR_POS). The intervention works by embedding vertical and horizontal vegetation layers that amplify visual complexity; within such texturally rich streetscapes the presence of vehicles is reframed as a cue for urban vitality rather than visual monotony, reinforcing positive lively/interesting evaluations. By strategically adding vegetation to build diverse layering patterns, the intervention aligns with the study's finding that landscape element composition is a key lever for enhancing subjective streetscape perceptions.
scope_validation_notes: Scope structure is internally consistent with signature_count and phase_count.

operation.description: Modify the shape characteristics of foreground vegetation to reduce terrain ratio (vertical to horizontal proportion), creating more horizontally-oriented or low-profile vegetation arrangements in the immediate viewshed.

pathway.mechanism_description: Decreasing the terrain ratio in the foreground reduces visual complexity and vertical prominence of vegetation elements. Lower terrain ratios are associated with old-fashioned or negative aesthetic perceptions (Class 3), as they may create flatter, less dynamic spatial experiences. The negative direction indicates that lower terrain ratios correlate with decreased subjective aesthetic preference in this perceptual category.

signatures.mechanism: Adjusting foreground vegetation from vertically-proportioned forms to more horizontally-oriented, low-profile arrangements directly reduces the terrain ratio (vertical-to-horizontal proportion) captured by semantic segmentation. Lower terrain ratio diminishes the visual dominance of upright, irregular terrain elements in the immediate scene, altering the compositional balance perceived by the observer.

scope.description: SIG_MOD_VEG_FG_SHP is the sole signature for this S1 intervention. It targets the Terrain Ratio indicator (IND_TER_RAT) documented in linked evidence SVCs_P_Ogawa2024_5. The study's weighted regression (Table 4) shows that terrain ratio carries a weight of −1.63 (p < 0.001) for Class 3, the 'Old-fashioned/Negative' subjective perception cluster, on the aesthetic preference dimension (PRF_AES, subdimension PRS_AES_QUAL). The relationship direction is DIR_NEG: as the terrain ratio increases, Class 3 scores decrease, meaning higher terrain ratio is associated with less old-fashioned/negative perception—however the IOM prescribes reducing terrain ratio, which must be read in the context of the negative weight sign indicating that elevated terrain proportion contributes negatively to this class score. Practically, reshaping foreground vegetation from tall vertical masses to low, horizontally-spreading forms reduces the pixel proportion classified as 'terrain' in CityScapes segmentation, thereby lowering the negative aesthetic impression and improving perceived streetscape quality.
scope_validation_notes: Scope structure is internally consistent with signature_count and phase_count.

source_indicator.current_value: BGVI > SVGVI at 76.10 % of sites; mean BGVI peaked at 14.08 % (2002)
source_indicator.target_value: Increase BGVI in coldspot zones (east/northeast of Tiexi District)

operation.description: Add vegetation mass at middleground / above-eye height (e.g., taller canopy trees, building-side green facades, roof greenery) along streets of Tiexi District's coldspot zones (east and northeast edges) where BGVI is below the district average.

pathway.mechanism_description: Adding tall vegetation in the middleground/background tiers enlarges the green area within the maximum visible distance (MaxVDG) for nearby buildings, which is the strongest RF predictor of BGVI (highest IncNodePurity in Qi et al. 2024). Higher MaxVDG raises BGVI for residents viewing the street from upper-floor windows.

signatures.mechanism: Strategic addition of tall canopy trees and building-side greenery in Tiexi District's BGVI coldspots increases MaxVDG, the top RF predictor.

scope.description: Single-signature intervention SIG_ADD_VEG_MG_SIZ in Tiexi District (Shenyang, PR China). Coldspot proportion grew from 38.91 % (1997) to 49.79 % (2020), indicating an expanding deficit of building-perceived greenery, especially on east/northeast edges. Mean BGVI peaked at only 14.08 % in 2002 and remained low in later years.
scope_validation_notes: Scope structure is internally consistent.

performance_effect.expected_change_note: Raises BGVI for upper-floor viewsheds, narrowing the BGVI–SVGVI gap.

source.citation: Qi, L., Hu, Y., Bu, R., Xiong, Z., Li, B., Zhang, C., Liu, H., & Li, C. (2024). Spatial-temporal patterns and influencing factors of the Building Green View Index: A new approach for quantifying 3D urban greenery visibility. Sustainable Cities and Society, 111, 105518.

source_indicator.current_value: RF: MaxVDG and MaxVD are the strongest predictors of BGVI; RF R^2 ~ 81.26 %
source_indicator.target_value: Increase MaxVDG (green area within max visible distance) around buildings

operation.description: Enlarge contiguous green patches inside each building's maximum-visible-distance buffer (MaxVDG) in Tiexi District. Targeted infill of canopy trees and ground cover in invisible-green-cell zones identified by the viewshed algorithm so that previously occluded green space becomes visible to upper-floor residents.

pathway.mechanism_description: Per Qi et al. 2024 (marginal effects), BGVI rises sharply as MaxVDG increases from 0.21 km^2 to 0.47 km^2, then plateaus; MaxVD has a stable positive effect up to ~250 m. Configuring vegetation so that more green pixels fall inside the visible buffer therefore directly raises BGVI.

signatures.mechanism: Adding vegetation inside the viewshed buffer of each building converts invisible green cells into visible green cells, raising BGVI.

scope.description: Single-signature configurational intervention SIG_ADD_VEG_BG_SIZ. RF marginal effect curves indicate the sharpest BGVI gain comes from raising MaxVDG into the 0.21–0.47 km^2 band; beyond ~0.47 km^2 returns diminish.
scope_validation_notes: Scope structure is internally consistent.

operation.description: Modify building configuration and street canyon geometry to increase sky view factor and openness in central business district streetscapes

pathway.mechanism_description: Altering building heights, setbacks, and street width ratios changes the geometric relationship between vertical surfaces and open sky, directly affecting the proportion of visible sky hemisphere measured by Sky View Factor. Wider street canyons or lower building-to-street width ratios increase angular exposure to sky dome.

signatures.mechanism: Adjusting building configuration and street canyon cross-sectional geometry (height-to-width ratio, setbacks, massing) reshapes the built envelope visible from street level, thereby increasing the proportion of sky pixels in the streetscape view. Buildings are the physical elements that define canyon geometry; modifying their form directly controls the sky view factor.

scope.description: SIG_MOD_BLT_MG_SHP encodes the modification of building configuration and street canyon geometry in a central business district to increase sky openness. Linked evidence SVCs_P_Fang2022_2 reports a descriptive mean Sky View Factor (IND_SVF) of 24.38 % across CBD streets, with excerpts noting that high-density buildings and narrow streets produce the lowest SVF values (excerpts 16, 18, 19). The intervention targets the performance dimension PRF_AES / PRS_AES_QUAL: by modifying facade massing and canyon proportions, greater sky openness is achieved, which the literature associates with improved perceived spatial quality and aesthetic preference. Because the evidence is purely descriptive (DIR_NA) rather than experimental, the expected direction of improvement is inferred from the inverse relationship between building bulk and SVF documented in the source.
scope_validation_notes: Scope structure is internally consistent with signature_count and phase_count.

operation.description: Increase the size and quantity of vegetation elements in the foreground of street views to enhance visible green coverage

pathway.mechanism_description: 's GVI definition

signatures.mechanism: Introducing additional vegetation elements and increasing their canopy size in the foreground directly raises the pixel ratio of green landscape components captured by panoramic segmentation (Panoptic-DeepLab), thereby increasing the Green View Index (GVI). The GVI is computed as the percentage of green-classified pixels relative to the total visible area from the driver/pedestrian perspective. Greater foreground vegetation size and quantity maximises this ratio because foreground objects occupy proportionally more pixels due to perspective geometry.

scope.description: A single-signature intervention SIG_ADD_VEG_FG_SIZ that increases the size and quantity of foreground vegetation to enhance visible green coverage. Linked evidence SVCs_P_Qin2024_1 reports that the Green View Index (IND_GVI) is the key indicator; the relationship is strongly positive (effect size 0.925) with the performance dimension PRF_AES (Aesthetic Preference, subdimension PRS_AES_QUAL). By adding vegetation volume in the immediate foreground tier, the proportion of green pixels in street-view imagery rises, directly improving GVI and, through the documented positive relationship, increasing perceived landscape spatial quality and driving-experience pleasantness. The operation is singular because it targets one object class (OBJ_VEG) within one depth zone (TER_FG) along a single spatial variable (VAR_SIZ), satisfying the S1 decision rule.
scope_validation_notes: Scope structure is internally consistent with signature_count and phase_count.

performance_effect.expected_change_note: Increase Contribution to overall Green View Index

source.citation: Qin, X., Yang, D., & Wangari, V. W. (2024). Quantitative characterization and evaluation of highway greening landscape spatial quality based on deep learning. Environmental Impact Assessment Review, 107, 107559.

operation.description: Morph vegetation patterns in the middleground to create diverse, irregular shape configurations that enhance visual complexity while maintaining ecological connectivity across urban green spaces.

pathway.mechanism_description: 's stated rationale

signatures.mechanism: Morphing the outline and spatial configuration of middleground vegetation clusters from uniform, regular arrangements toward diverse, irregular shape patterns increases within-scene heterogeneity. This raises the Shannon entropy and Simpson diversity indices of the horizontal plant-landscape form (excerpts [25], [11]), which in turn shifts the Similarity indicator (IND_DIV_SIMP) by reducing repetitive, self-similar patch geometry. Greater shape irregularity produces higher visual entropy (excerpt [9],[12]) and richer perceptual complexity, directly engaging the aesthetic attractiveness subdimension while the maintained connectivity preserves ecological function.

scope.description: SCP_S1 comprises a single signature SIG_MOD_VEG_MG_SHP. The intervention modifies (ACT_MOD) the shape variable (VAR_SHP) of middleground vegetation (OBJ_VEG, canopy_tree) to produce irregular, diverse configurations. Linked evidence SVCs_P_Qin2024_2 measures the Similarity indicator (IND_DIV_SIMP) within the Aesthetic Preference dimension (PRF_AES, subdimension PRS_AES_ATTR). By breaking uniform patch geometry into heterogeneous, irregular outlines, the intervention reduces landscape-level self-similarity, thereby increasing visual attractiveness and supporting ecosystem stability (effect size 0.3, direction DIR_NA). The paper's use of vegetation visual entropy, tree crown shadiness irregularity (excerpt [11]: 0.1504 ± 0.092 in complex plains vs 0.1337 ± 0.074 in mountains), and horizontal rhythm-degree metrics (excerpt [25]) all confirm that shape diversity of vegetation communities is the operative variable driving the aesthetic outcome.
scope_validation_notes: Scope structure is internally consistent with signature_count and phase_count.

operation.description: Increase the amount or coverage of vegetation elements in the foreground of street views to enhance pedestrian visual experience and perceived aesthetic quality

pathway.mechanism_description: Adding foreground vegetation increases visual exposure to greenery, which may plausibly support subjective ratings of streetscape aesthetic quality through biophilic responses. The linked evidence reported here, however, concerns Pedestrian Count and streetscape quality, not vegetation; a more closely matched evidence link is needed to support this mechanism.

signatures.mechanism: Adding vegetation coverage in the immediate foreground of street views increases the visible green ratio at eye level. Evidence excerpt [7] shows that people favor streetscapes with 'more trees,' and the increased vegetative presence raises the proportion of green pixels captured in street-level imagery, which the machine-learning model associates with higher subjectively rated aesthetic quality scores (Pedestrian Count proxy for streetscape preference).

scope.description: A single-signature intervention SIG_ADD_VEG_FG_SIZ that increases the amount and coverage of vegetation in the foreground tier (0–10 m). Linked evidence SVCs_P_Qiu2022_2 demonstrates that higher foreground vegetation view indices are positively associated (DIR_POS) with the indicator Pedestrian Count (IND_PED_CNT) under the performance dimension PRF_AES / PRS_AES_QUAL. The mechanism operates through enhanced green visibility at pedestrian eye level: as foreground tree canopy coverage grows, subjective streetscape quality ratings improve, attracting more pedestrian activity. The relationship is positive—greater vegetative coverage yields higher aesthetic preference scores—consistent with excerpt [7] ('People seemed to favor streetscapes with … more trees'). No effect size was reported (not numerically reported).
scope_validation_notes: Scope structure is internally consistent with signature_count and phase_count.
