operation.description: Add natural vegetation elements to increase naturalness levels in the middle ground of urban streetscapes, creating diverse textures and natural patterns that enhance visual complexity.

pathway.mechanism_description: Higher naturalness (increased vegetation texture and natural patterns) captures greater visual attention through bottom-up attentional mechanisms, resulting in more frequent visits, longer fixation durations, and increased glance counts. Natural elements provide visual novelty and biophilic stimuli that unconsciously draw viewer gaze and sustain attention.

signatures.mechanism: Introducing natural vegetation elements with diverse textures and natural patterns into the middle ground increases visual Habitat Diversity (HD) and Plant Community Structure (PCS), which raises the Naturalness Index. Higher naturalness attracts stronger visual focus—more eye visits, longer fixation durations, and increased glances—acting as a mediator between the landscape stimulus and favourable ecosystem aesthetic perception. Natural elements require lower cognitive load to process than artificial elements, freeing attentional resources that reinforce perceived Beauty and Imageability through Naturalness.

scope.description: SCP_S1 encodes a single operation SIG_ADD_VEG_MG_TEX: adding natural vegetation to the middle ground of urban streetscapes to introduce diverse textures and natural patterns. Linked evidence SVCs_P_Qiu2023_1 demonstrates that this intervention increases the Naturalness Index (IND_NAT) within the performance dimension PRF_AES (Aesthetic Preference, subdimension PRS_AES_NATU). The outcome measures—visual attention metrics including visits, fixations, and glances—show a positive relationship (DIR_POS) with a reported effect size of 66.67, confirming that the added vegetation textures attract significantly stronger visual focus than artificial elements, which in turn triggers favourable ecosystem aesthetic perception.
scope_validation_notes: Scope structure is internally consistent with signature_count and phase_count.

performance_effect.expected_change_note: Increase Visual attention (visits, fixations, glances)

source.citation: Qiu, Y., Pan, H., Kalantari, Z., Giusti, M., & Che, S. (2023). The natural focus: Combining deep learning and eye-tracking to understand public perceptions of urban ecosystem aesthetics. Ecological Indicators, 156, 111181.

operation.description: Modify the complexity of vegetation composition in the middleground by diversifying species mix, structural layers, and spatial arrangement patterns to create varied visual experiences and rich aesthetic qualities.

pathway.mechanism_description: Increased vegetation complexity enhances ecosystem aesthetic perception through multiple pathways: (1) species diversity creates varied color, texture, and form combinations that stimulate visual interest; (2) multi-layered structures provide depth and spatial richness; (3) heterogeneous spatial patterns generate visual rhythm and focal points; (4) complexity signals ecosystem health and naturalness, triggering positive psychological responses, consistent with the observed positive correlation between visual complexity and EAP.

signatures.mechanism: Diversifying the species mix, structural layering, and spatial arrangement patterns of middleground vegetation increases textural heterogeneity and visual pattern richness, which raises Visual Complexity (IND_CPX). The paper defines Complexity as 'Variation and richness of the landscape' and measures it through Visual Entropy (VE) and Plant Community Structure (PCS). Enhanced texture variety across species and vertical layers produces a more complex, legible composition that positively mediates Ecosystem Aesthetic Perception via soft fascination and ecological quality cues.

scope.description: SIG_MOD_VEG_MG_TEX encodes a single modification of vegetation texture/pattern complexity in the middleground tier. Linked evidence SVCs_P_Qiu2023_3 reports that Visual Complexity (IND_CPX) is positively associated (β = 0.564) with Ecosystem Aesthetic Perception (EAP) on the performance dimension PRF_AES (Aesthetic Preference, subdimension PRS_AES_QUAL). By increasing compositional heterogeneity—species mix, structural layers, and arrangement patterns—the intervention enriches visual texture, raising IND_CPX and thereby improving EAP in the positive direction (DIR_POS). The operation remains a single signature because only one element type (OBJ_VEG) in one depth zone (TER_MG) is manipulated; the three diversification tactics (species, layers, arrangement) collectively alter the same textural-pattern attribute of the vegetation ensemble.
scope_validation_notes: Scope structure is internally consistent with signature_count and phase_count.

performance_effect.expected_change_note: Increase Ecosystem Aesthetic Perception (EAP)

source.citation: Qiu, Y., Pan, H., Kalantari, Z., Giusti, M., & Che, S. (2023). The natural focus: Combining deep learning and eye-tracking to understand public perceptions of urban ecosystem aesthetics. Ecological Indicators, 156, 111181.

operation.description: Add vegetation with higher visual complexity and textural richness in the foreground of street view to increase visual interest and engagement along pedestrian pathways

pathway.mechanism_description: Adding visually rich foreground vegetation may capture and hold attention, plausibly reducing scanning-style eye movement. The linked evidence here, however, concerns Water View Index and glance count, not vegetation texture; a more closely matched evidence link is needed for this mechanism.

signatures.mechanism: Introducing texturally rich, visually complex vegetation (diverse foliage forms, layered planting) in the foreground of the street view increases green coverage and plant-community outer-profile variation (PCOV) while reducing the relative proportion of water visible in the scene (Water View Index). Since IND_WAT is negatively correlated with Average Glance Count (effect size −0.419), displacing water-dominated view area with complex vegetation raises glance frequency, thereby boosting active visual scanning and engagement.

scope.description: SIG_ADD_VEG_FG_TEX is a single-signature intervention that introduces texturally rich shrub-layer vegetation in the immediate foreground of pedestrian pathways. Per linked evidence SVCs_P_Qiu2023_4, the Water View Index (IND_WAT) exhibits a negative relationship (DIR_NEG, effect size −0.419) with Average Glance Count under the performance dimension PRF_AES (Aesthetic Preference, subdimension PRS_AES_PREF). By adding diverse, structurally complex vegetation that occupies foreground view area otherwise dominated by water surfaces, the intervention effectively lowers IND_WAT and consequently increases Average Glance Count—i.e., more active visual exploration and sustained aesthetic engagement. Supporting excerpts confirm that complexity and the presence of diverse natural elements attract visual focus (Glance positively correlated with Complexity and Beauty) and enhance Ecosystem Aesthetic Perception, while vertical vegetation complexity and plant-community profile variation are key visually perceptible indicators of landscape richness. The expected outcome direction is therefore an increase in aesthetic preference through heightened visual interest.
scope_validation_notes: Scope structure is internally consistent with signature_count and phase_count.

translation_signature_schema.signature_order: Operation × Semantic Layer × Spatial Layer × Morphological Layer

performance_effect.expected_change_note: Increase Average Glance Count

source.citation: Qiu, Y., Pan, H., Kalantari, Z., Giusti, M., & Che, S. (2023). The natural focus: Combining deep learning and eye-tracking to understand public perceptions of urban ecosystem aesthetics. Ecological Indicators, 156, 111181.

operation.description: Reduce the size and visual dominance of vegetation elements in the middle ground of street views to decrease the Road View Index (RVI) values.

pathway.mechanism_description: Decreasing vegetation size in the middleground reduces the RVI (vegetation visibility metric), which according to the negative direction relationship (DIR_NEG) leads to improved spatial quality scores. Lower vegetation dominance may enhance visual permeability and spatial openness perceived by users.

signatures.mechanism: Reducing the size and visual dominance of middle-ground vegetation (e.g., pruning or thinning canopy trees) decreases the proportion of green pixels that occlude the road plane in streetscape imagery, thereby lowering the computed Road View Index (RVI). Because RVI has a strong negative association with perceived spatial quality (effect size −0.7), a reduction in RVI translates to improved aesthetic quality scores.

scope.description: A single-signature operation SIG_MOD_VEG_MG_SIZ targets the size/amount of vegetation in the middle ground (10–50 m) through selective reduction (ACT_MOD on VAR_SIZ). As documented in evidence SVCs_P_Rui2023_2, the Road View Index (IND_RVI) is negatively correlated with the Spatial Quality Score under the aesthetic-preference performance dimension (PRF_AES / PRS_AES_QUAL) with an effect size of −0.7. By moderating the visual mass of middle-ground canopy trees—through crown reduction, selective thinning, or lower branch removal—the intervention decreases the vegetation-induced inflation of the road-view pixel ratio, thus lowering RVI and shifting aesthetic quality in the positive (DIR_NEG inverted) direction.
scope_validation_notes: Scope structure is internally consistent with signature_count and phase_count.

translation_signature_schema.signature_order: Operation × Semantic Layer × Spatial Layer × Morphological Layer

performance_effect.expected_change_note: May increase Spatial Quality Score

source.citation: Rui, Q., & Cheng, H. (2023). Quantifying the spatial quality of urban streets with open street view images: A case study of the main urban area of Fuzhou. Ecological Indicators, 156, 111204.

operation.description: Increase visible greenery and vegetation coverage within the visual field along walking routes to enhance Green View Index (GVI) measured through street-level imagery

pathway.mechanism_description: More visible street greenery is positively associated with self-reported leisure walking time, plausibly because more visually appealing routes can support the choice to walk for leisure. The linked evidence is correlational and does not directly establish a causal effect of increasing GVI on walking behavior.

signatures.mechanism: Adding canopy trees along neighbourhood walking routes increases the proportion of green pixels captured in street-level panoramic imagery, thereby raising the Green View Index (GVI). The study demonstrates that greenery at higher positions in the visual field (67.5°–90.0° elevation, corresponding to tall canopy trees) shows the strongest positive association with leisure walking time, indicating that the size/amount of overhead canopy is the critical variable driving the perceptual greenness of the streetscape.

scope.description: SIG_ADD_VEG_MG_SIZ encodes a single-signature intervention that increases the amount of street-level visible vegetation—primarily canopy trees—along walking routes. As evidenced by SVCs_P_Sakamoto2023_1, this raises the Green View Index (IND_GVI) which is positively associated (DIR_POS, β = 1.938 min/week per 1-point GVI increase, Model B) with the performance dimension PRF_USE (Spatial use and physical activity, subdimension PRS_WAL_WALK), measured as self-reported leisure walking time per week. The paper concludes that enriching street greenery, especially at higher canopy positions, encourages residents to extend their leisure walking time.
scope_validation_notes: Scope structure is internally consistent with signature_count and phase_count.

performance_effect.expected_change_note: Increase Self-reported leisure walking time per week

Effects of greenery at different heights in neighbourhood streetscapes on leisure walking: A cross-sectional study using machine learning of streetscape images in Sendai City, Japan. International Journal of Health Geographics, 22(1).

operation.description: Modify the ratio of vegetation interface depth (visual depth occupied by greenery) to the total visible distance along street corridors to optimize visual engagement and spatial enclosure.

pathway.mechanism_description: Higher Rider Ratio (visible cyclists/riders in the streetscape) is positively correlated with perceived visual comfort scores, suggesting that visible active-transport users may signal a comfortable, well-used street environment. Vegetation-depth interventions operate through a separate pathway not captured by this linked relationship.

signatures.mechanism: Adjusting the proportional depth of street-side vegetation canopy relative to the total visible corridor length changes the Rider Ratio (IND_RID_RAT). By calibrating how much of the visual corridor is occupied by greenery—neither too shallow (under-enclosed) nor too deep (over-enclosed)—the intervention tunes spatial enclosure and visual engagement to an optimal band, increasing perceived visual comfort.

scope.description: SIG_MOD_VEG_MG_SIZ encodes a single-layer adjustment of the amount of vegetation canopy depth experienced along the street corridor at middle-ground distances. Linked evidence SVCs_P_Shao2023_3 reports that the Rider Ratio indicator (IND_RID_RAT) is positively associated (R = 0.640, DIR_POS) with the performance dimension PRF_AES / PRS_AES_CMFT (Perceived Visual Comfort Score). The intervention modifies vegetation canopy depth so that the ratio of greenery-occupied visual depth to total visible corridor distance shifts toward the comfort-optimal range identified in the study, thereby increasing perceived visual comfort. Because only one object type (vegetation) is manipulated in one depth zone, the operation is classified as SCP_S1.
scope_validation_notes: Scope structure is internally consistent with signature_count and phase_count.

performance_effect.expected_change_note: Increase Perceived Visual Comfort Score

source.citation: Shao, Y., Yin, Y., Xue, Z., & Ma, D. (2023). Assessing and Comparing the Visual Comfort of Streets across Four Chinese Megacities Using AI-Based Image Analysis and the Perceptive Evaluation Method. Land, 12(4), 834.

operation.description: Increase the size and coverage of vegetation elements in the middle-ground visual field to enhance green view index in urban streetscapes

pathway.mechanism_description: Higher urbanization intensity is negatively correlated with GVI, meaning that more densely built-up areas tend to have less visible street greenery. Increasing middle-ground vegetation in such areas could partially offset this gap, but the linked evidence speaks to the urbanization-greenery relationship rather than directly to aesthetic perception.

signatures.mechanism: Increasing the size and areal coverage of vegetation elements in the middle-ground (10–50 m) visual field raises the proportion of green pixels captured in street-view imagery, thereby elevating the Green View Index (IND_GVI). The linked evidence (SVCs_P_Shen2018_1) shows a DIR_NEG relationship between GVI and urbanization level, meaning that higher greenery coverage corresponds to lower perceived urbanization and improved urban environmental quality.

scope.description: SCP_S1 operation comprising a single signature SIG_MOD_VEG_MG_SIZ. The intervention modifies the size and coverage attribute (VAR_SIZ) of middle-ground vegetation (OBJ_VEG, predominantly canopy trees) via ACT_MOD to enlarge and densify existing green elements in urban streetscapes. According to linked evidence SVCs_P_Shen2018_1, this directly increases the Green View Index (IND_GVI), the measured indicator. The performance dimension is PRF_AES / PRS_AES_QUAL (Aesthetic Preference – Perceived Aesthetic Quality). The evidence reports a DIR_NEG relationship between GVI and urbanization level: as greenery coverage rises, perceived urbanization decreases, signalling improved human-scale urban quality. Therefore, the expected outcome of this intervention is a positive shift in streetscape aesthetic quality through enhanced green visual proportion in the middle-ground field.
scope_validation_notes: Scope structure is internally consistent with signature_count and phase_count.

performance_effect.expected_change_note: May decrease Urbanization Level/Urban Quality

source.citation: Shen, Q., Zeng, W., Ye, Y., Arisona, S. M., Schubiger, S., Burkhard, R., & Qu, H. (2018). StreetVizor: Visual Exploration of Human-Scale Urban Forms Based on Street Views. IEEE Transactions on Visualization and Computer Graphics, 24(1), 1004–1013.

operation.description: Modify the shape and configuration of vegetation elements in the middleground to enhance visual diversity and aesthetic quality of streetscape composition.

pathway.mechanism_description: Human perception of streetscape aesthetics is positively correlated with the diversity and visual interest created by vegetation forms. Varying vegetation shapes in the middleground creates visual rhythm and focal points that enhance overall perceptual quality and user experience.

signatures.mechanism: Modifying the shape/outline of middleground vegetation elements (e.g., canopy form, crown silhouette) alters the visual complexity and compositional variety of the streetscape, directly contributing to perceived aesthetic quality as captured by the Human Perception Score.

Adjusting the spatial configuration/arrangement of vegetation elements in the middleground changes rhythmic spacing, grouping patterns, and layering, which enhances visual diversity and compositional balance perceived by observers and reflected in the overall Human Perception Score.

scope.description: This S2 coordinated intervention superimposes two modifications on middleground vegetation: SIG_MOD_VEG_MG_SHP (primary) adjusts the form and outline of vegetation elements, while SIG_MOD_VEG_MG_POS (secondary) reconfigures their spatial arrangement. Together they target streetscape compositional diversity. Linked evidence SVCs_P_Tao2022_2 reports a Human Perception Score (IND_HPS) with Mean=3.30 (SD=0.59) under performance dimension PRF_AES / PRS_AES_QUAL. The relationship direction is DIR_NA (descriptive baseline), meaning the combined shape-and-configuration modification is expected to shift perceived aesthetic quality upward from this measured baseline by enriching the visual rhythm and morphological variety of the vegetation layer in the 10–50 m social field.
scope_validation_notes: Scope structure is internally consistent with signature_count and phase_count.

source.citation: Tao, Y., Wang, Y., Wang, X., Tian, G., & Zhang, S. (2022). Measuring the Correlation between Human Activity Density and Streetscape Perceptions: An Analysis Based on Baidu Street View Images in Zhengzhou, China. Land, 11(3), 400.

operation.description: Add diverse vegetation forms and configurations in the middleground of streetscapes to enhance visual richness and perceptual quality of the street environment

pathway.mechanism_description: Adding diverse vegetation forms in the middleground may make streetscapes more visually engaging, which is plausibly associated with higher human activity density given the reported positive correlation between Human Perception Score and HAD. The linked evidence is correlational; a direct causal effect of vegetation interventions on activity density is not demonstrated.

signatures.mechanism: Introducing vegetation of diverse morphological forms (trees, shrubs, ground cover) and spatial configurations in the middleground layer increases the visual complexity and heterogeneity of the streetscape. This shape diversity enriches the scene's perceived greenness, enclosure, and layering, which elevates the Human Perception Score. The higher perceptual quality, in turn, is positively associated with greater Human Activity Density as people are drawn to visually engaging street environments.

scope.description: SCP_S1 encodes a single coordinated addition of diverse vegetation forms: SIG_ADD_VEG_MG_SHP. The intervention introduces varied vegetation shapes and planting arrangements into the middleground (10–50 m) zone of streetscapes. Linked evidence SVCs_P_Tao2022_3 demonstrates that streets with richer vegetation configurations score higher on the Human Perception Score (IND_HPS), which belongs to the Social Outcomes dimension (PRF_SOC, subdimension PRS_SOC_VITL). The relationship direction is DIR_POS with an effect size of 0.0347, meaning that the added shape diversity in vegetation positively increases Human Activity Density (HAD). The mechanism operates through enhanced visual richness—layered vegetation forms create a more enclosed, green, and perceptually stimulating streetscape that attracts and sustains pedestrian activity.
scope_validation_notes: Scope structure is internally consistent with signature_count and phase_count.

performance_effect.expected_change_note: Increase Human Activity Density (HAD)

source.citation: Tao, Y., Wang, Y., Wang, X., Tian, G., & Zhang, S. (2022). Measuring the Correlation between Human Activity Density and Streetscape Perceptions: An Analysis Based on Baidu Street View Images in Zhengzhou, China. Land, 11(3), 400.

operation.description: Increase the size and coverage of overhead tree canopy in the background layer of street corridors to enhance vertical greenery visibility

pathway.mechanism_description: Expanding overhead tree canopy size increases NDVI values and Green View Index by adding vegetation mass to the upper visual field, creating continuous green overhead coverage that is captured in both satellite imagery and street-level views

signatures.mechanism: Introducing new canopy trees along background-layer street corridors increases the total overhead green coverage detected by satellite NDVI, simultaneously raising eye-level Green View Index as new canopy mass enters the visual field

Enlarging the canopy spread of existing trees expands their overhead projection area, boosting NDVI values and increasing the proportion of green pixels captured in street-view imagery, thereby reinforcing vertical greenery visibility

scope.description: This S2 superposition coordinates two signatures operating on the same object and tier: SIG_ADD_VEG_BG_SIZ (primary) plants additional canopy trees to expand coverage, while SIG_MOD_VEG_BG_SIZ (secondary) increases the canopy spread of existing trees. Together they amplify overhead greenery measured by NDVI—the outcome variable in linked evidence SVCs_P_Tong2020_1—which exhibits a positive correlation (r = 0.492, DIR_POS) with the Green View Index (IND_GVI). By jointly raising NDVI through both addition and enlargement, the intervention enhances perceived naturalness (PRS_AES_NATU) within the aesthetic preference dimension (PRF_AES). The superposition pattern is appropriate because both signatures share the same variable (VAR_SIZ), tier (TER_BG), and object (OBJ_VEG/canopy_tree) yet employ distinct actions (ACT_ADD vs ACT_MOD) whose effects compound additively on the same indicator.
scope_validation_notes: Scope structure is internally consistent with signature_count and phase_count.

performance_effect.expected_change_note: Increase Overhead Greenery (NDVI)

source.citation: Tong, M., She, J., Tan, J., Li, M., Ge, R., & Gao, Y. (2020). Evaluating Street Greenery by Multiple Indicators Using Street-Level Imagery and Satellite Images: A Case Study in Nanjing, China. Forests, 11(12), 1347.

operation.description: Increase the amount and size of visible vegetation elements in the foreground of the visual field to enhance green view index as perceived from eye-level human perspective.

pathway.mechanism_description: Greater foreground vegetation size directly increases the proportion of green pixels in eye-level imagery, which correlates positively with human perception of greenness. The foreground positioning maximizes visual prominence and salience of vegetation elements, leading to higher self-reported perceived greenery scores.

signatures.mechanism: Introducing new vegetation elements (and increasing the dimensional extent of existing ones) within the immediate foreground of the street-level visual field directly raises the proportion of green pixels captured by image-based segmentation, thereby increasing the Green View Index. The strong correlation (r = 0.893) between semantic GVI and self-reported perceived greenery confirms that greater vegetated area in the eye-level scene translates into higher perceived naturalness.

scope.description: A single-signature intervention SIG_ADD_VEG_FG_SIZ that increases the amount and size of vegetation in the foreground tier. Linked evidence SVCs_P_Torkko2023_1 demonstrates that the indicator Green View Index (IND_GVI), computed via semantic object-based segmentation of street-level imagery, correlates strongly (r = 0.893) with self-reported perceived greenery (0–100 scale). The performance dimension is PRF_AES (Aesthetic Preference), subdimension PRS_AES_NATU, with a positive relationship direction (DIR_POS): adding foreground vegetation raises GVI, which in turn elevates perceived naturalness and greenery ratings. Because only one spatial variable (VAR_SIZ), one action (ACT_ADD), one object type (OBJ_VEG), and one depth zone (TER_FG) are involved, the operation is correctly scoped as SCP_S1.
scope_validation_notes: Scope structure is internally consistent with signature_count and phase_count.

performance_effect.expected_change_note: Increase Self-reported perceived greenery (0-100 scale)

source.citation: Torkko, J., Poom, A., Willberg, E., & Toivonen, T. (2023). How to best map greenery from a human perspective? Comparing computational measurements with human perception. Frontiers in Sustainable Cities, 5.

operation.description: Increase the proportion and visibility of green vegetation elements in the foreground of street-level views to enhance visual greenery perception from human perspective.

pathway.mechanism_description: Foreground vegetation occupies a larger angular share of the visual field than equivalent background vegetation, so increasing foreground green size raises GVI values. The descriptive evidence here concerns the comparison between mapped and reported greenery; whether foreground placement specifically improves the match between mapped and reported greenery is plausible but not directly tested.

signatures.mechanism: Adding green vegetation elements in the immediate street-level foreground increases the pixel proportion of greenery captured by image-based segmentation methods, thereby raising the Green View Index (IND_GVI). The study (SVCs_P_Torkko2023_2) demonstrates that computational GVI values derived from semantic segmentation of street-view imagery strongly correlate with human-reported perceived greenery, so increasing the physical amount of visible vegetation in the foreground directly enhances the perceived greenery value.

scope.description: A single-signature intervention SIG_ADD_VEG_FG_SIZ that increases the amount of green vegetation in the immediate foreground of street-level views. As evidenced by SVCs_P_Torkko2023_2, the Green View Index (IND_GVI) — computed via semantic image segmentation of street-level photography — serves as a strong proxy for human-perceived greenery. By adding vegetation elements that occupy a larger proportion of the foreground visual field, the intervention raises GVI values. This targets the performance dimension PRF_AES (Aesthetic Preference), subdimension PRS_AES_NATU (perceived naturalness). Although the linked evidence reports DIR_NA for relationship direction (the study validates measurement methods rather than testing a directional design intervention), the logical expectation is that a greater proportion of visible foreground greenery will improve perceived greenery ratings, consistent with the paper's finding that all tested computational greenery metrics correlate strongly with human perception of street-level greenery in Helsinki.
scope_validation_notes: Scope structure is internally consistent with signature_count and phase_count.

translation_signature_schema.signature_order: Operation × Semantic Layer × Spatial Layer × Morphological Layer

performance_effect.expected_change_note: Descriptive evidence only; directional effect on Perceived Greenery Value (Mapped vs Reported) is not established.

source.citation: Torkko, J., Poom, A., Willberg, E., & Toivonen, T. (2023). How to best map greenery from a human perspective? Comparing computational measurements with human perception. Frontiers in Sustainable Cities, 5.

operation.description: Add diverse vegetation types and textures in the middleground to increase visual variety and complexity of urban surroundings

pathway.mechanism_description: Adding diverse vegetation textures in the middleground increases visual variety and complexity of the surroundings. The linked evidence is descriptive of Diversity Index in relation to exploration of surroundings; whether richer textures actively encourage exploratory behaviour is plausible but not directly tested.

signatures.mechanism: Introducing canopy trees with distinct foliage textures increases the textural heterogeneity of the middleground scene, directly raising the Diversity Index (IND_DIV) by contributing a coarse-grained textural class to the pixel-level element composition captured in street-view imagery.

Adding shrubs of contrasting size and mass relative to canopy trees multiplies the number of distinct vegetation element classes detected in the scene, amplifying the Diversity Index (IND_DIV) through increased richness of identifiable urban element categories.

scope.description: This S2 superposition coordinates SIG_ADD_VEG_MG_TEX (primary, canopy_tree) and SIG_ADD_VEG_MG_SIZ (secondary, shrub) to simultaneously layer textural variety and size diversity into the middleground vegetation matrix. Together they target the Diversity Index (IND_DIV) linked in evidence SVCs_P_Verma2018_2: diverse vegetation types increase the count and evenness of identifiable urban element classes in street-view images, raising scene complexity. Under the performance dimension PRF_AES / PRS_AES_RICH, higher diversity is associated with greater visual richness and stimulated exploration of surroundings (Excerpt 13: 'exploration of the surroundings is stimulated by the diverse surroundings'). Because the source reports no directional effect size (no stable direction / no quantified effect size), the expected outcome direction is inferred qualitatively—adding diverse vegetation increases IND_DIV and thereby enhances aesthetic richness, though the magnitude remains unquantified.
scope_validation_notes: Scope structure is internally consistent with signature_count and phase_count.

performance_effect.expected_change_note: Descriptive evidence only; directional effect on Exploration of surroundings is not established.

source.citation: Verma, D., Jana, A., & Ramamritham, K. (2018). Quantifying Urban Surroundings Using Deep Learning Techniques: A New Proposal. Urban Science, 2(3), 78.

operation.description: Increase the size and coverage of natural vegetation elements in the middle ground of urban park scenes to enhance visible greenery and natural composition.

pathway.mechanism_description: Expanding natural vegetation size in the middleground increases the naturalness indicator (IND_NAT), which positively influences aesthetic performance (PRF_AES) by creating more appealing and nature-rich visual scenes. Greater natural elements enhance perceived attractiveness through biophilic responses and preference for natural landscapes over built environments.

signatures.mechanism: Increasing the size and areal coverage of natural vegetation in the middle ground raises the proportion of green pixels within the scene, directly elevating the Green View Index and, by extension, the Naturalness Index. Larger, denser canopy masses at 10–50 m strengthen the perception of lush, continuous greenery that dominates the visual field without occluding the full scene composition, thereby boosting perceived naturalness.

scope.description: A single-signature intervention SIG_MOD_VEG_MG_SIZ that modifies the size and coverage of existing vegetation in the middle ground of urban park scenes. Linked evidence SVCs_P_Zhao2024_1 demonstrates that an increase in the Naturalness Index (IND_NAT) is positively associated (DIR_POS, β = 0.60) with the Aesthetic Preference dimension (PRF_AES), specifically the Attractiveness subdimension (PRS_AES_ATTR). By enlarging vegetation mass at middle-ground distance, the visible proportion of natural elements rises relative to built and paved elements, increasing IND_NAT scores and consequently enhancing perceived attractiveness of the park scene. Excerpts [10] and [11] confirm that higher vegetation proportions positively impact preference, with thresholds around 30–48 % coverage being critical, supporting the rationale that expanding existing vegetation size/coverage in this zone is the operative lever.
scope_validation_notes: Scope structure is internally consistent with signature_count and phase_count.

translation_signature_schema.signature_order: Operation × Semantic Layer × Spatial Layer × Morphological Layer

performance_effect.expected_change_note: Increase Attractiveness (degree to which a park scene appeals to individuals)

source.citation: Zhao, X., Lu, Y., & Lin, G. (2024). An integrated deep learning approach for assessing the visual qualities of built environments utilizing street view images. Engineering Applications of Artificial Intelligence, 130, 107805.

operation.description: Simplify vegetation composition by reducing the diversity and complexity of plant species, structural layers, and spatial arrangements in urban green spaces to create more visually coherent and less cluttered landscapes.

pathway.mechanism_description: Reducing the variety of vegetation species and structural layers decreases the richness of the park scene. According to the linked evidence, higher Ground Cover Ratio is negatively correlated with Richness, so simplifying composition would be consistent with that relationship; effects on aesthetic perception are not directly characterized by the linked evidence.

signatures.mechanism: Reducing the diversity of plant species, structural layers, and spatial arrangements simplifies the visual texture and pattern complexity of the vegetation matrix. This modification lowers the compositional heterogeneity captured by the Ground Cover Ratio (IND_GRC), producing a more visually uniform and coherent green surface with fewer competing plant forms and layering contrasts.

scope.description: SIG_MOD_VEG_MG_TEX encodes a single-signature intervention that modifies the textural complexity of vegetation within urban green spaces. According to linked evidence SVCs_P_Zhao2024_2, the Ground Cover Ratio (IND_GRC) exhibits a negative relationship (β = −0.48) with the Richness subdimension (PRS_AES_RICH) of Aesthetic Preference (PRF_AES). This means that as the ground cover becomes more dominant and homogeneous—displacing multi-species, multi-layered assemblages—perceived richness (diversity and complexity of park elements) decreases. The intervention intentionally leverages this negative pathway: by simplifying vegetation composition (fewer species, fewer structural layers, streamlined spatial arrangements), the textural pattern of the vegetation field is made more uniform, directly reducing the visual richness score. The operation is a single attribute adjustment (ACT_MOD) of one element type (OBJ_VEG) within one perceptual tier, satisfying SCP_S1 criteria.
scope_validation_notes: Scope structure is internally consistent with signature_count and phase_count.

translation_signature_schema.signature_order: Operation × Semantic Layer × Spatial Layer × Morphological Layer

performance_effect.expected_change_note: Increase Richness (diversity and complexity of park elements)

source.citation: Zhao, X., Lu, Y., & Lin, G. (2024). An integrated deep learning approach for assessing the visual qualities of built environments utilizing street view images. Engineering Applications of Artificial Intelligence, 130, 107805.

operation.description: Add or increase shrub plantings in the middleground of urban green spaces to enhance visual enclosure and spatial intimacy.

pathway.mechanism_description: Increased shrub presence in the middleground creates spatial enclosure and visual softness that may reduce perceived openness and ambient brightness, potentially inducing feelings of melancholy or introspection. The intermediate scale vegetation moderates visual prospect and creates more enclosed, contemplative settings that correlate with depressive emotional responses.

signatures.mechanism: Introducing or increasing shrub mass in the middleground raises the Shrub Ratio (IND_SHR) within the scene. A greater proportion of shrub-level vegetation fills the intermediate visual field, strengthening perceived enclosure and spatial intimacy. According to the linked evidence (SVCs_P_Zhao2024_3), a higher shrub ratio is positively associated (β = 0.65) with the Depression perception dimension, meaning that as shrub proportion increases the scene is perceived as more melancholic or subdued in mood, likely through the enclosing, inward-focusing spatial character that dense shrub layers create.

scope.description: A single-signature intervention (SIG_ADD_VEG_MG_SIZ) that adds or increases shrub plantings in the middleground tier of urban green spaces. The operation directly elevates the Shrub Ratio indicator (IND_SHR) referenced in evidence SVCs_P_Zhao2024_3. That evidence links IND_SHR to the performance dimension PRF_AES (Aesthetic Preference), subdimension PRS_AES_QUAL, with the outcome measure Depression scored in a positive direction (DIR_POS, effect size 0.65). The increased shrub volume at 10–50 m enhances visual enclosure, which the study associates with stronger Depression perception—scenes feel more introspective and spatially intimate. Designers should calibrate shrub density to balance the desired enclosure benefit against the risk of excessive melancholic atmosphere.
scope_validation_notes: Scope structure is internally consistent with signature_count and phase_count.

translation_signature_schema.signature_order: Operation × Semantic Layer × Spatial Layer × Morphological Layer

performance_effect.expected_change_note: Increase Depression (extent to which a park induces feelings of melancholy)

source.citation: Zhao, X., Lu, Y., & Lin, G. (2024). An integrated deep learning approach for assessing the visual qualities of built environments utilizing street view images. Engineering Applications of Artificial Intelligence, 130, 107805.

operation.description: Modify the shape and form characteristics of water features in urban green spaces to enhance visual appeal and spatial definition

pathway.mechanism_description: Modifying the shape of water features influences aesthetic perception by creating distinctive visual focal points, enhancing spatial rhythm and balance, and generating dynamic reflective surfaces that increase landscape attractiveness through improved compositional harmony and sensory engagement.

signatures.mechanism: Adjusting the shape and form characteristics of water features alters how water bodies register in semantic segmentation of street view images, thereby changing the Water View Index. Varied edge geometry and spatial configuration of water elements can increase visual complexity and attractiveness, influencing the proportion of water pixels captured in the view.

scope.description: A single-signature intervention (SIG_MOD_WAT_MG_SHP) targeting the modification of water-feature geometry in urban green spaces. Linked evidence SVCs_P_Zhao2024_5 reports a descriptive Water View Index (IND_WAT) distribution (mean = 31.84%, SD = 14.67%, range 6.94%–64.19%) across sampled street view images. By modifying the shape and planform of water bodies, the intervention aims to shift the IND_WAT distribution toward configurations associated with higher aesthetic preference (PRF_AES, subdimension PRS_AES_ATTR – Attractiveness). Because the relationship direction is DIR_NA (non-linear / no single monotonic trend), the strategy is not simply to maximise water area but to optimise its form—edge complexity, curvature, and spatial definition—so that the resulting water view proportion falls within the range most positively perceived. The evidence indicates substantial variability in existing water visibility, suggesting that shape modifications can meaningfully reposition a site along this distribution.
scope_validation_notes: Scope structure is internally consistent with signature_count and phase_count.

translation_signature_schema.signature_order: Operation × Semantic Layer × Spatial Layer × Morphological Layer

performance_effect.expected_change_note: Descriptive evidence only; directional effect on Attractiveness is not established.

source.citation: Zhao, X., Lu, Y., & Lin, G. (2024). An integrated deep learning approach for assessing the visual qualities of built environments utilizing street view images. Engineering Applications of Artificial Intelligence, 130, 107805.

operation.description: Increase building density and height to reduce sky view factor in urban areas, creating more enclosed spatial configurations through vertical development.

pathway.mechanism_description: Higher building density and taller structures reduce SVF by blocking sky visibility, which correlates with increased total population through higher residential capacity and urban density. The negative relationship indicates that lower SVF (more enclosed spaces) associates with higher population concentrations in socially active urban environments.

signatures.mechanism: Adding built volume through increased building density and height introduces additional facade mass into the urban canyon, progressively occluding the overhead sky hemisphere from street level and thereby reducing Sky View Factor (SVF). The accumulated vertical surfaces create a more enclosed three-dimensional spatial configuration.

scope.description: SCP_S1 operation comprising a single signature SIG_ADD_BLT_BG_SIZ. As evidenced by SVCs_P_Amiruzzaman2023_4, the indicator Sky View Factor (IND_SVF) exhibits a negative relationship (r = −0.400) with Total Population under the performance dimension PRF_SOC / PRS_SOC_DEMO. The intervention adds built facade mass at the background tier, increasing urban enclosure and reducing SVF. The evidence shows that areas with lower SVF (i.e., denser, taller building configurations blocking the sky) are associated with higher total population—consistent with the study's finding that 'inner-city areas are covered with high-rise buildings…with a high population where high-rise buildings prevent fully viewing the sky from the street level' and that higher building variety and density correlate with greater population density. The expected outcome direction is that increased built volume decreases SVF, which is associated with increased population density.
scope_validation_notes: Scope structure is internally consistent with signature_count and phase_count.

performance_effect.expected_change_note: Increase Total Population

source.citation: Amiruzzaman, M., Zhao, Y., Amiruzzaman, S., Karpinski, A. C., & Wu, T. H. (2023). An AI-based framework for studying visual diversity of urban neighborhoods and its relationship with socio-demographic variables. Journal of Computational Social Science, 6(1), 315–337.

operation.description: Increase the quantity and coverage of vegetation elements at pedestrian eye-level (foreground) to enhance green view index visibility from ground perspective, including street trees, hedges, and vertical greenery within 0-10m viewing distance.

pathway.mechanism_description: Pedestrian-level vegetation directly occupies the visual field captured by street-view analysis, increasing GVI percentage. Higher foreground greenery creates immediate visual contact with nature, enhancing perceived abundance and satisfaction with urban greenery through increased green pixel proportion in human visual cone.

signatures.mechanism: Adding vegetation elements (street trees, hedges, vertical greenery) within the 0–10 m pedestrian eye-level zone directly increases the pixel proportion of green in street-view imagery. Because GVI is computed as the ratio of semantically segmented green pixels to total pixels in a panoramic image captured at ground level (excerpt [1], [13]), introducing new plant mass in the foreground tier maximises per-pixel green contribution owing to perspective foreshortening, thereby raising the Green View Index value.

scope.description: A single signature SIG_ADD_VEG_FG_SIZ encodes this intervention. It adds vegetation quantity and coverage (VAR_SIZ × ACT_ADD) in the foreground tier (TER_FG) to the object class OBJ_VEG. The linked evidence SVCs_P_Wang2023_1 associates the Green View Index (IND_GVI) with the performance dimension PRF_AES (Aesthetic Preference, subdimension PRS_AES_PREF) through a positive relationship (DIR_POS, β = 0.09). The intervention therefore increases GVI, which in turn is expected to improve Perceived Greenery Satisfaction. The paper notes that current GVI values in Hong Kong are generally low (0–0.2, excerpt [10]) and that greening visible at pedestrian eye-level directly shapes walking perception (excerpts [11], [17]). By concentrating added vegetation within the immediate sensory field, SIG_ADD_VEG_FG_SIZ targets the exact spatial zone captured by street-view semantic segmentation, producing the most efficient uplift in GVI per unit of planted area.
scope_validation_notes: Scope structure is internally consistent with signature_count and phase_count.

source.citation: Wang, H., & van Ameijde, J. (2023). Quantifying the Impact of Pedestrian-level Greening on the Perception of Walking. CAADRIA Proceedings, 2, 451–460.

operation.description: Increase the size and coverage of urban greenery (trees, shrubs, and grass) in the middleground area to enhance tree view factor visibility from pedestrian viewpoints.

pathway.mechanism_description: Expanded vegetation coverage increases the Tree View Factor (TVF), which mediates thermal comfort through microclimate regulation mechanisms including solar radiation interception, evapotranspiration cooling, and air temperature reduction. Higher TVF correlates with improved Thermal Comfort Vote (TCV) by creating cooler microclimatic conditions in urban spaces.

signatures.mechanism: Adding canopy trees in the middleground directly increases the Tree View Factor (TVF) by enlarging the vertical overhead canopy coverage captured in upward fisheye imagery from pedestrian viewpoints, thereby mediating microclimate variables (reducing solar radiation penetration, moderating wind) that positively shift Thermal Comfort Vote (TCV).

Adding shrubs at the middleground layer supplements the tree canopy stratum by shielding low-level solar radiation in winter and increasing overall greenery density, reinforcing the microclimate regulation pathway through which TVF improvements translate into better thermal comfort outcomes.

Adding grass ground cover in the middleground completes the multi-layer planting structure, contributing to the overall greenery area in sight and supporting the composite vegetation mass that underpins improved microclimate moderation captured by the TVF–TCV pathway.

scope.description: This S2 coordinated intervention employs a superposition pattern, layering three vegetation strata within the same middleground depth zone. The primary signature SIG_ADD_VEG_MG_SIZ (canopy_tree) directly increases the Tree View Factor (IND_TVF) by expanding overhead canopy coverage as measured from vertical-upward fisheye images at pedestrian level. Two secondary signatures—SIG_ADD_VEG_MG_SIZ (shrub) and SIG_ADD_VEG_MG_SIZ (grass)—augment the planting structure by filling the intermediate and ground strata, reinforcing microclimate regulation through wind attenuation and low-angle solar radiation shielding. Per linked evidence SVCs_P_Wang2022_4, increases in TVF positively mediate microclimate variables (mean radiant temperature, wind speed) that in turn improve Thermal Comfort Vote (TCV), within the performance dimension PRF_THR (Thermal Comfort), subdimension PRS_THR_VOTE, with a strong positive relationship (DIR_POS, effect size 0.8902). The multi-layer superposition ensures that the canopy, shrub, and grass layers operate simultaneously to maximise the vertical and horizontal greenery coverage perceived from pedestrian viewpoints.
scope_validation_notes: Scope structure is internally consistent with signature_count and phase_count.

operation.description: Add vegetation layers in the middleground to reduce building visibility and create natural visual screening between viewer and built structures

pathway.mechanism_description: Adding middleground vegetation can screen built surfaces, lowering the Building Visibility Index and potentially reducing perceived depression scores through enhanced exposure to natural elements. The linked evidence here, however, concerns Water View Index, not vegetation or Building Visibility Index; a more closely matched evidence link is needed to support this mechanism.

signatures.mechanism: Introducing vegetation layers (shrubs and understory planting) in the middleground increases green visual coverage and creates a natural screening buffer that reduces the proportion of visible built facades. This restructures the visual composition of the scene, modifying view indices including the Water View Index by altering relative proportions of landscape elements in the field of view. The enriched greenery triggers positive psychological responses through visual-perceptual pathways, consistent with the paper's finding that vegetation in sight directly regulates comfort and mental feelings (Excerpts 6, 11, 14).

scope.description: SCP_S1 operation comprising a single signature SIG_ADD_VEG_MG_SIZ. The intervention adds vegetation volume in the middleground tier to visually screen built structures. According to linked evidence SVCs_P_Wang2022_5, this manipulation influences the Water View Index (IND_WAT) — by increasing vegetated area in the visual field, the relative share of other view components (water, built, sky) is redistributed. The performance dimension is PRF_RST (Stress relief and psychological restoration), subdimension PRS_MNT_DEPR (Perceived Depression Score, 0–100), with a DIR_POS relationship, meaning that the vegetative screening intervention is associated with improved (reduced) depression outcomes. The paper supports this through evidence that greenery perceived via the Green View Index directly regulates psychological well-being and thermal comfort through visual stimulation rather than solely through microclimatic mediation (Excerpts 6, 11, 14).
scope_validation_notes: Scope structure is internally consistent with signature_count and phase_count.

operation.description: Reduce visual dominance of pavement in streetscapes by introducing vegetation and permeable surfaces to improve visual walkability classification

pathway.mechanism_description: Reducing the pixel proportion of hard pavement visible in street-level imagery shifts the Type of Visual Walkability classification from pavement-dominated toward greenery-dominated. Panoptic segmentation quantifies both pixel coverage and object instances, so interventions that replace pavement with vegetated surfaces directly alter the TVW classification and its seasonal stability.

signatures.mechanism: Removing obstructive building masses in the middleground view corridor eliminates visual barriers, opening sightlines to background vegetation. This reduces built-environment pixel dominance in panoptic segmentation, thereby increasing the Panoramic View Green View Index (PVGVI). Evidence SVCs_P_Wang2024_4 reports a negative coefficient (−0.066) between Water View Index (IND_WAT) and PVGVI, indicating that non-green visual elements competing for pixel share suppress green visibility; removing middleground built mass reverses this suppression and improves perceived walkability (PRF_USE / PRS_WAL_PERC).

scope.description: SCP_S1 encodes a single operation: SIG_REM_BLT_MG_SIZ. The intervention removes or substantially eliminates obstructive building facades in the 10–50 m middleground zone so that background vegetation becomes visually accessible. Linked evidence SVCs_P_Wang2024_4 shows that the Water View Index (IND_WAT) has a negative relationship (−0.066) with the Panoramic View Green View Index (PVGVI), implying that non-vegetation visual elements occupying pixel share reduce greenery perception. By removing built mass, the proportion of vegetation pixels in street-level panoramic imagery increases, improving the PVGVI outcome within the spatial use and physical activity performance dimension (PRF_USE, subdimension PRS_WAL_PERC). The expected outcome direction is positive: less built obstruction → higher green view → enhanced visual walkability.
scope_validation_notes: Scope structure is internally consistent with signature_count and phase_count.

operation.description: Increase tree view factor (TVF) in courtyard spaces by adding vegetation elements that enhance visual canopy coverage from occupant viewpoints

pathway.mechanism_description: Higher Tree View Factor reflects more visible canopy coverage, which is negatively associated with heat-discomfort frequency above a threshold, per the linked evidence. Plausible contributing pathways include perceived shading/cooling cues and, where canopy is dense enough, biophysical cooling, though the threshold evidence does not directly partition physiological from psychological effects.

signatures.mechanism: Introducing canopy trees into courtyard spaces increases the proportion of the visual field occupied by tree canopy (Tree View Factor). The added vertical vegetation intercepts incoming shortwave and longwave radiation within the occupant's hemispherical view, raising TVF above the preferred threshold of 0.18 identified in the source study.

scope.description: SIG_ADD_VEG_FG_SIZ encodes the addition of canopy trees to courtyard spaces to increase the Tree View Factor (IND_TVF). According to linked evidence SVCs_P_Wu2023_4, a higher TVF is associated with reduced Heat Discomfort Frequency on the thermal comfort dimension (PRF_THR / PRS_THR_VOTE) with a negative relationship direction (DIR_NEG) and an effect-size threshold of 0.18. The intervention therefore adds vegetation volume within the immediate courtyard envelope so that tree canopy coverage, as seen from occupant viewpoints, rises, directly lowering the frequency of thermal discomfort episodes.
scope_validation_notes: Scope structure is internally consistent with signature_count and phase_count.

operation.description: Increase the size and density of vegetation elements in the foreground of street spaces, particularly at eye-level and within immediate visual proximity to pedestrians in Nanjing urban streets.

pathway.mechanism_description: Expanding foreground vegetation coverage directly increases the Green View Index (GVI) by occupying a larger proportion of the visual field captured from pedestrian viewpoints. Street-level greenery in the foreground has the most significant impact on perceived greenery exposure due to its proximity and visual dominance in the observer's field of view.

signatures.mechanism: Adding vegetation volume and density in the immediate pedestrian foreground directly increases the proportion of green pixels captured in street-view imagery, thereby raising the Green View Index (GVI). In streets identified as having high accessibility but limited greenery visibility, new tree and shrub plantings at eye-level fill the visual field within the 0–10 m zone, boosting greenery exposure for daily pedestrian encounters.

scope.description: A single-signature intervention SIG_ADD_VEG_FG_SIZ targets the addition of vegetation size and density in the immediate foreground of Nanjing's urban streets. Linked evidence SVCs_P_Wu2024_1 demonstrates that the Green View Index (IND_GVI), measured through semantic segmentation of Baidu Street View images, is positively associated (DIR_POS, effect size 26.50) with equitable greenery exposure across the social outcomes dimension (PRF_SOC / PRS_SOC_EQTY). The study identified approximately 139.62 km of roads with good accessibility but insufficient greenery visibility, establishing a clear spatial equity deficit. By adding vegetative mass—primarily canopy trees and associated shrub layers—within the pedestrian foreground, the intervention directly increases GVI values on these priority streets, thereby improving residents' daily visual contact with greenery and advancing equitable distribution of green space benefits across the most-frequented urban corridors.
scope_validation_notes: Scope structure is internally consistent with signature_count and phase_count.

operation.description: Reduce the visual coverage of green vegetation in the middle ground of street views to decrease overall greenery density and visibility

pathway.mechanism_description: Lower Green View Index (GVI) reduces visual screening and natural surveillance opportunities, creating less defined territorial boundaries and potentially increasing perceived vulnerability. Reduced vegetation coverage may expose more undefined spaces and decrease environmental legibility, leading to lower perceived safety scores

signatures.mechanism: Removing middle-ground vegetation elements reduces the proportion of green pixels (trees, grass, plants) captured in street-view imagery, thereby lowering the composite Green View Index (GVI). The greenness formula aggregates tree, grass, and plant pixel ratios across four viewing directions; removing canopy trees in the 10–50 m zone directly diminishes these pixel proportions and overall greenery density.

scope.description: A single-signature intervention (SIG_REM_VEG_MG_SIZ) that removes vegetative mass in the middle-ground tier to reduce the Green View Index (GVI). Linked evidence SVCs_P_Dai2021_2 reports a regression coefficient of −0.575 between GVI and perceived safety (IND_GVI → PRS_SOC_SAFE within PRF_SOC). The negative relationship direction (DIR_NEG) indicates that as the intervention decreases greenery coverage, perceived safety scores decline. This encodes the inverse scenario tested in the study: streets with lower visual greenness are associated with reduced feelings of safety among residents.
scope_validation_notes: Scope structure is internally consistent with signature_count and phase_count.

operation.description: Reduce the size or density of middleground vegetation to decrease visual enclosure in the streetscape

pathway.mechanism_description: Lower Green View Index creates less spatial enclosure, which indirectly reduces aesthetic preference by diminishing the sense of shelter and definition in the visual field. The negative relationship operates through the mediating factor of enclosure perception.

signatures.mechanism: Reducing the size or density of middleground canopy trees lowers the Green View Index (IND_GVI) by decreasing the proportion of vegetation pixels in the streetscape view. This in turn reduces visual enclosure—the sense of being hemmed in by dense tree canopies and buildings—which the evidence (SVCs_P_Dai2021_4) shows mediates aesthetic preference. A lower enclosure degree alleviates depressing perception and can shift aesthetic evaluation, consistent with the reported negative indirect effect (β = -0.38) of GVI on aesthetic preference via enclosure.

scope.description: SCP_S1 comprising a single signature SIG_MOD_VEG_MG_SIZ. The intervention modifies (ACT_MOD) the size/density (VAR_SIZ) of middleground (TER_MG) vegetation (OBJ_VEG, subtype canopy_tree) to decrease visual enclosure in the streetscape. Linked evidence SVCs_P_Dai2021_4 reports that the Green View Index (IND_GVI) exerts a negative indirect effect (β = -0.38) on aesthetic preference (PRF_AES / PRS_AES_PREF) mediated by enclosure: higher greenness increases enclosure, which suppresses positive aesthetic perception. By strategically reducing canopy mass or tree density at the middleground scale, GVI and enclosure both decrease, yielding an expected positive shift in aesthetic preference (DIR_NEG relationship reversed through the reduction intervention).
scope_validation_notes: Scope structure is internally consistent with signature_count and phase_count.

operation.description: Enhance walkable street network with integrated vegetation to improve both street connectivity and visible greenery

pathway.mechanism_description: Street-level greenery (GVI) is positively correlated with housing price at the county/jiedao level in Shanghai, indicating that visible vegetation aligns with higher property values. The walkable-network pathway described in the operation is plausible as a confounder or co-correlate but is not directly the relationship in this linked evidence.

signatures.mechanism: Introducing additional vegetation and enlarging existing plantings in the immediate foreground increases the proportion of green pixels captured in eye-level street-view imagery, directly raising the Green View Index (GVI) percentage.

scope.description: SIG_ADD_VEG_FG_SIZ is a single-signature intervention that adds vegetation volume and coverage within the foreground tier to boost the Green View Index (IND_GVI). Linked evidence SVCs_P_Xiao2021_1 demonstrates a positive relationship (DIR_POS, effect size 0.433) between increased GVI and the performance dimension PRF_SOC (Social Outcomes, subdimension PRS_SOC_DEMO), specifically housing price at county/jiedao level in downtown Shanghai. By adding vegetation size and quantity in the immediate visual field, the intervention raises the share of green pixels in street-view images, shifting GVI values upward and contributing to positive socio-demographic associations in the neighbourhood.

operation.description: Increase the quantity and coverage of vegetation elements in the middleground visual field to achieve higher Green View Index (GVI) threshold levels in urban streetscapes.

pathway.mechanism_description: Increasing middleground vegetation raises the Green View Index. The linked evidence reports a positive correlation between GVI and the Discomfort Index in this study; if DI is scaled so that higher values indicate greater discomfort, higher GVI would correspond to greater rather than lower discomfort under the reported correlation. The mechanism should explicitly address the DI scale and align its directional claim with the linked evidence.

signatures.mechanism: Adding vegetation quantity and coverage in the middleground increases the proportion of green pixels captured in streetscape imagery, directly raising the Green View Index (GVI). Greater canopy and foliar mass at this distance provides radiative shielding and evapotranspirational cooling, lowering the Discomfort Index (DI) perceived by pedestrians.

scope.description: SCP_S1 encodes a single operation, SIG_ADD_VEG_MG_SIZ, that increases the amount and spatial coverage of vegetation within the middleground (10–50 m) zone of urban streetscapes. According to linked evidence SVCs_P_Xin2024_2, this intervention raises the Green View Index (IND_GVI), which is positively associated (DIR_POS, β = 0.3598) with the thermal-comfort performance dimension (PRF_THR / PRS_THR_COOL), as measured by the Discomfort Index (DI). Higher GVI thresholds achieved through greater middleground vegetation presence promote cooler micro-climatic conditions and more positive thermal comfort perceptions, consistent with the source paper's recommendation to plan a large amount of touchable nature provided by vegetation in urban parks to mitigate adverse thermal environments.

operation.description: Increase the quantity and coverage of vegetation in the middle ground view to enhance the Green View Index (GVI) within the urban visual field.

pathway.mechanism_description: Higher GVI through increased middleground vegetation is negatively associated with sad facial-expression scores in regression analyses. Plausible contributing pathways include visual access to nature supporting positive affect, though the linked evidence is observational and does not directly demonstrate the underlying psychological mechanism.

signatures.mechanism: Adding vegetation quantity and coverage in the middle-ground view directly increases the proportion of green pixels captured in streetscape imagery, thereby raising the Green View Index (GVI). Greater visible greenery in the 10–50 m social field enhances perceived naturalness and thermal comfort cues, which in turn reduces negative emotional responses (sad facial expressions).

scope.description: A single-signature intervention (SIG_ADD_VEG_MG_SIZ) that increases the quantity and areal coverage of vegetation within the middle-ground viewing zone of urban park streetscapes. According to linked evidence SVCs_P_Xin2024_3, raising the Green View Index (IND_GVI) is associated with a negative relationship (DIR_NEG, effect size −0.20) with the Sad Score measured via facial expression analysis, operating within the Emotion regulation and cognitive modulation performance dimension (PRF_EMO) and its Mood subdimension (PRS_MNT_MOOD). Therefore, adding vegetation mass in TER_MG is expected to elevate GVI and consequently reduce sadness-related emotional perception among park visitors.

operation.description: Modify building height, mass, and spatial configuration to control the proportion of visible sky hemisphere in urban canyon spaces

pathway.mechanism_description: Building geometry directly determines the occlusion angle of the sky dome from ground-level viewpoints. Reduced building heights, increased setbacks, or optimized building arrangement increases the visible sky portion, thereby raising SVF values. The SVF range (likely 0-1 scale) in Qinhuai District reflects varying degrees of sky obstruction by built form across different urban morphologies

signatures.mechanism: Adjusting building height and volumetric mass directly changes the extent of sky hemisphere obstruction as viewed from street-level observation points, thereby modifying the Sky View Factor. Taller, bulkier facades intercept a larger solid angle of the sky dome, reducing SVF; lower, thinner massing increases it.

Reconfiguring the spatial arrangement of buildings alters canyon geometry—width-to-height ratio, building setbacks, and orientation—redistributing sky exposure across observation points and modulating the spatial pattern of SVF values throughout the canyon network.

scope.description: SIG_MOD_BLT_MG_SIZ (primary) and SIG_MOD_BLT_MG_POS (secondary) operate in superposition to jointly control the Sky View Factor (IND_SVF) referenced in evidence SVCs_P_Xu2024_1. The primary signature targets building height and mass—the dominant geometric parameters governing how much of the sky hemisphere is obstructed at street level. The secondary signature targets spatial configuration (spacing, setbacks, canyon proportions), which distributes that obstruction across the urban cross-section. Their coordinated modification produces the observed SVF range of 0.15–0.85 across Qinhuai District. This links to the thermal comfort performance dimension (PRF_THR, subdimension PRS_THR_MRT): SVF governs incoming and outgoing longwave radiation exchange within the canyon, directly influencing mean radiant temperature experienced by pedestrians. The relationship direction is reported as DIR_NA (descriptive/mapping study; no single monotonic trend isolated), consistent with the evidence's descriptive effect-size characterisation.
scope_validation_notes: Scope structure is internally consistent with signature_count and phase_count.

operation.description: Modify building height and configuration to increase sky view factor in urban streetscapes, allowing more open sky visibility from ground level viewing positions.

pathway.mechanism_description: Increased SVF enhances visual openness and spatial perception by expanding the visible sky dome, reducing visual enclosure from built environment, and improving the sense of spaciousness which positively contributes to visual quality assessment scores.

signatures.mechanism: Reducing building height lowers the vertical obstruction of sky from street-level viewpoints, directly enlarging the visible sky dome and increasing Sky View Factor

Reconfiguring building massing and plan arrangement (setbacks, stepped profiles, staggered footprints) opens additional sky-exposure corridors between and above structures, further increasing SVF beyond what height reduction alone achieves

scope.description: An S2 coordinated deformation combining SIG_MOD_BLT_MG_SIZ (primary) and SIG_MOD_BLT_MG_SHP (secondary). The two signatures jointly reshape the built envelope of the street canyon: the first reduces facade height while the second reconfigures three-dimensional building massing. As documented by linked evidence SVCs_P_Ye2019_5, this coordinated modification increases Sky View Factor (IND_SVF), which exhibits a positive relationship (DIR_POS, effect size = 0.33) with the Aesthetic Preference dimension (PRF_AES), specifically visual quality scores under subdimension PRS_AES_QUAL. By widening the visible sky dome from pedestrian-level viewpoints, the intervention improves the perceived visual quality of streetscapes as rated by expert urban designers in the Ye et al. (2019) evaluation model trained on ~140,000 Baidu Street View images.

operation.description: Add vegetation along street walkways to enhance textural variety and visual richness in the pedestrian environment, creating layered visual experiences through diverse plant forms and patterns.

pathway.mechanism_description: Higher Visual Walkability Ratio is positively associated with visual quality scores, suggesting that streetscapes legible and supportive for pedestrians tend to be rated as visually higher-quality. Vegetation-texture additions may contribute to walkability ratio indirectly but are not the direct variable in the linked evidence.

signatures.mechanism: Introducing canopy trees along the walkway establishes an upper vegetation layer whose foliage patterns and bark textures add coarse-grained textural richness, increasing the greenery pixel ratio captured in street-view imagery and thereby raising the Visual Walkability Ratio.

Adding a mid-height shrub layer provides medium-grained textures and leaf-form diversity that visually bridge the canopy and ground planes, amplifying the diversity key element identified in the SegNet segmentation and contributing to perceived visual quality.

Ground-cover planting fills the lowest visual band with fine-grained textural patterns, completing the vertical vegetation layering and maximising the pixel-wise greenery proportion that the evaluation model associates with higher visual quality scores.

scope.description: This S2 superposition intervention coordinates three vegetation signatures—SIG_ADD_VEG_FG_TEX (canopy_tree), SIG_ADD_VEG_FG_TEX (shrub), and SIG_ADD_VEG_FG_TEX (ground_cover)—within the same foreground tier along street walkways. As documented in evidence SVCs_P_Ye2019_6, street greenery is one of six key pixel-segmented elements driving the Visual Walkability Ratio (IND_WLK_RAT). By stacking three distinct vegetation strata in the pedestrian zone, the intervention simultaneously increases greenery pixel share and the diversity element, producing a positive (DIR_POS) shift in the visual quality score under the Aesthetic Preference dimension (PRF_AES / PRS_AES_QUAL), with a reported effect size of 0.04. The superposition pattern ensures that the layered plant forms generate overlapping textural variety rather than uniform green mass, directly addressing the source paper's recommendation that 'encouraging street greenery should be considered as a priority strategy in promoting high visual quality in streets.'
scope_validation_notes: Scope structure is internally consistent with signature_count and phase_count.

operation.description: Increase the amount and coverage of urban greenspace vegetation in residential neighborhoods and community areas accessible to older adults, enhancing the overall size and extent of vegetated areas within the urban fabric.

pathway.mechanism_description: Greater neighbourhood vegetation coverage is positively associated with mental wellbeing scores among older adults. Plausible contributing pathways include increased opportunities for nature contact and outdoor activity, though the linked evidence is correlational and does not directly partition contributions from contact, activity, social interaction, or environmental quality.

signatures.mechanism: Introducing additional vegetation coverage across residential neighborhoods raises the aggregate greenness captured by satellite-derived NDVI and land-cover-based vegetation coverage metrics. Greater total vegetated area within the urban fabric increases older adults' routine exposure to greenspace, which—mediated by improved environmental perceptions (aesthetics, noise reduction, satisfaction with recreational opportunities)—supports mental wellbeing.

scope.description: A single-signature operation (SIG_ADD_VEG_BG_SIZ) that adds vegetated area at the neighbourhood massing scale. Linked evidence SVCs_P_Yue2022_1 uses the Total Vegetation Index (IND_GVI), operationalised through NDVI (Landsat-8, 30 m) and land-cover vegetation coverage computed within residential neighbourhood boundaries. The intervention increases IND_GVI by expanding the absolute extent and density of greenspace across neighbourhood buffers. The performance dimension is PRF_EMO (Emotion regulation and cognitive modulation), subdimension PRS_MNT_WLBG (Mental Wellbeing), with a positive relationship direction (DIR_POS) and an effect size of 1.06, indicating that greater neighbourhood-level vegetation quantity is associated with higher mental wellbeing scores among older adults (≥60 years).
scope_validation_notes: Scope structure is internally consistent with signature_count and phase_count.

translation_signature_schema.signature_order: Operation × Semantic Layer × Spatial Layer × Morphological Layer

performance_effect.expected_change_note: Increase Mental Wellbeing Score

source.citation: Yue, Y., Yang, D., & Van Dyck, D. (2022). Urban greenspace and mental health in Chinese older adults: Associations across different greenspace measures and mediating effects of environmental perceptions. Health & Place, 76, 102856.

operation.description: Increase the amount and coverage of vegetation in the middle-ground visual field to enhance green view index (GVI) within the typical viewing range of older adults in urban settings.

pathway.mechanism_description: Higher GVI creates more visible greenery in the middle-ground visual field, which may provide psychological benefits through nature exposure, though this study found no significant relationship with mental wellbeing scores in Chinese older adults, suggesting other mediating factors or contextual variables may influence the green space-mental health relationship.

signatures.mechanism: Adding vegetation amount and coverage in the middle-ground streetscape increases the proportion of green pixels captured at eye-level, thereby raising the Green View Index (GVI). In this study, streetscape greenness (the GVI proxy) was measured from Tencent Street View imagery using a fully convolutional neural network, representing the visual green exposure older adults encounter during daily activities in their neighbourhood.

scope.description: A single-signature intervention (SIG_ADD_VEG_MG_SIZ) that increases the amount and spatial coverage of vegetation—primarily canopy trees—within the middle-ground visual field (10–50 m) of urban streetscapes frequented by older adults. According to linked evidence SVCs_P_Yue2022_2, the target indicator is the Green View Index (IND_GVI), measured as streetscape greenness from street-view imagery. The performance dimension is Emotion regulation and cognitive modulation (PRF_EMO), subdimension Wellbeing (PRS_MNT_WLBG), with the outcome measure being a Mental Wellbeing Score. The reported relationship direction is DIR_NS (not statistically significant; standardised coefficient β = 0.25, p > 0.05), meaning that while the point estimate is positive, the streetscape-level GVI measure did not reach significance for older adults' mental wellbeing in this sample—unlike overhead-view metrics (NDVI, vegetation coverage) which were significant. The intervention therefore targets a plausible but empirically uncertain pathway at the street-view scale.
scope_validation_notes: Scope structure is internally consistent with signature_count and phase_count.
