performance_effect.expected_change_note: No stable directional effect established for Mental Wellbeing Score.

source.citation: Yue, Y., Yang, D., & Van Dyck, D. (2022). Urban greenspace and mental health in Chinese older adults: Associations across different greenspace measures and mediating effects of environmental perceptions. Health & Place, 76, 102856.

operation.description: Modify tree crown shape to optimize shading coverage on road surfaces through selective pruning or species selection that promotes specific canopy geometries (e.g., spreading vs. columnar forms).

pathway.mechanism_description: Modifying tree crown shape changes the spatial extent and timing of shade cast on road surfaces. The linked evidence reports a positive association between Shade Coverage and Road Surface Temperature in this study; this contrasts with the more common expectation that shade reduces RST and should be addressed explicitly. If the intent is to lower RST, the mechanism should either align with the reported positive direction or flag the discrepancy with the linked evidence.

signatures.mechanism: Modifying the crown geometry of street trees (e.g., selecting spreading over columnar forms, or selectively pruning to widen the canopy profile) increases the lateral extent of cast shadow on the road surface below. A broader, more spreading crown intercepts a larger portion of the sky hemisphere as seen from the road, thereby reducing the sky view factor (SVF) and increasing the green view factor (GVF). Since GVF is strongly negatively correlated with road surface temperature (r = −0.93), optimizing crown shape toward spreading geometries maximizes shade coverage during peak irradiance, lowering RST.

scope.description: A single-signature intervention (SIG_MOD_VEG_MG_SHP) that modifies the crown shape of street-canyon canopy trees to optimize shade cast onto road surfaces. Linked evidence SVCs_P_Zeng2023_2 demonstrates that increased Shade Coverage (IND_SHA) reduces Road Surface Temperature (RST) within the thermal comfort performance dimension (PRF_THR / PRS_THR_TEMP) with a positive relationship direction (DIR_POS, effect size 6.5), meaning greater canopy shade coverage yields greater RST reduction. By reshaping tree crowns from narrow/columnar to broader/spreading forms, the intervention enlarges the projected shadow footprint, raises GVF, and lowers SVF at the street-canyon floor, directly driving the shade-to-RST reduction pathway documented in the evidence.
scope_validation_notes: Scope structure is internally consistent with signature_count and phase_count.

translation_signature_schema.signature_order: Operation × Semantic Layer × Spatial Layer × Morphological Layer

performance_effect.expected_change_note: May decrease Road Surface Temperature (RST)

source.citation: Zeng, L., Lindberg, F., Zhang, X., Pan, H., & Lu, J. (2023). Road surface temperature evaluated with streetview-derived parameters in a hot and humid megacity. Urban Climate, 51, 101585.

operation.description: Add vegetation elements in the foreground to modify the shape and visual composition of urban streetscapes

pathway.mechanism_description: Adding foreground vegetation may enhance perceived safety by softening urban edges and signalling care of the environment. The linked evidence here, however, concerns Fence Ratio and the safety dimension of the Landsense Index, not vegetation; a more closely matched evidence link is needed for this mechanism.

signatures.mechanism: Introducing vegetation elements in the foreground modifies the shape outline of the streetscape by increasing the Fence Ratio (IND_FNC). Newly added foreground vegetation instances act as visual fencing components that redefine spatial edges and enclosure. This increased visual boundary definition contributes positively to perceived safety (LSI-Safety), as well-delineated foreground vegetation signals a maintained, surveilled environment and reduces visual exposure to potential threats.

scope.description: A single-signature intervention (SIG_ADD_VEG_FG_SHP) encoding the addition of foreground vegetation to reshape the visual composition of urban streetscapes. According to linked evidence SVCs_P_Zhang2021_1, this operation increases the Fence Ratio indicator (IND_FNC)—a measure of the proportion and count of foreground instances that visually fence or enclose the scene. The relationship direction is DIR_POS with an effect size of 0.247 on the Landsense Index for Safety, situated within the performance dimension PRF_SOC (Social Outcomes), subdimension PRS_SOC_SAFE. By adding shrub-scale vegetation that was previously absent, the intervention introduces new foreground instances that modify the shape boundary of the streetscape, thereby enhancing perceived enclosure and safety.
scope_validation_notes: Scope structure is internally consistent with signature_count and phase_count.

translation_signature_schema.signature_order: Operation × Semantic Layer × Spatial Layer × Morphological Layer

performance_effect.expected_change_note: Increase Landsense Index (LSI) - Safety

source.citation: Zhang, Y., Li, S., Dong, R., Deng, H., Fu, X., Wang, C., Yu, T., Jia, T., & Zhao, J. (2021). Quantifying physical and psychological perceptions of urban scenes using deep learning. Land Use Policy, 111, 105762.

operation.description: Reduce the size and scale of buildings in the middleground to decrease their visual dominance and perceived wealth impression in urban scenes

pathway.mechanism_description: Reducing building scale in the middleground may decrease the perceived prosperity of the urban scene through reduced visual material dominance. The linked evidence here, however, concerns Dirt Ratio and the wealthy dimension of LSI, not building size; a more closely matched evidence link is needed for this mechanism.

signatures.mechanism: Reducing the size and scale of middleground buildings diminishes their visual dominance in the scene. As building mass shrinks, the relative pixel proportion of exposed ground and unpaved surfaces (dirt) increases, raising the Dirt Ratio (IND_DIR_RAT). Given the strong negative correlation (−0.731) between Dirt Ratio and the Wealthy Landsense Index, this upward shift in dirt visibility drives a decrease in perceived wealth.

scope.description: A single-signature intervention SIG_MOD_BLT_MG_SIZ targeting built-structure massing in the middleground tier. By down-scaling building facades at the 10–50 m range, the operation reduces the visual weight of architecture and proportionally elevates the Dirt Ratio indicator (IND_DIR_RAT) linked in evidence SVCs_P_Zhang2021_2. The evidence demonstrates a strong negative relationship (effect size −0.731, DIR_NEG) between Dirt Ratio and the Wealthy Landsense Index within the social-outcomes performance dimension (PRF_SOC, subdimension PRS_SOC_DEMO). Therefore, the expected outcome is a reduction in the perceived wealth impression of the urban scene, aligning with the stated intervention goal.
scope_validation_notes: Scope structure is internally consistent with signature_count and phase_count.

performance_effect.expected_change_note: Decrease Landsense Index (LSI) - Wealthy

source.citation: Zhang, Y., Li, S., Dong, R., Deng, H., Fu, X., Wang, C., Yu, T., Jia, T., & Zhao, J. (2021). Quantifying physical and psychological perceptions of urban scenes using deep learning. Land Use Policy, 111, 105762.

operation.description: Increase the size and coverage of street vegetation within 200m-1km buffer zones around residential areas to enhance Green View Index (GVI) along streetscapes.

pathway.mechanism_description: The linked evidence reports a positive correlation between GVI and the number of psychiatric disorder hospitalization cases within 200m-1km buffer zones. This may reflect confounding with urban density, healthcare access, or population distribution rather than greenery causing more hospitalizations. The expected stress-reducing pathway of street greenery is not directly supported by this association.

signatures.mechanism: Adding street trees and expanding their canopy coverage within streetscapes increases the proportion of green pixels captured by street-level imagery, directly raising the Green View Index (GVI). Higher GVI reflects greater visible greenness from a pedestrian perspective, which evidence links to reduced psychiatric disorder hospitalizations.

scope.description: A single-signature intervention (SIG_ADD_VEG_MG_SIZ) that increases the amount and coverage of street vegetation—primarily canopy trees—along streetscapes within 200m–1km buffer zones around residential areas. Per linked evidence SVCs_P_Zhang2022_1, raising the Green View Index (IND_GVI) shows a positive reported association (DIR_POS, effect size 0.027) with the study's outcome measure—Number of psychiatric disorder hospitalization cases within buffer zones (200m–1000m)—mapped here to the performance dimension PRF_RST / PRS_MNT_DEPR. Because this reported outcome is a contextual population-health proxy rather than a direct restorative score, its desirability should be interpreted cautiously. The intervention operates through a single object type (OBJ_VEG) perceived within one depth zone, satisfying S1 scope.
scope_validation_notes: Scope structure is internally consistent with signature_count and phase_count.

translation_signature_schema.signature_order: Operation × Semantic Layer × Spatial Layer × Morphological Layer

performance_effect.expected_change_note: Reported association with psychiatric-disorder hospitalization cases requires cautious interpretation

source.citation: Zhang, A., Zhai, S., Liu, X., Song, G., & Feng, Y. (2022). Investigating the Association between Streetscapes and Mental Health in Zhanjiang, China: Using Baidu Street View Images and Deep Learning. International Journal of Environmental Research and Public Health, 19(24), 16634.

operation.description: Reduce the size or extent of vegetation elements in the foreground to decrease visual density and increase perceived openness in urban green spaces

pathway.mechanism_description: Decreasing vegetation size in the foreground reduces visual obstruction and spatial enclosure, thereby increasing the perceived spaciousness and ease of movement. This reduction in spatial definition (SPD) creates a more open visual field, enhancing the subjective perception of freedom and reducing feelings of confinement, which positively impacts aesthetic performance through improved spatial quality ratings

signatures.mechanism: Reducing the size or extent of foreground vegetation lowers its contribution to the Spatial Division metric (sum of area proportions of elements that fragment a coherent activity space). Smaller or less expansive vegetation masses in the immediate sensory zone act less as visual barriers, thereby decreasing spatial division and increasing the perceived spaciousness and freedom of movement within the green space.

scope.description: A single-signature intervention SIG_MOD_VEG_FG_SIZ that modifies the size/extent of foreground vegetation to reduce the Spatial Division indicator (IND_SPD). Linked evidence SVCs_P_Zhang2023_3 demonstrates a negative relationship (β = −0.528) between Spatial Division and the Aesthetic Preference dimension (PRF_AES), specifically the legibility subdimension (PRS_AES_LEGI), measured by subjective ratings of 'The place is spacious for free movement and uneasy to be disturbed.' By shrinking or pruning foreground vegetation, the area proportion of space-dividing elements decreases, which—given the negative coefficient—results in higher spaciousness and legibility ratings, improving aesthetic preference.
scope_validation_notes: Scope structure is internally consistent with signature_count and phase_count.

translation_signature_schema.signature_order: Operation × Semantic Layer × Spatial Layer × Morphological Layer

performance_effect.expected_change_note: Increase Subjective rating of 'The place is spacious for free movement and uneasy to be disturbed'

source.citation: Zhang, X., Lin, E. S., Tan, P. Y., Qi, J., & Waykool, R. (2023). Assessment of visual landscape quality of urban green spaces using image-based metrics derived from perceived sensory dimensions. Environmental Impact Assessment Review, 102, 107200.

operation.description: Modify building height and configuration in the background to increase sky view factor, creating more open visual space and reducing visual enclosure from built structures

pathway.mechanism_description: Higher sky view factor (SVF) increases the proportion of visible sky from observation points, creating a sense of openness and spatial freedom. This reduces the oppressive feeling from tall buildings and enhances perceived landscape quality by improving visual comfort and aesthetic preference through better sky exposure

signatures.mechanism: Reducing building height and reconfiguring building massing in the background decreases the area proportion of visible built surfaces (Building View Index) and correspondingly increases the Sky View Index (SVI), the area proportion of sky visible in a panoramic view. This lowers visual enclosure and enhances perceived openness.

scope.description: SIG_MOD_BLT_BG_SIZ encodes a single-signature intervention that modifies the height and massing of background built structures to increase the Sky View Factor (IND_SVF). According to linked evidence SVCs_P_Zhang2023_4, SVI—defined as the area proportion of sky in a view (Excerpt 8)—serves as the primary metric for the 'Open' perceived sensory dimension, representing unobstructed visual environments (Excerpt 2, 6). The study finds a positive relationship (DIR_POS, β = 0.404) between SVI and Landscape Preference on the Aesthetic Preference dimension (PRF_AES / PRS_AES_PREF). By reducing building height in the background, the proportion of sky visible from the observer's viewpoint increases, directly raising SVI and thereby improving aesthetic preference ratings. Excerpt 1 explicitly notes that sky visibility in residential areas is most constrained by high-rise buildings, confirming that modifying built-structure size is the operative lever for increasing SVF.
scope_validation_notes: Scope structure is internally consistent with signature_count and phase_count.

translation_signature_schema.signature_order: Operation × Semantic Layer × Spatial Layer × Morphological Layer

performance_effect.expected_change_note: Increase Landscape Preference

source.citation: Zhang, X., Lin, E. S., Tan, P. Y., Qi, J., & Waykool, R. (2023). Assessment of visual landscape quality of urban green spaces using image-based metrics derived from perceived sensory dimensions. Environmental Impact Assessment Review, 102, 107200.

operation.description: Add vegetation to increase visible greenery ratio (RVI) in the middleground view range, creating higher proportion of natural elements within the visual field.

pathway.mechanism_description: Higher Road View Index (visible vegetation/greenery) is positively correlated with standardized Skin Conductance Level, an electrodermal indicator of physiological arousal. Whether this reflects autonomic activation, attentional engagement, or other factors is not directly distinguished by the linked correlational evidence.

signatures.mechanism: Introducing new vegetation (trees) into the middleground visual field increases the proportion of natural greenery visible to pedestrians while simultaneously occluding hard urban surfaces such as road pavement. This shifts the pixel composition of the street-view image, reducing the Road View Index (IND_RVI). Because RVI has a positive relationship with standardized Skin Conductance Level (SCL), lowering road visibility through added canopy cover reduces physiological arousal and stress.

scope.description: SCP_S1 encodes a single signature SIG_ADD_VEG_MG_SIZ. The intervention adds vegetation in the middleground (10–50 m) to raise the visible greenery ratio and, as a direct corollary, reduce the Road View Index (IND_RVI). Linked evidence SVCs_P_Cai2023_3 demonstrates that IND_RVI relates positively (DIR_POS, β = 0.242) to standardized Skin Conductance Level under the performance dimension PRF_RST / PRS_MNT_PHYS. Therefore, decreasing road surface dominance in the visual field by adding canopy trees is expected to lower physiological stress. The intervention operates on a single object type (OBJ_VEG) within a single depth zone, satisfying the S1 unit-operation criterion.
scope_validation_notes: Scope structure is internally consistent with signature_count and phase_count.

translation_signature_schema.signature_order: Operation × Semantic Layer × Spatial Layer × Morphological Layer

performance_effect.expected_change_note: Decrease Standardized Skin Conductance Level (SCL)

source.citation: Cai, M., Xiang, L., & Ng, E. (2023). How does the visual environment influence pedestrian physiological stress? Evidence from high-density cities using ambulatory technology and spatial machine learning. Sustainable Cities and Society, 96, 104695.

operation.description: Increase the amount and coverage of vegetation in the middle-ground view to improve Green View Index (GVI) threshold performance at the street level and neighborhood scale.

pathway.mechanism_description: Higher GVI is negatively correlated with monthly Max-Min temperature differences in this study. Plausible contributing pathways include evapotranspiration cooling and shade where canopy is sufficient, though the linked evidence is correlational and does not directly attribute the temperature-range moderation to these biophysical mechanisms.

signatures.mechanism: Adding vegetation volume and canopy coverage in the middle-ground view directly increases the proportion of green pixels captured by street-view imagery, raising the Green View Index (GVI 2.0). The evidence (coefficient −0.049, p<0.001) shows that higher GVI is negatively associated with average ambient temperature, meaning the added vegetative mass contributes to evapotranspiration and shading that reduces the average monthly temperature difference.

scope.description: SIG_ADD_VEG_MG_SIZ encodes a single-signature intervention that increases the amount and spatial coverage of vegetation within the middle-ground tier (10–50 m). According to linked evidence SVCs_P_Chan2024_2, this manipulation targets the Green View Index (IND_GVI), which is measured as the ratio of green vegetation pixels to total pixels in Google Street View panoramas. The performance dimension is thermal comfort (PRF_THR / PRS_THR_TEMP), with the outcome measure being the average monthly temperature difference (Max–Min). The relationship direction is DIR_NEG: as GVI increases through added vegetation, the temperature metric decreases, indicating a moderated and more comfortable thermal environment at the neighborhood scale.
scope_validation_notes: Scope structure is internally consistent with signature_count and phase_count.

translation_signature_schema.signature_order: Operation × Semantic Layer × Spatial Layer × Morphological Layer

performance_effect.expected_change_note: Decrease Average monthly temperature difference (Max-Min)

source.citation: Chan, T.-C., Lee, P.-H., Lee, Y.-T., & Tang, J.-H. (2024). Exploring the spatial association between the distribution of temperature and urban morphology with green view index. PLOS ONE, 19(5), e0301921.

operation.description: Reduce textural diversity and complexity of vegetation composition in the middleground by limiting species variety and creating more homogeneous planting patterns

pathway.mechanism_description: Decreasing vegetation diversity reduces visual texture variation and compositional complexity, which leads to lower aesthetic quality and reduced landscape preference as uniform vegetation patterns diminish visual interest and perceptual richness

signatures.mechanism: Limiting species variety and enforcing more homogeneous planting patterns reduces the textural diversity and structural complexity of the vegetation composition. This directly lowers the Shannon-based Diversity Index (diversity of plant groups), which measures the area-proportional richness across tree, grass, and flora categories. The shift from varied, multi-layered plantings toward monotonous spatial traits decreases the perceived richness and sensory variation associated with the 'Diverse' perceived sensory dimension.

scope.description: A single-signature intervention SIG_MOD_VEG_MG_TEX that modifies the textural attribute of middleground vegetation to reduce compositional diversity. As documented in evidence SVCs_P_Zhang2023_5, the target indicator is the Diversity Index (IND_DIV), computed as a Shannon diversity index over plant-group area proportions. By homogenising planting patterns and limiting species variety, the intervention decreases IND_DIV. The performance dimension is Aesthetic Preference (PRF_AES, subdimension PRS_AES_PREF), measured through Landscape Preference ratings. The relationship direction is DIR_NEG with an effect size of −0.200, meaning the reduction in diversity is associated with a decline in landscape preference—viewers perceive the simplified, less texturally varied middleground vegetation as aesthetically less preferred.
scope_validation_notes: Scope structure is internally consistent with signature_count and phase_count.

translation_signature_schema.signature_order: Operation × Semantic Layer × Spatial Layer × Morphological Layer

performance_effect.expected_change_note: Increase Landscape Preference

source.citation: Zhang, X., Lin, E. S., Tan, P. Y., Qi, J., & Waykool, R. (2023). Assessment of visual landscape quality of urban green spaces using image-based metrics derived from perceived sensory dimensions. Environmental Impact Assessment Review, 102, 107200.

operation.description: Add water features of sufficient size in the middleground of urban green spaces to enhance visual presence and accessibility for users.

pathway.mechanism_description: Water features in the middleground create focal visual elements that capture attention and may support psychological restoration through fascination and stress reduction. Larger water bodies tend to offer greater visual impact, ambient sound benefits, and opportunities for contemplation. Middleground placement supports visual engagement while maintaining accessibility, consistent with the positive association between Water View Index and Perceived Restorative Potential.

signatures.mechanism: Introducing a water feature of sufficient size in the middleground (10–50 m) directly increases the proportion of water visible in the scene, thereby raising the Water View Index (WVI). A larger water surface area at social-field distance ensures that the element registers prominently in the viewer's field of vision without overwhelming the foreground composition, enhancing perceived restorative potential.

scope.description: A single-signature intervention (SIG_ADD_WAT_MG_SIZ) that introduces a sufficiently sized water body into the middleground of an urban green space. According to linked evidence SVCs_P_Zhang2024_1, increasing the Water View Index (IND_WAT) is positively associated (DIR_POS, effect size 20.007) with the performance dimension PRF_EMO (Emotion regulation and cognitive modulation), subdimension PRS_MNT_REST, measured as Perceived Restorative Potential. By adding a visually prominent water feature at middleground distance, the intervention raises IND_WAT, which in turn is expected to improve users' perceived restorative experience of the riverfront landscape.
scope_validation_notes: Scope structure is internally consistent with signature_count and phase_count.

translation_signature_schema.signature_order: Operation × Semantic Layer × Spatial Layer × Morphological Layer

performance_effect.expected_change_note: Increase Perceived Restorative Potential

source.citation: Zhang, S., Lu, J., Guo, R., & Yang, Y. (2024). Exploring the Relationship Between Visual Perception of the Urban Riverfront Core Landscape Area and the Vitality of Riverfront Road: A Case Study of Guangzhou. Land, 13(12), 2142.

operation.description: Reduce variation in green view index by standardizing vegetation texture and visual complexity across the middle ground of street scenes to create more uniform green visual patterns.

pathway.mechanism_description: High variation in green view index creates visual inconsistency and unpredictability in the streetscape, which may increase cognitive load and reduce the calming, restorative qualities associated with coherent natural environments. Lower GVI variation promotes visual harmony and perceptual fluency, enhancing psychological restoration through consistent nature exposure.

signatures.mechanism: Standardizing the texture and visual complexity of vegetation in the middle ground reduces the spatial variation in green view index (GVI). By selecting species with comparable foliage density, leaf size, and canopy grain, the street scene presents a more uniform green visual pattern. This lowers the coefficient of variation (or analogous dispersion metric) of GVI across sequential viewpoints, directly decreasing GVI Variation (IND_GVI_VAR).

scope.description: A single-signature intervention (SIG_MOD_VEG_MG_TEX) that modifies the texture attribute of middle-ground vegetation to homogenise green visual patterns along riverfront streets. According to linked evidence SVCs_P_Zhang2024_2, GVI Variation (IND_GVI_VAR) has a strong negative relationship (β = −6.281) with Perceived Restorative Potential under the Emotion regulation and cognitive modulation performance dimension (PRF_EMO / PRS_MNT_REST). By reducing texture heterogeneity—replacing visually disparate species mixes with consistently grained canopy trees and shrubs—the intervention lowers GVI Variation, which is expected to improve perceived restorative potential. The negative direction of the effect means that decreasing GVI variation yields a positive mental-health outcome.
scope_validation_notes: Scope structure is internally consistent with signature_count and phase_count.

translation_signature_schema.signature_order: Operation × Semantic Layer × Spatial Layer × Morphological Layer

performance_effect.expected_change_note: Increase Perceived Restorative Potential

source.citation: Zhang, S., Lu, J., Guo, R., & Yang, Y. (2024). Exploring the Relationship Between Visual Perception of the Urban Riverfront Core Landscape Area and the Vitality of Riverfront Road: A Case Study of Guangzhou. Land, 13(12), 2142.

operation.description: Modify the position and alignment of riverfront road to optimize water view accessibility and visual corridors toward water bodies.

pathway.mechanism_description: Repositioning the riverfront road enhances visual connections between users and water features, increasing the mean Water View Index by improving sight lines and reducing visual obstructions to the waterfront, thereby enhancing aesthetic experience through better water visibility.

signatures.mechanism: Repositioning and realigning the riverfront road changes the spatial relationship between pedestrian viewpoints and the river, opening visual corridors toward the water body. The current Mean Water View Index of only 0.05 reflects poor utilization of water surfaces (excerpt 12); modifying road alignment shifts the vantage geometry so that a greater proportion of the field of view captures water, thereby increasing WVI.

scope.description: SIG_MOD_GRD_MG_POS encodes the modification of the riverfront road's position and alignment to improve visual access to the river. Linked evidence SVCs_P_Zhang2024_5 reports a descriptive Mean Water View Index (IND_WAT) of just 0.05 along Pearl River Riverfront Road, indicating that water bodies occupy a negligible share of the pedestrian visual field despite the road's riverfront location. By adjusting road geometry—its horizontal alignment and setback relative to the water edge—the intervention aims to widen and multiply visual corridors toward the river, increasing WVI. This targets the performance dimension PRF_AES (Aesthetic Preference), subdimension PRS_AES_QUAL; the expected outcome direction is an increase in perceived water presence and associated aesthetic quality, though the source evidence records DIR_NA because the current value is purely descriptive rather than a before-after comparison.
scope_validation_notes: Scope structure is internally consistent with signature_count and phase_count.

translation_signature_schema.signature_order: Operation × Semantic Layer × Spatial Layer × Morphological Layer

performance_effect.expected_change_note: Descriptive evidence only; directional effect on Mean Water View Index on Riverfront Road is not established.

source.citation: Zhang, S., Lu, J., Guo, R., & Yang, Y. (2024). Exploring the Relationship Between Visual Perception of the Urban Riverfront Core Landscape Area and the Vitality of Riverfront Road: A Case Study of Guangzhou. Land, 13(12), 2142.

operation.description: Increase visible greenery along riverfront road to improve comprehensive visual perception score of the core landscape area

pathway.mechanism_description: GVI is one of four key visual indices (GVI, SVI, BVI, WVI) that compose the comprehensive Visual Perception Score (VPS) for riverfront landscape. Increasing green vegetation along the Pearl River riverfront directly raises the GVI component, improving the VPS. Higher VPS contributes to perceived landscape quality, which the study relates to riverfront road vitality through spatial analysis of Baidu hotspot activity data.

signatures.mechanism: Adding street trees and green vegetation along pedestrian sidewalks directly increases the proportion of green pixels captured in street-level imagery, raising the Green View Index (GVI). Lush canopy trees at the foreground tier fill the pedestrian's visual cone with greenery, enhancing three-dimensional visual exposure to vegetation and producing a greener streetscape experience.

scope.description: SIG_ADD_VEG_FG_SIZ encodes a single-signature intervention (SCP_S1) that increases street-level green vegetation coverage by adding canopy trees along pedestrian routes. Linked evidence SVCs_P_Zhang2024_7 is descriptive (DIR_NA): it reports that GVI contributes to a comprehensive visual perception score (VPS) for Riverfront Road landscape in Guangzhou, falling within the performance dimension PRF_AES (Aesthetic Preference, subdimension PRS_AES_QUAL). Because the evidence is descriptive, no directional effect on VPS is established. The intervention raises GVI by introducing new foreground vegetation where it is sparse or absent—addressing conditions noted in the source where areas have 'almost no greenery except for linearly planted banyan trees'—thereby increasing the share of green pixels in the visual field and potentially improving visual quality of the riverfront streetscape.
scope_validation_notes: Scope structure is internally consistent with signature_count and phase_count.

translation_signature_schema.signature_order: Operation × Semantic Layer × Spatial Layer × Morphological Layer

performance_effect.expected_change_note: Descriptive evidence only; directional effect on Comprehensive visual perception score (VPS) for Riverfront Road landscape is not established.

source.citation: Zhang, S., Lu, J., Guo, R., & Yang, Y. (2024). Exploring the Relationship Between Visual Perception of the Urban Riverfront Core Landscape Area and the Vitality of Riverfront Road: A Case Study of Guangzhou. Land, 13(12), 2142.

operation.description: Enhance water surface visibility along riverfront road by managing sightlines and removing visual obstructions to water bodies

pathway.mechanism_description: Increasing the proportion of visible water surface raises the Water View Index (WVI), one of four components of the comprehensive Visual Perception Score. For riverfront roads, WVI distinguishes waterfront from non-waterfront streetscapes. Its relationship with road vitality is spatially varying and non-linear, with some sections showing significant spatial covariation between visual perception and activity hotspots.

signatures.mechanism: Modifying the surface quality, material finish, and paving pattern of pedestrian sidewalks improves the visually assessed walkability index by signalling safe, continuous, and well-maintained pedestrian infrastructure. Enhanced pavement texture communicates comfort and accessibility, which semantic-segmentation models capture as higher walkability scores in street-view imagery.

scope.description: SIG_MOD_GRD_MG_TEX encodes a single-signature intervention that modifies the texture and material quality of sidewalk ground surfaces (OBJ_GRD, subtype sidewalk) at the street-scale middleground tier (TER_MG, 10–50 m) to raise the Water View Index (IND_WAT). Linked evidence SVCs_P_Zhang2024_8 reports a relationship (DIR_NA) between IND_WAT and the performance dimension PRF_AES (Aesthetic Preference, subdimension PRS_AES_QUAL), measuring overall visual quality of riverfront road landscape in Guangzhou. No stable directional estimate was established. The intervention acts by upgrading pavement texture, delineation, and surface continuity—features associated with separated walkways, optimised walkway scales, and humanised pedestrian spaces—thereby improving visual cues of infrastructure quality and contributing to the overall visual perception score.
scope_validation_notes: Scope structure is internally consistent with signature_count and phase_count.

operation.description: Modify vegetation texture and pattern complexity in the middleground to influence visual perception and landscape compositional richness

pathway.mechanism_description: The variable described in the linked evidence (Compression Ratio) is positively associated with the SVM model's prediction of visual complexity. Whether this aligns with crown-projection density depends on how the variable is defined in the underlying study; modifications to vegetation texture and crown overlap could plausibly increase this metric, contributing to higher predicted visual complexity.

signatures.mechanism: Adjusting the texture and pattern complexity of middleground vegetation alters the spatial frequency distribution captured by image compression algorithms, thereby increasing the Compression Ratio (IND_CPR). Greater foliage heterogeneity—varied leaf sizes, branching patterns, and layered canopy edges—reduces compressibility, signalling higher visual complexity and compositional richness to observers.

scope.description: SIG_MOD_VEG_MG_TEX encodes a single operation: modifying the textural and pattern attributes of middleground vegetation. Linked evidence SVCs_P_Zhao2024_6 demonstrates that the Compression Ratio indicator (IND_CPR) positively contributes (DIR_POS, effect size 29.02%) to a Support Vector Machine model predicting visual complexity, situated within the performance dimension PRF_AES (Aesthetic Preference) and its subdimension PRS_AES_RICH (richness). By increasing vegetation textural heterogeneity—through species mixing, layered planting, or varied crown forms—the intervention raises the compression ratio of the scene's image representation, which the evidence associates with enhanced perceived landscape compositional richness. The single-signature scope reflects that only one spatial variable (texture), one action (modification), one object class (vegetation), and one depth tier (middleground) are manipulated.

source.citation: Zhao, X., Lu, Y., & Lin, G. (2024). An integrated deep learning approach for assessing the visual qualities of built environments utilizing street view images. Engineering Applications of Artificial Intelligence, 130, 107805.

operation.description: Modify the shape configuration and spatial arrangement of vegetation elements in the middleground to enhance green view index and canopy coverage metrics that contribute to visual complexity prediction.

pathway.mechanism_description: Modifying vegetation shape and spatial arrangement in the middleground may alter the textural contrast (e.g., GLCM grayscale contrast) of street imagery, which is positively associated with visual complexity prediction in the linked evidence. The original mechanism's framing in terms of green rate and canopy coverage should be reformulated to refer to grayscale-contrast-based texture features to align with the linked evidence.

signatures.mechanism: Modifying the shape configuration of canopy trees (e.g., crown form, outline complexity) in the middleground increases the heterogeneity of vegetation edges and foliage mass visible in the scene, raising grayscale contrast (GLCM) by creating more varied light–dark adjacencies within the canopy zone and thereby boosting visual complexity.

Adjusting the spatial arrangement of vegetation elements in the middleground redistributes the proportion of green pixels across the street-view image, enhancing the Green View Index (GVI) and canopy coverage. This redistribution increases textural variation captured by GLCM grayscale contrast, supporting higher visual complexity prediction.

scope.description: This S2 superposition operation coordinates two simultaneous modifications on the same object and tier: SIG_MOD_VEG_MG_SHP (primary) reshapes canopy tree crowns while SIG_MOD_VEG_MG_POS (secondary) rearranges their spatial distribution, both within the middleground (10–50 m). Together they target the indicator Grayscale Contrast (GLCM) (IND_GRC_CON) documented in evidence SVCs_P_Zhao2024_7, which has a positive relationship (DIR_POS, β = 0.2669) with Visual Complexity Prediction under the performance dimension PRF_AES / PRS_AES_RICH. By jointly increasing crown-form diversity and optimising canopy spacing, the intervention elevates Green View Index and canopy coverage metrics, which in turn amplify pixel-level tonal heterogeneity (GLCM contrast), driving improved aesthetic richness perception.

operation.description: Modify the visual texture and composition of vegetation elements in the foreground to enhance walkability perception through improved visual quality and spatial definition.

pathway.mechanism_description: Modifying the visual texture of foreground vegetation may influence how pedestrians experience walkability through changes in visual interest, wayfinding cues, and perceived comfort. The linked evidence is descriptive of the Walkability Index in relation to walkability visual impact; direction or magnitude of effects is not directly characterized.

signatures.mechanism: Adjusting the visual texture and compositional pattern of foreground vegetation refines the perceived grain, layering, and spatial definition of the streetscape edge. Per the linked evidence (SVCs_P_Zhao2024_8), SHAP analysis reveals that the proportion and visual character of vegetation elements contribute to subjective perception scores including walkability. By modifying the textural richness and arrangement coherence of planting in the immediate sensory zone, the intervention alters the semantic segmentation profile of street-view imagery in ways that elevate the Walkability Index (Ma et al.), which integrates objective visual element ratios with subjective quality predictions.

scope.description: SCP_S1 comprises a single signature SIG_MOD_VEG_FG_TEX. According to evidence SVCs_P_Zhao2024_8, the Walkability Index (IND_WLK_RAT) is computed from the proportional composition of visual elements—including vegetation—in street-view images and validated against machine-learning perception predictions (Table 2). The intervention modifies the textural quality of foreground vegetation (VAR_TEX × ACT_MOD), which the deep-learning pipeline (SegFormer-B5 semantic vectors + ConvNeXt-B perception classifier) identifies as a contributing factor to subjective walkability scores. This operates within the performance dimension PRF_AES, subdimension PRS_AES_QUAL (aesthetic quality). Because the evidence reports only descriptive model-validation statistics (DIR_NA) rather than a directional effect size, the expected outcome is an improvement in perceived visual quality and spatial definition that the walkability metric captures, though the magnitude remains descriptive rather than quantified directionally.

operation.description: Increase the quantity and coverage of visible vegetation elements in the middle-ground viewing zone to enhance green view index (GVI) levels within urban street corridors and public spaces.

pathway.mechanism_description: Higher GVI values, achieved through increased vegetative coverage in the viewer's primary sight line, directly correlate with enhanced visual preference and perceived aesthetic beauty. The presence of green elements in the middle-ground creates a balanced visual composition that reduces harsh urban elements while providing natural focal points, leading to improved psychological response and aesthetic satisfaction among observers.

signatures.mechanism: Introducing additional vegetation elements (primarily canopy trees) in the middle-ground viewing zone increases the pixel proportion of greenery captured in street-view imagery, directly raising the Green View Index (GVI). As GVI quantifies the visibility of vegetation as perceived by the human eye, adding canopy mass and foliar coverage at 10–50 m enlarges the green area within the observer's visual field without overwhelming the immediate foreground, thereby lifting GVI past the ~30 % preference threshold noted in the literature (Aoki 1991, cited in the source) and toward the 45–48 % critical point associated with enhanced aesthetic preference.

scope.description: A single-signature intervention (SIG_ADD_VEG_MG_SIZ) that adds vegetation quantity and coverage in the middle-ground tier of urban street corridors. Linked evidence SVCs_P_Zhao2024_9 demonstrates that higher Green View Index (IND_GVI) is positively associated (DIR_POS) with the performance dimension PRF_AES (Aesthetic Preference, subdimension PRS_AES_PREF — Visual Preference / Beauty). By increasing the amount of visible vegetation at 10–50 m, the intervention raises GVI values in street-view imagery, which in turn improves residents' aesthetic preference ratings for the streetscape. The effect size is not numerically reported (not numerically reported), but the directional relationship is consistently positive across the deep-learning perception models described in the source paper.
scope_validation_notes: Scope structure is internally consistent with signature_count and phase_count.

source.citation: Zhao, X., Lu, Y., & Lin, G. (2024). An integrated deep learning approach for assessing the visual qualities of built environments utilizing street view images. Engineering Applications of Artificial Intelligence, 130, 107805.

operation.description: Modify vegetation texture and density to alter brightness and reflectance characteristics in the foreground visual field, adjusting the perceived luminance and contrast of vegetated surfaces.

pathway.mechanism_description: Higher perceived brightness is negatively associated with perceived thermal coolness in this study, meaning brighter scenes are rated as hotter. Modifying vegetation texture to reduce brightness (denser canopy, more shadow) could shift thermal-sensation ratings toward cooler perceptions, but this two-step inference goes beyond the direct linked relationship.

signatures.mechanism: Adjusting the foliage texture and density of foreground vegetation alters the surface reflectance properties and luminance contrast of the vegetated scene. Denser, finer-textured canopies absorb and scatter more light, reducing perceived brightness (IND_BRT). Because the relationship is DIR_NEG (effect size −0.51), lowering the Perceived Brightness Score shifts thermal sensation toward cooler evaluations, enhancing psychological thermal comfort.

scope.description: A single-signature intervention (SIG_MOD_VEG_FG_TEX) that modifies the texture and density attributes of foreground vegetation to change the brightness and reflectance characteristics perceived by greenway users. Linked evidence SVCs_P_Zheng2024_1 reports that the Perceived Brightness Score (IND_BRT) has a negative relationship (−0.51) with thermal sensation within the performance dimension PRF_THR / PRS_THR_VOTE (Thermal Sensation, 1 = Much hotter → 5 = Much cooler). By increasing foliage density and modulating leaf texture to reduce surface luminance and contrast, the intervention lowers perceived brightness, which in turn shifts thermal sensation toward cooler ratings, thereby improving psychological thermal comfort in hot-humid summer conditions along Fuzhou greenways.
scope_validation_notes: Scope structure is internally consistent with signature_count and phase_count.

performance_effect.expected_change_note: Directional change in Thermal Sensation (Visual evaluation: 1=Much hotter, 5=Much cooler) is context-dependent because the indicator adjustment is non-directional.

source.citation: Zheng, Y., Lan, S., Zhao, J., Liu, Y., He, S., & Liu, C. (2024). Modeling the Effect of Greenways' Multilevel Visual Characteristics on Thermal Perception in Summer Based on Bayesian Network and Computer Vision. Land, 13(11), 1796.

operation.description: Reduce the size and coverage of vegetation in the foreground to decrease Green View Index (GVI) percentage in the visual field

pathway.mechanism_description: Lower GVI reduces the amount of vegetation blocking sky and built surfaces, allowing more light penetration and increasing perceived brightness. Less foliage coverage decreases shadow casting and light absorption, resulting in higher luminance levels perceived by viewers

signatures.mechanism: Reducing the size and areal coverage of foreground vegetation lowers the proportion of green pixels captured by semantic segmentation, thereby decreasing the Green View Index (GVI). According to evidence SVCs_P_Zheng2024_3, lower GVI is associated with higher perceived brightness (Perceived Light), because less vegetation canopy in the immediate visual field allows more sky exposure and light penetration. The Bayesian network model shows GVI negatively influences perceived light (coefficient −0.01), so a downward modulation of vegetation size increases brightness perception.

scope.description: SCP_S1 comprises a single signature SIG_MOD_VEG_FG_SIZ. The intervention modifies (ACT_MOD) the size/coverage (VAR_SIZ) of foreground vegetation (OBJ_VEG, TER_FG) by reducing canopy and shrub mass. Linked evidence SVCs_P_Zheng2024_3 reports that the Green View Index (IND_GVI) has a negative relationship (DIR_NEG, effect size −0.01) with perceived light (brightness) under the performance dimension PRF_AES / PRS_AES_CMFT. By decreasing GVI through vegetation size reduction, perceived brightness in the visual scene is expected to increase. This aligns with the paper's finding that lower green coverage increases sky exposure and light intensity reaching the observer, thus raising the brightness component of aesthetic comfort. The operation is singular—one variable, one action, one object class, one tier—meeting S1 criteria.
scope_validation_notes: Scope structure is internally consistent with signature_count and phase_count.

operation.description: Increase vegetation texture complexity by adding diverse plant species with varied leaf patterns, growth forms, and structural characteristics in the middleground viewing zone to enhance visual plant richness.

pathway.mechanism_description: Higher Green View Index is positively associated with Perceived Plant Richness, suggesting that more visible greenery supports observers' impressions of plant diversity. Texture-complexity additions may contribute to this by raising GVI when more plant material occupies the visual field, though texture itself is not the direct variable in this linked relationship.

signatures.mechanism: Introducing diverse plant species with varied leaf patterns, growth forms, and structural characteristics increases the textural heterogeneity of the vegetation layer. This raises the Green View Index (GVI) as measured through semantic segmentation of greenway imagery, and critically elevates the perceived plant richness (PR) dimension—evaluated via species count, hierarchical structure, colour composition, and spatial form—which mediates aesthetic preference.

scope.description: A single-signature operation SIG_ADD_VEG_MG_TEX encodes the addition of texturally diverse vegetation in the middleground zone. As documented in linked evidence SVCs_P_Zheng2024_4, increasing texture complexity of the vegetation layer positively shifts the Green View Index (IND_GVI), which in turn enhances the Perceived Plant Richness outcome measure within the aesthetic preference dimension (PRF_AES / PRS_AES_RICH). The reported effect size of 0.46 (DIR_POS) indicates a moderate positive relationship: greater textural variety in the planting palette leads to higher perceived plant richness, consistent with the paper's finding that richer visual variety amplifies spatial richness and aesthetic enjoyment (Excerpts 1, 2, 6, 9).

operation.description: Modify the shape and configuration of background buildings to increase sky view factor (SVF) in urban spaces by adjusting building heights, setbacks, and spatial arrangements to open up more visible sky area from ground level.

pathway.mechanism_description: Higher SVF is associated with greater visual access to sky and daylight and lower perceived enclosure, which mediate a positive association with WHO-5 Well-being scores; additional microclimate pathways are plausible but not directly tested.

signatures.mechanism: Adjusting building heights and setbacks reshapes the silhouette profile of background built mass, reducing the solid angle subtended by buildings on the sky hemisphere and thereby increasing SVF as measured from ground level.

Rearranging the spatial distribution and spacing of background buildings opens wider gaps between structures, allowing additional sky exposure and further increasing SVF beyond what shape modification alone achieves.

scope.description: This S2 intervention coordinates two simultaneous modifications to background built form via a superposition pattern. SIG_MOD_BLT_BG_SHP (primary) modifies building heights and setbacks—i.e., the massing outline—while SIG_MOD_BLT_BG_POS (secondary) modifies building spacing and spatial arrangement. Together they increase the Sky View Factor (IND_SVF) by reducing the proportion of the overhead hemisphere occluded by structures, opening more visible sky from ground level. Linked evidence SVCs_P_Zheng2024_6 demonstrates that higher SVF is associated with a positive effect (DIR_POS, β = 3.164) on the WHO-5 Well-being Index, operating within the Stress relief and psychological restoration performance dimension (PRF_RST, subdimension PRS_MNT_DEPR). The source paper confirms that a higher sky view index reflects greater spatial openness, stronger perceived brightness, and warmer visual hues (Excerpts 3, 9), all of which contribute to improved subjective well-being in hot-humid greenway environments.

operation.description: Reduce the size and coverage of green vegetation in the middleground view to decrease Green View Index (GVI) levels in urban streetscapes and public spaces

pathway.mechanism_description: Lower mid-ground GVI is linearly associated with lower WHO-5 well-being scores; reduced visual contact with vegetation may diminish stress-recovery and perceived environmental quality, though these mediating mechanisms are not directly tested.

signatures.mechanism: Reducing the size and areal coverage of middleground vegetation lowers the proportion of green pixels captured in street-level imagery, thereby decreasing the Green View Index (GVI). The evidence (SVCs_P_Zheng2024_7) shows that GVI reflects the degree of greening and that more vegetation provides stronger shading and richer perceived plant diversity. Diminishing this coverage removes visual-psychological restorative cues and degrades perceived plant richness, leading to poorer mental-health outcomes.

scope.description: A single-signature intervention SIG_MOD_VEG_MG_SIZ that modifies (reduces) the size and coverage of middleground vegetation (OBJ_VEG, primarily canopy trees) to lower the Green View Index (IND_GVI). Linked evidence SVCs_P_Zheng2024_7 demonstrates a negative relationship (DIR_NEG, β = −0.454) between this GVI reduction and the WHO-5 Well-being Index score under the Stress relief and psychological restoration performance dimension (PRF_RST, subdimension PRS_MNT_DEPR). In other words, decreasing middleground green coverage is associated with worsened depressive symptomatology and reduced psychological well-being, consistent with the paper's finding that GVI positively contributes to perceived plant richness, aesthetic value, and thermal-visual comfort in greenway environments.
scope_validation_notes: Scope structure is internally consistent with signature_count and phase_count.

operation.description: Increase green view index (GVI) by adding vegetation elements in the middleground visual field to enhance textural density and coverage of greenery within urban streetscapes

pathway.mechanism_description: Higher GVI in the mid-ground is negatively correlated with air temperature, plausibly through evapotranspiration cooling and canopy shading; the causal magnitude of these biophysical pathways is not directly tested in this correlational analysis.

signatures.mechanism: Introducing new vegetation elements in the middleground (10–50 m) increases the proportion of green pixels captured in street-level imagery, thereby raising the Green View Index (GVI). Greater canopy presence augments evapotranspiration and shading surfaces, which collectively lower sensible heat flux and reduce ambient air temperature.

scope.description: A single-signature intervention SIG_ADD_VEG_MG_SIZ that adds vegetation volume in the middleground depth zone of urban streetscapes to raise the Green View Index (GVI). Linked evidence SVCs_P_Zhou2021_1 reports that higher GVI is associated with lower air temperature (Ta) under the performance dimension PRF_THR / PRS_THR_TEMP with a negative relationship (DIR_NEG) and an effect size of 0.20. The intervention therefore targets an increase in GVI to achieve a cooling outcome, consistent with the evidence that greater visual greenery coverage corresponds to reduced thermal load in the street canyon.

operation.description: Increase the quantity and coverage of vegetation elements in the middle-ground view to enhance the Green View Index (GVI) within the urban streetscape.

pathway.mechanism_description: Higher GVI is positively correlated with absolute humidity, consistent with plant transpiration adding moisture to the local boundary layer; the causal contribution of transpiration to the observed humidity differences is not directly quantified.

signatures.mechanism: Adding vegetation elements (quantity and coverage) in the middle-ground streetscape directly increases the proportion of green pixels captured in street-level imagery, thereby raising the Green View Index (GVI). As GVI encapsulates 3D greening effects (Excerpt 25), greater vegetative mass in the 10–50 m viewing zone enlarges the visible green fraction, which in turn is positively correlated with absolute humidity—an indicator of evapotranspiration-driven moisture release that contributes to thermal comfort regulation.

scope.description: SIG_ADD_VEG_MG_SIZ represents a single-signature intervention (SCP_S1) that increases the quantity and spatial coverage of vegetation in the middle-ground urban streetscape. According to linked evidence SVCs_P_Zhou2021_2, this operation targets the Green View Index (IND_GVI) measured through semantic segmentation of street-level imagery. The performance dimension is thermal comfort (PRF_THR / PRS_THR_TEMP), where a positive relationship (DIR_POS) was observed between GVI and absolute humidity (H), indicating that higher visible greenery enhances evapotranspiration and moisture levels in the local microclimate. The intervention therefore works by populating the middle-ground view with additional vegetation, directly lifting GVI values and, per the evidence, improving thermal environmental conditions.

operation.description: Modify walkway network configuration to optimize pedestrian accessibility and connectivity based on walkability index parameters

pathway.mechanism_description: Walkway network configurations characterized by higher walkability indices co-occur with running trajectories in this descriptive analysis; the evidence is non-directional and does not establish that connectivity changes drive trajectory density.

signatures.mechanism: Adjusting the spatial arrangement and connectivity of the walkway network reconfigures pedestrian route options and path continuity. This positional reorganisation of ground-surface circulation directly alters the visually assessed Walkability Index by improving legibility, directness, and perceived accessibility of the streetscape, which in turn shifts running trajectory density patterns across the network.

scope.description: SCP_S1 encodes a single operation, SIG_MOD_GRD_MG_POS, that modifies the positional configuration of walkway ground surfaces (OBJ_GRD, sidewalk) within the mid-ground tier. Linked evidence SVCs_P_Zhu2024_3 measures the outcome through the Walkability Index (Visual) (IND_WLK_RAT) under the performance dimension PRF_USE (Spatial use and physical activity), subdimension PRS_WAL_USE, with running trajectory density as the outcome measure. By reconfiguring walkway network layout—optimising connectivity and accessibility—the intervention is expected to redistribute and potentially increase trajectory density along improved routes, though the relationship direction is reported as DIR_NA (descriptive comparative analysis across walkability index levels, Table 2). The single-object, single-tier manipulation satisfies S1 scope.

operation.description: Increase the amount and coverage of vegetation in the middleground view to enhance Green View Index (GVI) levels in street environments.

pathway.mechanism_description: Higher mid-ground GVI is positively associated with visual comfort scores, plausibly via increased exposure to natural elements and reduced monotony; these psychological mediators are hypothesized but not directly tested.

signatures.mechanism: Adding vegetation (primarily canopy trees) in the middleground increases the pixel proportion of green vegetation visible in street-view imagery, directly raising the Green View Index (GVI). Larger canopy sizes and denser roadside planting fill more of the pedestrian's visual field at the 10–50 m range, which the linked evidence associates with improved visual comfort.

scope.description: A single-signature intervention (SIG_ADD_VEG_MG_SIZ) that increases the amount and spatial coverage of middleground vegetation in street environments. As demonstrated by linked evidence SVCs_P_Sun2023_1, raising the Green View Index (IND_GVI)—the pixel proportion of green vegetation in street-view images—produces a positive effect (DIR_POS, β = 0.769) on the Aesthetic Preference performance dimension (PRF_AES), specifically the Visual Comfort subdimension (PRS_AES_CMFT) measured via Semantic Differential (1–5 scale). The operation targets streets with poor green coverage (e.g., wide vehicular lanes, high-rise contexts) where GVI is currently low, adding canopy trees whose size and spread directly enlarge the green pixel ratio perceived by pedestrians.

source.citation: Sun, D., Ji, X., Gao, W., Zhou, F., Yu, Y., Meng, Y., Yang, M., Lin, J., & Lyu, M. (2023). The Relation between Green Visual Index and Visual Comfort in Qingdao Coastal Streets. Buildings, 13(2), 457.

operation.description: Modify the proportion and extent of visible vegetation in the foreground to achieve optimal Green View Index levels that balance greenness with visual openness, avoiding excessive green coverage that may is associated with visual oppression.

pathway.mechanism_description: Green View Index exhibits a non-linear relationship with visual comfort in aesthetic performance. Moderate GVI levels enhance visual comfort by providing natural elements and psychological restoration. However, excessive vegetation coverage can reduce visual openness and create feelings of enclosure or oppression, thereby diminishing visual comfort. The optimal GVI range balances biophilic benefits with spatial openness.

signatures.mechanism: Adjusting the proportion and spatial extent of foreground vegetation coverage modifies the pixel ratio of visible greenery captured in the pedestrian field of view, thereby shifting the Green View Index (GVI) toward an optimal, non-linear threshold that maximises visual comfort without inducing visual oppression from excessive green enclosure.

scope.description: SCP_S1 comprises a single signature SIG_MOD_VEG_FG_SIZ. Per linked evidence SVCs_P_Sun2023_2, the Green View Index (GVI) — measuring the pixel proportion of visible vegetation in street-view imagery — exhibits a non-linear relationship (DIR_NLN, effect size 1.184) with Visual Comfort (VICO) under the performance dimension PRF_AES / PRS_AES_CMFT. The intervention calibrates the amount of foreground vegetation so GVI reaches an optimal band: increasing it where greenery is deficient to improve perceived comfort, while preventing over-greening that would suppress visual openness and degrade comfort. Because the relationship is curvilinear, the operation is a modulation (ACT_MOD) of vegetation extent (VAR_SIZ) rather than a simple addition or removal, targeting the peak of the inverted-U curve identified in the evidence.

operation.description: Increase the size and quantity of vegetation elements in the foreground view plane to enhance green visibility from street-level perspectives

pathway.mechanism_description: Adding more vegetation in foreground areas directly increases the proportion of green pixels captured in street-level imagery, thereby improving the Green View Index (GVI) which measures visible greenery from human eye-level. The spatial distribution analysis shows that foreground vegetation has the strongest impact on GVI scores due to proximity to observation points

signatures.mechanism: Introducing additional vegetation elements and increasing their volumetric presence in the immediate foreground view plane enlarges the proportion of green pixels captured in street-level panoramic imagery, thereby raising the Green View Index (GVI). Greater canopy mass and foliage quantity within the 0–10 m zone maximises the solid angle of green that falls on the observer's visual field.

scope.description: A single-signature intervention (SIG_ADD_VEG_FG_SIZ) that adds vegetative volume and quantity in the foreground tier to boost street-level green visibility. Linked evidence SVCs_P_Chen2020_4 uses the Green View Index (IND_GVI) as its indicator, situated within the aesthetic-preference performance dimension (PRF_AES, subdimension PRS_AES_QUAL). The paper reports mean GVI values of only 11.3 ± 7.5 % across the Pearl River Delta urban agglomeration, well below benchmarks in developed-country cities, indicating substantial room for improvement. By increasing the size and number of vegetation elements closest to the observer (TER_FG), the intervention directly enlarges the green-pixel ratio in panoramic street views, pushing GVI upward. Although the evidence records no explicit effect-size value or directional tag (DIR_NA), the analytical framework consistently treats higher GVI as a positive indicator of people-oriented greening quality and aesthetic ecosystem service provision, so the expected outcome direction is an increase in GVI and, consequently, perceived aesthetic quality at street level.
scope_validation_notes: Scope structure is internally consistent with signature_count and phase_count.

operation.description: Increase the size and coverage of visible vegetation elements in the foreground of street-level views through strategic addition of trees, shrubs, and other green elements along roadways and pedestrian pathways.

pathway.mechanism_description: Expanding the proportion of vegetation visible from street level directly increases Green View Index (GVI) values by maximizing green pixels captured in horizontal field-of-view imagery. Foreground vegetation has the strongest visual impact on street view exposure measures and demonstrates positive correlation with both satellite-derived NDVI and ground-level green space metrics, validating GVI as an effective complement to traditional overhead assessment methods.

signatures.mechanism: Adding trees, shrubs, and other green elements along roadways and pedestrian pathways directly increases the proportion of green pixels captured in street-level panoramic imagery, thereby raising the Green View Index (GVI). The foreground placement maximises the solid angle of vegetation in the visual field, which is the primary determinant of GVI values.

scope.description: SIG_ADD_VEG_FG_SIZ is a single-signature intervention that increases the amount and spatial coverage of vegetation in the immediate foreground of street-level views. Linked evidence SVCs_P_Larkin2019_1 demonstrates that the Green View Index (IND_GVI)—a pixel-based measure of visible greenness from Google Street View panoramas—correlates positively (r = 0.41, DIR_POS) with traditional green space metrics (NDVI, EnviroAtlas) while capturing unique street-scale exposure information. By strategically adding vegetation elements in the foreground tier, the intervention directly elevates GVI, which in turn is associated with improved general physical health outcomes (PRF_USE / PRS_PHY_GNRL). The paper notes that high-GVI locations exhibit extensive vertical vegetative growth in the foreground of images, confirming that foreground vegetation addition is the primary lever for increasing this indicator.
scope_validation_notes: Scope structure is internally consistent with signature_count and phase_count.

operation.description: Increase the amount and coverage of visible vegetation in the foreground and immediate viewing field to enhance green view index (GVI) at eye level, creating more vegetated surfaces within the primary visual cone of streetscape observers.

pathway.mechanism_description: Higher foreground GVI is descriptively associated with higher Relaxation Perception Scores; greater eye-level vegetation may elicit restorative psychological responses, though no directional or causal claim is supported by the evidence.

signatures.mechanism: Adding vegetation elements (street trees, shrubs, ground cover) within the immediate viewing field increases the proportion of green pixels captured in eye-level street-view imagery, directly raising the Green View Index (GVI). A higher GVI saturates the primary visual cone with biophilic stimuli, which the linked evidence associates with enhanced relaxation perception.

scope.description: A single-signature intervention SIG_ADD_VEG_FG_SIZ that introduces additional vegetation volume and coverage in the foreground tier to raise the Green View Index (IND_GVI). Linked evidence SVCs_P_Zhang2024_11 reports mean Relaxation Perception Scores across different GVI levels (Figure 10B), indicating that higher proportions of visible greenery within the streetscape observer's field of view support the Emotion regulation and cognitive modulation performance dimension (PRF_EMO, subdimension PRS_MNT_REST). Although the relationship direction is recorded as DIR_NA (descriptive comparison of means rather than a tested directional coefficient), the pattern of scores and supporting excerpts (e.g., 'A high GVI will increase human visual comfort, especially in urban environments with a strong sense of isolation') consistently point toward a positive association between increased foreground vegetation amount and improved relaxation outcomes. The intervention therefore targets a single object type (OBJ_VEG) within a single depth zone (TER_FG), satisfying the S1 unit scope.

operation.description: Increase the green view index (GVI) by adding visible vegetation elements in the foreground layer of urban streets to enhance visual green coverage within pedestrian sightlines.

pathway.mechanism_description: Higher foreground GVI co-occurs with higher Safety and Familiarity Perception Scores in this descriptive analysis; visible greenery may signal maintained, legible environments, but no directional or causal pathway is empirically established.

signatures.mechanism: Introducing visible vegetation (predominantly street trees, supplemented by shrubs and ground cover) into the foreground layer increases the pixel proportion of green plants within the pedestrian field of view, directly raising the Green View Index (GVI). A higher GVI enriches the three-dimensional visual green coverage perceived at eye level, which the linked evidence associates with enhanced feelings of safety and familiarity.

scope.description: A single-signature intervention (SIG_ADD_VEG_FG_SIZ) that adds vegetation mass in the foreground tier to raise the Green View Index (IND_GVI). Linked evidence SVCs_P_Zhang2024_12 reports that GVI is associated with Safety and Familiarity Perception Scores (Safety Mean±SD: 3.92±0.78; Familiarity Mean±SD: 3.95±0.74) under the performance dimension PRF_SOC / PRS_SOC_SAFE. The relationship direction is DIR_NA (descriptive), yet the broader evidence excerpts consistently indicate that higher GVI improves visual comfort, pleasure, and perceived safety along urban riverfront streets. By increasing the amount (VAR_SIZ) of vegetation (OBJ_VEG) through addition (ACT_ADD) in the immediate pedestrian sightline zone (TER_FG), the intervention directly enlarges the green pixel share captured by street-view imagery, thereby elevating GVI and its associated social-safety outcomes.

operation.description: Increase the size and coverage of vegetation in the middleground view by adding more green elements to enhance the Green View Index (GVI) within the visible urban landscape.

pathway.mechanism_description: Higher mid-ground GVI co-occurs with higher Stayability Scores in this descriptive evidence; visual interest and biophilic cues may relate to staying behaviour, but causal direction is not established.

signatures.mechanism: Adding green elements (street trees and associated vegetation) in the middleground zone increases the proportion of green pixels captured in streetscape imagery, thereby raising the Green View Index (GVI). A higher GVI signals a lusher, more comfortable visual environment along the riverfront, which the linked evidence associates with improved stayability and walkability.

scope.description: A single-signature intervention (SIG_ADD_VEG_MG_SIZ) that increases the size and areal coverage of vegetation in the middleground (10–50 m) view field by introducing additional green elements. According to linked evidence SVCs_P_Zhang2024_13, this raises the Green View Index (IND_GVI), a pixel-ratio metric of visible greenery in the streetscape. The target performance dimension is Spatial use and physical activity (PRF_USE, subdimension PRS_WAL_USE), measured through a Stayability Score. While the paper reports no definitive directional effect size (no stable direction / no quantified effect size), the qualitative excerpts consistently associate higher GVI with enhanced pedestrian comfort, willingness to stay, and riverfront vitality—suggesting a positive contribution to walkability outcomes.

operation.description: Increase the size and coverage of vegetation elements in the street-level foreground to enhance visual greenery perceived by pedestrians and captured in street view imagery.

pathway.mechanism_description: Expanding vegetation size in the foreground directly increases the Green View Index (GVI) by maximizing the proportion of green pixels visible at eye level. Street-level greenery creates immediate visual contact with nature, enhancing aesthetic experience through increased canopy coverage, vertical greenery, and street tree density that dominate the pedestrian viewshed.

signatures.mechanism: Enlarging existing tree canopy size in the street-level foreground directly increases the proportion of green pixels captured at eye-level, raising the Green View Index by expanding the visual area occupied by foliage within the pedestrian's cone of vision.

Introducing new shrub-layer vegetation beneath the canopy fills the lower portion of the street-view frame that canopy trees alone cannot occupy, increasing overall green coverage and supplementing GVI by adding a continuous green understory visible to pedestrians.

scope.description: This S2 coordinated intervention superimposes two vegetation layers in the immediate foreground. SIG_MOD_VEG_FG_SIZ (canopy_tree) modifies existing street trees to increase their canopy size, while SIG_ADD_VEG_FG_SIZ (shrub) introduces new shrub plantings to extend ground-level green coverage. Together they target the indicator measured in evidence SVCs_P_Zhu2022_1—the Green View Index (IND_GVI, mean = 28.27%)—by maximising the proportion of visible greenery across multiple vertical strata of the street-view image. The performance dimension is PRF_AES / PRS_AES_NATU (Aesthetic Preference – Naturalness): increasing both canopy extent and understory density is expected to raise perceived street-side greenery above the current descriptive baseline, amplifying the naturalness quality of the streetscape as experienced by pedestrians.
scope_validation_notes: Scope structure is internally consistent with signature_count and phase_count.

operation.description: Increase green vegetation coverage along street corridors to enhance Green View Index (GVI) within pedestrian visual range

pathway.mechanism_description: Greater vegetation size and coverage in the middleground increases the proportion of greenery visible from pedestrian eye level, directly contributing to higher visual walkability scores by creating more pleasant and naturalistic walking environments

signatures.mechanism: Adding canopy trees along street corridors increases the proportion of green pixels captured in the pedestrian's visual field, directly raising the Green View Index (GVI). At the foreground tier, new tree canopies occupy a large angular share of the streetscape image, maximising the per-unit contribution to pixel-level greenery segmentation used in GVI computation.

scope.description: A single-signature intervention (SIG_ADD_VEG_FG_SIZ) that increases the amount of vegetation within the immediate pedestrian visual field along street corridors. As documented in evidence SVCs_P_Wang2024_5, the Green View Index (IND_GVI) is a pixel-ratio indicator derived from panoptic segmentation of street-level imagery; it serves as a component in the visual walkability score under the performance dimension PRF_USE (Spatial use and physical activity), subdimension PRS_WAL_PERC. Adding foreground canopy trees enlarges the green-pixel share in the segmented image, thereby raising GVI. The evidence reports a descriptive relationship (DIR_NA) whereby GVI contributes to the composite Type of Visual Walkability (TVW) classification—greenery-dominated streets (Type A) are characterised by 'sufficient greenery' and are associated with higher visual walkability. Increasing vegetation coverage is therefore expected to shift street segments toward the greenery-dominated type, improving the perceived visual walkability outcome.

operation.description: Modify the configuration and spatial arrangement of street vegetation to create distinct walkability environments that influence pedestrian visual experience and behavioral patterns

pathway.mechanism_description: Different vegetation configurations are descriptively associated with distinct walkability types in this study; varied enclosure and spatial definition may underlie these typologies, but no directional or causal claim about safety/comfort outcomes is established.

signatures.mechanism: Modifying the spatial arrangement and configuration of street vegetation alters the tree view index—the proportion of vegetation visible in the pedestrian's visual field—which is a key input to the Type of Visual Walkability (IND_TVW) classification. By redistributing canopy trees along the street corridor, the street scene shifts between walkability types (e.g., greenery-dominated Type A versus other types), thereby reshaping the pedestrian visual experience and associated behavioural patterns.

scope.description: SCP_S1 encodes a single operation, SIG_MOD_VEG_MG_POS, that modifies the spatial arrangement of street vegetation (canopy trees) within the mid-ground zone of the street corridor. Linked evidence SVCs_P_Wang2024_7 shows that the Type of Visual Walkability indicator (IND_TVW) classifies street environments based on the tree view index and other visual proportions derived from street-level imagery. The intervention acts on the performance dimension PRF_USE (Spatial use and physical activity), subdimension PRS_WAL_PERC (perceptual walkability). Because the study is descriptive and classificatory (DIR_NA), no single directional outcome is asserted; rather, repositioning vegetation shifts the street's TVW category, producing qualitatively distinct walkability environments (e.g., greenery-dominated vs. pavement-dominated) that influence pedestrian perception and use.
