operation.description: Modify the texture and visual characteristics of vegetation elements in the street middleground to enhance overall visual quality perception through improved greenery composition and aesthetic arrangement.

pathway.mechanism_description: Mid-ground vegetation texture and visual characteristics co-occur with composite street visual quality scores in this descriptive analysis; aesthetic coherence and naturalness are plausible mediators but causal direction is not established.

signatures.mechanism: Modifying the textural qualities of middleground vegetation—foliage density, leaf grain, canopy surface pattern—directly alters the high-dimensional semantic features extracted by deep-learning segmentation models (excerpts [11],[13]), shifting the composite Visual Quality Index toward higher aesthetic ratings by replacing visually monotonous or degraded greenery textures with richer, more layered ones.

Reorganising the spatial arrangement and compositional grouping of vegetation elements in the street middleground improves the overall greenery composition perceived in street-view imagery; better-distributed and aesthetically structured planting positions increase the view index of ecological elements at the block level, which the evidence links to enhanced positive psychological perceptions (excerpt [3]) and reduced visual dominance of negative elements such as bare ground (excerpt [23]).

scope.description: This S2 intervention superimposes two simultaneous modifications on middleground vegetation: SIG_MOD_VEG_MG_TEX adjusts the surface texture and foliage quality of street trees and planting, while SIG_MOD_VEG_MG_POS restructures the spatial arrangement and compositional grouping of shrubs and understorey greenery. Together they target the composite Visual Quality Index (IND_VIS_QUA) reported in evidence SVCs_P_ZhangQ2021_1. The linked evidence, situated in the performance dimension PRF_AES / subdimension PRS_AES_QUAL, demonstrates that higher visual exposure to well-composed ecological elements at block level increases positive psychological perceptions and aesthetic quality scores (excerpts [3],[8],[22]). Although the recorded relationship direction is DIR_NA and effect size is not explicitly reported—reflecting the study's composite, machine-learning-derived scoring rather than a single directional coefficient—the qualitative pattern across the source paper consistently indicates that enriched greenery texture and improved planting composition elevate the street visual quality composite score. The superposition pattern is warranted because both variables act on the same object class (OBJ_VEG) within the same depth zone (TER_MG) and are inseparable aspects of the same design intent: one cannot meaningfully improve 'greenery composition and aesthetic arrangement' without simultaneously addressing textural quality and spatial positioning.
scope_validation_notes: Scope structure is internally consistent with signature_count and phase_count.

operation.description: Increase the quantity and coverage of green vegetation in the middle-ground visual field to enhance the Green View Index (GVI) within urban street environments and residential viewing perspectives.

pathway.mechanism_description: A positive dose-response relationship between mid-ground GVI and GHQ-12 mental-health scores is observed; visual contact with vegetation may support restoration via attention-recovery and stress-reduction pathways, although these psychological mediators are not directly tested.

signatures.mechanism: Adding green vegetation (primarily canopy trees) in the middle-ground visual field increases the pixel-ratio of green in street-view imagery, directly raising the Green View Index (GVI). A higher GVI enriches the everyday visual exposure to greenness experienced by residents along urban streets and from residential viewing perspectives, which is associated with improvements mental health outcomes.

scope.description: SCP_S1 comprises a single signature SIG_ADD_VEG_MG_SIZ. The intervention adds vegetation quantity and coverage within the middle-ground (10–50 m) zone of urban streets and residential viewsheds, thereby increasing the Green View Index (IND_GVI). Linked evidence SVCs_P_Cheng2024_1 demonstrates that a higher GVI is positively associated (DIR_POS) with mental health as measured by GHQ-12 scores, with an effect size of 8.450, under the performance dimension PRF_EMO (Emotion regulation and cognitive modulation) and subdimension PRS_MNT_WLBG (Wellbeing). The study computed GVI from Baidu Street View imagery within neighbourhood buffers and found that migrants in inner-suburban areas with higher GVI reported better mental health, supporting the rationale that increasing the visible amount of green vegetation in everyday streetscapes yields measurable mental health benefits.
scope_validation_notes: Scope structure is internally consistent with signature_count and phase_count.

operation.description: Add tree canopy elements in the foreground to reduce sky view factor in pedestrian zones, creating overhead shading structures that block direct sky exposure while maintaining visual permeability at eye level.

pathway.mechanism_description: Trees with spreading canopy forms reduce SVF by occluding portions of the hemispherical sky dome visible from ground level. Lower SVF values decrease shortwave and longwave radiation exchange between ground surfaces and the sky vault, reducing radiative heat gain and thereby improving thermal comfort as reflected in thermal sensation votes.

signatures.mechanism: Introducing tree canopy elements increases the overhead vegetation mass within the pedestrian zone, directly occupying the visible sky hemisphere and reducing the Sky View Factor (SVF) as measured from street level. Greater canopy coverage intercepts solar irradiation and lowers PET, improving thermal comfort.

Complementary overhead shading structures are adjusted in coverage extent to further block direct sky exposure, reinforcing SVF reduction. Their open or latticed design maintains visual permeability at eye level, ensuring pedestrian spatial orientation and comfort while maximising the overhead shielding effect.

scope.description: This S2-superposition operation coordinates two signatures in the same foreground tier. SIG_ADD_VEG_FG_SIZ introduces canopy trees that occupy the overhead sky hemisphere, while SIG_MOD_BLT_FG_SIZ adjusts overhead built shading structures to extend sky-blocking coverage. Together they reduce the Sky View Factor (IND_SVF) as documented in evidence SVCs_P_Chiang2023_3 (effect size r = 0.714). A lower SVF decreases intercepted solar irradiance reaching the pedestrian plane, which the evidence links to improved Thermal Sensation Vote under the performance dimension PRF_THR (Thermal Comfort), subdimension PRS_THR_VOTE, with a positive relationship direction (DIR_POS): as the design intervention lowers SVF, thermal comfort ratings improve. The layering of vegetation canopy and built overhead shade in the same depth zone creates a redundant shielding envelope that maximises SVF reduction while the differentiated material properties—porous foliage above and open structural frames at eye level—preserve visual permeability for pedestrians.
scope_validation_notes: Scope structure is internally consistent with signature_count and phase_count.

operation.description: Increase the size and coverage of vegetation elements in the foreground view to enhance Green View Index (GVI) values in street-level perspectives.

pathway.mechanism_description: Higher GVI represents greater visible greenery in the visual field. However, the study found no significant relationship between GVI and thermal sensation vote, suggesting that visual greenery alone does not necessarily translate to thermal comfort improvement without considering actual shading effectiveness and microclimate modification.

signatures.mechanism: Increasing the size and areal coverage of foreground vegetation raises the proportion of green pixels captured in street-level panoramic images, thereby elevating the Green View Index (GVI). Larger canopy spread and denser foliage within the immediate pedestrian view field directly increase perceived greenness at eye level.

scope.description: SIG_MOD_VEG_FG_SIZ is the single operation: modifying the size/coverage attribute of foreground vegetation (OBJ_VEG) to increase GVI at the street level. According to linked evidence SVCs_P_Chiang2023_4, although higher GVI accurately captures street-level greening, its relationship to the thermal comfort indicator Thermal Sensation Vote (IND_GVI → PRF_THR / PRS_THR_VOTE) is non-significant (DIR_NS, β = −0.078). The source paper explains that GVI accounts for all visible vertical greenery (trees, shrubs, ground cover, grass) but does not reliably represent overhead shade provision; therefore the shielding of solar radiation—the primary driver of pedestrian thermal comfort—is not strongly improved by simply enlarging visible green area in the foreground view. The intervention is expected to raise GVI but yield no statistically significant change in subjective thermal sensation.

operation.description: Add or increase tree canopy coverage in the foreground to reduce sky view factor and enhance overhead shading elements in urban spaces

pathway.mechanism_description: Increasing tree canopy size and coverage directly obstructs the visible sky hemisphere, thereby reducing SVF values. Lower SVF corresponds to greater overhead obstruction, which increases perceived shade by blocking direct solar radiation and creating cooler microenvironments for pedestrians

signatures.mechanism: Introducing new canopy trees in the immediate streetscape increases the total amount of overhead vegetation coverage, directly occluding visible sky area from the pedestrian vantage point and thereby reducing Sky View Factor (SVF).

Shaping and spreading the canopy outline laterally optimises the overhead shading footprint, ensuring contiguous shade coverage that further closes the hemispherical sky opening and enhances the functional shading element above the pedestrian plane.

scope.description: This S2 superposition coordinates two spatial-variable changes on the same object and tier. SIG_ADD_VEG_FG_SIZ (primary) adds canopy trees in the foreground, increasing vegetation amount and directly lowering the Sky View Factor indicator (IND_SVF). SIG_MOD_VEG_FG_SHP (secondary) adjusts the canopy spread to maximise the overhead shading envelope, reinforcing SVF reduction. Per linked evidence SVCs_P_Chiang2023_5, the relationship between SVF and perceived shade is strongly negative (effect size = −0.854), indicating that the combined increase in canopy size and optimised canopy shape substantially improves Perceived Shade within the thermal-comfort performance dimension (PRF_THR, subdimension PRS_THR_VOTE). Because lower SVF reduces solar irradiance reaching pedestrians—through both direct shade casting and evapotranspiration—the expected outcome is a marked improvement in outdoor thermal comfort on urban streets.
scope_validation_notes: Scope structure is internally consistent with signature_count and phase_count.

operation.description: Modify the size and configuration of surrounding buildings to increase sky view factor, allowing more open sky to be visible from ground level in urban spaces.

pathway.mechanism_description: Reducing building height or modifying building mass in the background increases the proportion of visible sky hemisphere (SVF), which directly increases the amount of sunlight that can reach ground level and enhances perceived sunlight exposure in urban environments.

signatures.mechanism: Reducing the height and bulk of surrounding buildings directly decreases the solid angle of built mass occluding the sky hemisphere, thereby increasing SVF and allowing more solar radiation and open sky to reach ground level.

Reconfiguring the spatial arrangement and setback of surrounding buildings redistributes the angular obstruction of sky across the hemisphere, opening wider sky corridors between structures and further increasing SVF from the pedestrian vantage point.

scope.description: This S2 intervention coordinates two simultaneous modifications on built structures in a superposition pattern. SIG_MOD_BLT_MG_SIZ (primary) reduces building height and bulk, while SIG_MOD_BLT_MG_POS (secondary) reconfigures building arrangement and setbacks. Together they increase the Sky View Factor (IND_SVF) as captured by linked evidence SVCs_P_Chiang2023_6. That evidence reports a strong positive relationship (β = 0.814, DIR_POS) between SVF and perceived sunlight within the thermal comfort performance dimension (PRF_THR, subdimension PRS_THR_VOTE). By enlarging the visible sky proportion from street level, the coordinated intervention enhances solar access and the subjective experience of sunlight, improving pedestrian thermal comfort evaluation in urban street canyons.

operation.description: Increase the size and coverage of vegetation elements along street corridors to enhance overall green visibility from pedestrian viewpoints.

pathway.mechanism_description: Expanding vegetation size increases the proportion of green pixels captured in street-level imagery, directly elevating Green View Index (GVI) measurements. Larger vegetation masses occupy more visual field area, improving aesthetic perception through enhanced green coverage in the middleground viewing plane.

signatures.mechanism: Increasing the profile area and spatial coverage of street corridor vegetation enlarges the proportion of green pixels captured in pedestrian-level street view images, directly raising the Green View Index (GVI). Excerpt [9] confirms: 'The fundamental aspect of enhancing street visual greenery is increasing the profile area of green vegetation along roads.'

scope.description: SIG_MOD_VEG_MG_SIZ is a single-signature intervention that modifies the size and coverage of existing vegetation (primarily canopy trees) along street corridors to increase the Green View Index (GVI) as perceived from pedestrian viewpoints. Linked evidence SVCs_P_Dong2018_1 reports a descriptive GVI distribution across Beijing's road network (mean = 17.1%, median = 15.2%, range 0.8%–58.4%), establishing a baseline with substantial room for improvement. By enlarging the visible profile area of green vegetation—the fundamental driver of GVI per the source paper—this intervention is expected to push GVI values upward from the current distribution. This directly serves the aesthetic-naturalness subdimension (PRF_AES / PRS_AES_NATU): higher GVI corresponds to greater perceived streetscape greenery, contributing to more relaxing pedestrian experiences (excerpt [1]). The relationship direction is DIR_NA (descriptive baseline), so the intervention targets upward movement from the reported distribution toward higher visual greenery levels.

source.citation: Dong, R., Zhang, Y., & Zhao, J. (2018). How Green Are the Streets Within the Sixth Ring Road of Beijing? An Analysis Based on Tencent Street View Pictures and the Green View Index. International Journal of Environmental Research and Public Health, 15(7), 1367.

operation.description: Increase vegetation coverage in the background layer of street view to enhance the Sky View Factor by adding green elements that frame the visual field without blocking sky visibility.

pathway.mechanism_description: Higher SVF is positively associated with Street Spatial Quality Scores; openness combined with appropriate background vegetation may underlie this association, but the linked evidence does not test these perceptual mediators directly.

signatures.mechanism: Adding canopy trees in the background layer (50 m+) increases the Green Visual Index by enlarging the vegetated pixel proportion in street-view imagery. Crucially, these elements are placed at sufficient distance and height to frame the visual field rather than occlude it, thereby preserving or perceptually enhancing the Sky View Factor (IND_SVF). The framing effect structures the sky opening, making it more legible and contributing positively to perceived spatial quality.

scope.description: Single-signature intervention SIG_ADD_VEG_BG_SIZ. As supported by evidence SVCs_P_Gao2024_1, the Sky View Factor (IND_SVF) has a positive relationship (DIR_POS, β = 0.209) with the Street Spatial Quality Score on the performance dimension PRF_AES / PRS_AES_QUAL. The operation adds background-tier canopy trees that increase vegetation coverage while framing—rather than obstructing—sky visibility, thereby maintaining or enhancing SVF. The expected outcome is an improvement in the aesthetic quality rating of the street space.
scope_validation_notes: Scope structure is internally consistent with signature_count and phase_count.

performance_effect.expected_change_note: May increase Street Spatial Quality Score

source.citation: Gao, L., Xiang, X., Chen, W., Nong, R., Zhang, Q., Chen, X., & Chen, Y. (2024). Research on Urban Street Spatial Quality Based on Street View Image Segmentation. Sustainability, 16(16), 7184.

operation.description: Increase the quantity and canopy coverage of street trees in the foreground view to enhance green visibility along urban streets

pathway.mechanism_description: Higher Traffic Recognition (vehicle/traffic-element visibility) is positively associated with Street Spatial Quality Score in this linear analysis; perceived traffic presence may signal active, well-defined street edges, but causal direction is not established.

signatures.mechanism: Introducing additional street trees where previously absent increases the total count of green elements captured in street-view image segmentation, directly raising the Green Visual Index (GVI) pixel ratio and thereby improving the Traffic Recognition indicator's contribution to overall Street Spatial Quality Score.

Expanding the canopy spread of existing and newly planted street trees enlarges the proportional area of vegetation pixels within the foreground viewing cone, amplifying green visibility beyond what tree count alone achieves and reinforcing the GVI uplift that feeds into the composite quality score.

scope.description: This S2 superposition coordinates two simultaneous manipulations of the same object-tier combination. SIG_ADD_VEG_FG_SIZ introduces new canopy trees into the foreground streetscape, while SIG_MOD_VEG_FG_SIZ enlarges the canopy coverage of those and existing trees. Together they target the Traffic Recognition indicator (IND_TRF) referenced in evidence SVCs_P_Gao2024_4. The linked evidence shows a positive relationship (DIR_POS, β = 0.1566) between enhanced green visibility and Street Spatial Quality Score on the Aesthetic Preference dimension (PRF_AES, subdimension PRS_AES_QUAL). By superimposing quantity increase (ACT_ADD) with canopy expansion (ACT_MOD), the intervention maximises the green pixel ratio captured by semantic segmentation, boosting the composite quality assessment that integrates GVI alongside sky visibility and enclosure metrics.
scope_validation_notes: Scope structure is internally consistent with signature_count and phase_count.

source.citation: Gao, L., Xiang, X., Chen, W., Nong, R., Zhang, Q., Chen, X., & Chen, Y. (2024). Research on Urban Street Spatial Quality Based on Street View Image Segmentation. Sustainability, 16(16), 7184.

operation.description: Add vegetation elements along street corridors to increase the size and coverage of green infrastructure within the urban ecological network, particularly focusing on foreground visual exposure in the street canyon environment.

pathway.mechanism_description: Adding vegetation in the foreground tier increases visible greenery from pedestrian and street-level perspectives, raising the Green View Index through a higher green-pixel proportion in street view imagery; this is linearly associated with the mean GVI observed in the Chengdu Third Ring Road area.

signatures.mechanism: Introducing canopy trees within the street canyon directly increases the proportion of green pixels captured in street-level panoramic imagery, raising the Green View Index by enlarging the visible vegetated area in the immediate visual field of pedestrians.

Positioning shrub-layer plantings along the linear street corridor alignment extends continuous green coverage into the social-field depth zone, bridging ecological pinch points identified in the network analysis and supplementing GVI gains through additional green mass visible further down the street perspective.

scope.description: This S2 coordinated intervention weaves SIG_ADD_VEG_FG_SIZ (primary canopy additions for immediate visual coverage) with SIG_ADD_VEG_MG_POS (strategic corridor-aligned shrub plantings). As documented in linked evidence SVCs_P_Hou2025_1, the combined operation targets the Green View Index (IND_GVI) measured via semantic segmentation of street-view imagery across Chengdu's Third Ring Road area. The intervention is expected to produce a positive change (DIR_POS) on the aesthetic preference performance dimension (PRF_AES, subdimension PRS_AES_NATU), with the study reporting a mean GVI effect size of 22.55. The primary signature directly increases the vegetated pixel ratio within the foreground street canyon view, while the secondary signature ensures that vegetation is distributed along ecological corridors identified through MSPA and circuit-theory analysis, thereby achieving the multi-scale coordination of ecological networks and street green space networks emphasised throughout the source study.
scope_validation_notes: Scope structure is internally consistent with signature_count and phase_count.

operation.description: Increase the size and quantity of vegetation elements in the middle ground of urban visual scenes to enhance green visual index coverage

pathway.mechanism_description: Expanding vegetation size in the middleground directly increases the proportion of green pixels captured in street-level imagery, thereby elevating GVI values and enhancing residents' visual exposure to greenness in their daily urban environment

signatures.mechanism: Introducing additional vegetation elements and enlarging their volumetric presence in the middle-ground zone increases the proportion of green pixels captured in street-level imagery, thereby raising the Green View Index (IND_GVI). As excerpt [17] notes, 'adding a structure of shrubs can significantly increase the visual greenery volume,' and excerpt [22] confirms the principle is 'an increase in the density of the greenery volume on the façade.' The added vegetation mass occupies a larger share of the observer's visual field at the 10–50 m range, directly boosting three-dimensional greenness exposure.

scope.description: A single-signature intervention (SIG_ADD_VEG_MG_SIZ) that adds vegetation size and quantity in the middle ground of urban streetscapes. Linked evidence SVCs_P_Hu2023_1 demonstrates that this operation positively shifts the Green View Index (IND_GVI) with a substantial effect size of 7.46 along the performance dimension PRF_AES (Aesthetic Preference, subdimension PRS_AES_NATU). The study shows that two-dimensional greenery indicators alone are insufficient (excerpts [18], [21], [25]) and that increasing the three-dimensional visual greenery volume—captured by GVI—meaningfully enhances perceived naturalness. The relationship direction is DIR_POS: greater vegetation size/amount in the middle ground yields higher visual greenness exposure, improving the aesthetic-naturalness outcome.
scope_validation_notes: Scope structure is internally consistent with signature_count and phase_count.

performance_effect.expected_change_note: May increase Visual greenness exposure

source.citation: Hu, A., Yabuki, N., Fukuda, T., Kaga, H., Takeda, S., & Matsuo, K. (2023). Harnessing multiple data sources and emerging technologies for comprehensive urban green space evaluation. Cities, 143, 104562.

operation.description: Increase street-level vegetation (visible greenery) along high-density urban blocks to modify the urban morphological profile and reduce pollutant accumulation at pedestrian level

pathway.mechanism_description: Increasing visible street-level vegetation raises GVI, a component of urban morphology used in the ML model. Higher GVI may co-occur with canopy coverage that alters wind flow and aids pollutant dispersion at pedestrian level, contributing to lower predicted PM2.5/PM10; the relationship is one of nine predictors in a multi-variable regression and is not isolated as causal.

signatures.mechanism: Adding vegetation quantity and coverage along running routes increases the proportion of green pixels captured at eye level in panoramic street-view imagery, directly raising the Green View Index (GVI). Canopy trees are the most effective subtype because their crown spread maximises green area within the runner's visual cone at eye level.

scope.description: SIG_ADD_VEG_FG_SIZ is a single-signature intervention that introduces additional vegetation volume and coverage directly along urban streets to elevate the Green View Index (IND_GVI). According to linked evidence SVCs_P_Huang2022_1, higher GVI is negatively associated (DIR_NEG) with the performance dimension PRF_USE (Physical Environment, subdimension PRS_PHY_AIRH), specifically PM2.5 and PM10 concentration distribution in high-density urban blocks. By increasing the size/amount (VAR_SIZ) of vegetation (OBJ_VEG) through addition (ACT_ADD) in the foreground tier (TER_FG), the percentage of greenery within the eye-level visual field rises, thereby improving GVI and contributing to reduced air pollution exposure in the surrounding area.
scope_validation_notes: Scope structure is internally consistent with signature_count and phase_count.

performance_effect.expected_change_note: May decrease PM2.5 and PM10 concentration distribution in high-density urban blocks
performance_effect.framework_mapping_note: Physical-health outcome retained within spatial use and physical activity as an embodied exposure/activity proxy.

source.citation: Huang, C., Hu, T., Duan, Y., Li, Q., Chen, N., Wang, Q., Zhou, M., & Rao, P. (2022). Effect of urban morphology on air pollution distribution in high-density urban blocks based on mobile monitoring and machine learning. Building and Environment, 219, 109173.

operation.description: Increase the amount and coverage of visible vegetation elements in the urban streetscape to enhance the Green View Index across the main urban area.

pathway.mechanism_description: Adding more vegetation in the middle ground increases the proportion of green pixels captured in street-level imagery, directly elevating the Green View Index which measures the percentage of greenery visible from pedestrian perspective.

signatures.mechanism: Introducing additional vegetation (primarily trees) into the urban streetscape increases the proportion of green pixels captured in street-level panoramic imagery, thereby raising the Green View Index. Excerpt [8] confirms GVI is quantified as 'the percentage of trees' in the street view, so adding canopy trees directly increases GVI values.

scope.description: A single-signature intervention (SIG_ADD_VEG_MG_SIZ) that increases the amount and spatial coverage of visible vegetation along urban streets to elevate the Green View Index. Linked evidence SVCs_P_Jin2023_1 reports a descriptive mean GVI of 23.08% across the main urban area, measured via semantic segmentation of Baidu street-view panoramas. The performance dimension is PRF_AES (Aesthetic Preference), subdimension PRS_AES_QUAL, where higher GVI values correspond to improved perceived streetscape quality. As noted in excerpt [11], 'spaces with higher green visibility are more attractive to urban users and the frequency of street use increases,' supporting the aesthetic-preference outcome direction. The relationship direction is DIR_NA (descriptive baseline), meaning the evidence establishes the current GVI level against which this additive intervention is proposed rather than reporting a tested causal effect.
scope_validation_notes: Scope structure is internally consistent with signature_count and phase_count.

operation.description: Add or increase vegetation elements along walkways and streets to enhance visual enclosure and green environment

pathway.mechanism_description: More visible vegetation along walkways is positively correlated with visual enclosure, which is in turn associated with walking activity; the directional causal chain through perceived safety and comfort is plausible but not directly tested.

signatures.mechanism: Adding or increasing vegetation elements (e.g., street trees, shrubs) along walkways and streets raises the proportion of vertical green surfaces flanking the pedestrian path, thereby increasing visual enclosure—the degree to which the street space is perceived as a defined, bounded outdoor room. Greater enclosure enhances the sense of a coherent, human-scaled green environment that encourages walking.

scope.description: SIG_ADD_VEG_FG_SIZ represents the addition of vegetation along walkways and streets to strengthen visual enclosure. Evidence SVCs_P_Kang2020_2 links the indicator Visual Enclosure (IND_VEN) to the performance dimension Spatial use and physical activity (PRF_USE, subdimension PRS_WAL_WALK) with a positive relationship direction (DIR_POS). The intervention increases IND_VEN by introducing vertical green mass at the street edge, which frames the pedestrian corridor and heightens spatial definition. This positive shift in visual enclosure is associated with greater walkability and walking activity, consistent with the evidence that street-view-measured enclosure predicts health-related walking behaviour.
scope_validation_notes: Scope structure is internally consistent with signature_count and phase_count.

performance_effect.expected_change_note: Increase Walkability/Walking activity

source.citation: Kang, Y., Zhang, F., Gao, S., Lin, H., & Liu, Y. (2020). A review of urban physical environment sensing using street view imagery in public health studies. Annals of GIS, 26(3), 261–275.

operation.description: Add diverse vegetation elements with varied textures and forms to increase visual complexity in the middleground viewing zone, creating layered planting compositions with mixed species that enhance visual richness

pathway.mechanism_description: Increased textural diversity and spatial layering of vegetation elevates visual complexity perceived by viewers, which positively correlates with subjective assessments of spatial interest and aesthetic engagement measured on visual complexity ranking scales

signatures.mechanism: Introducing canopy trees with irregular branching patterns and varied foliage textures generates high-frequency textural detail across the middleground. The paper notes that complexity increases with vegetation due to 'shape defining points' associated with 'irregular dispersion of the tree canopy and the branching patterns of trees,' directly raising the Fractal Dimension coefficient by increasing self-similar surface complexity.

Adding shrubs with diverse growth forms beneath the canopy layer introduces varied silhouette outlines and additional shape-defining points at a lower stratum. This increases the Shannon Diversity Index across vegetation species proportions and contributes irregular edge geometry that elevates Fractal Dimension, while maintaining medium-level visual permeability the paper associates with optimal perceived complexity.

scope.description: This is an SCP_S2 coordinated intervention combining SIG_ADD_VEG_MG_TEX (primary) and SIG_ADD_VEG_MG_SHP (secondary) in a superposition pattern, where textural diversity and shape diversity are layered onto the same middleground zone through different vegetation subtypes. As linked to evidence SVCs_P_Kawshalya2022_1, the combined addition of texturally rich canopy trees and morphologically diverse shrubs increases the Fractal Dimension indicator (IND_FRD) by amplifying self-similar geometric complexity and species diversity within the streetscape view. This targets the performance dimension PRF_AES, subdimension PRS_AES_RICH (visual richness), with an expected positive outcome direction (DIR_POS, effect size r = 0.661). The paper's finding that 'medium to high level vegetation can make the most significant impact on perceived complexity with the shape defining points of its irregular growth' supports the layered superposition of texture and form variables rather than a single-variable intervention.
scope_validation_notes: Scope structure is internally consistent with signature_count and phase_count.

performance_effect.expected_change_note: Increase Subjective ranking of visual complexity (1-7 scale)

source.citation: Kawshalya, L. W. G., Weerasinghe, U. G. D., & Chandrasekara, D. P. (2022). The impact of visual complexity on perceived safety and comfort of the users: A study on urban streetscape of Sri Lanka. PLOS ONE, 17(8), e0272074.

operation.description: Add vegetation with varied textures and forms in the middleground to increase visual complexity through diverse plant species, layered canopies, and mixed foliage patterns.

pathway.mechanism_description: Higher Road View Index (visible road presence/quality) is comparatively associated with higher perceived safety, plausibly via improved environmental legibility and signalled maintenance; the linked relationship is comparative, not necessarily causal.

signatures.mechanism: Adding canopy trees with irregular branching patterns and mixed foliage textures introduces shape-defining points that raise the Shannon Diversity Index, increasing visual complexity to medium levels and thereby reducing the road-dominated visual proportion captured by the Road View Index (IND_RVI)

Adding diverse shrub species with varied growth forms creates a layered vertical structure beneath the canopy tier, contributing additional shape-defining points and species richness that further diversify the visual field and displace road surface area in the scene, lowering IND_RVI

scope.description: This S2 coordinated intervention superimposes two vegetation signatures in the middleground: SIG_ADD_VEG_MG_TEX (canopy_tree) as the primary layer introduces textural complexity through irregular branching and foliage patterns, while SIG_ADD_VEG_MG_SHP (shrub) as the secondary layer adds form diversity through varied plant species at a lower stratum. Together they create the 'layered canopies and mixed foliage patterns' described in the IOM. As evidenced by SVCs_P_Kawshalya2022_4, increasing vegetation proportion and species diversity raises the Shannon Diversity Index toward medium visual complexity, which the study's bell-shaped cubic relationship (R²=0.439, p<.000) shows is optimal for perceived safety (PRS_SOC_SAFE within PRF_SOC). The combined signatures reduce the Road View Index (IND_RVI) by displacing road surface area in the visual frame with diverse vegetative cover, producing a positive (DIR_POS) effect on perceived safety outcomes.
scope_validation_notes: Scope structure is internally consistent with signature_count and phase_count.

Indicator-direction neutralized in the 2026-03-19 repair patch because the linked evidence reports a positive RVI–perceived-safety association, while the generated IOM narrative implied reduced road dominance; manual review is recommended before asserting a signed intervention effect.

operation.description: Increase green vegetation coverage within the visible field of view to enhance the Green View Index (GVI) along pedestrian routes and public spaces

pathway.mechanism_description: Higher GVI is linearly associated with longer leisure walking time; greener streetscapes may provide more pleasant walking environments via improved visual comfort and naturalness, but these mediators are inferred rather than directly tested.

signatures.mechanism: Adding canopy trees along pedestrian routes directly increases the proportion of green pixels captured in street-level panoramic imagery, thereby raising the Green View Index (GVI). Canopy trees maximise pixel coverage in the foreground zone because their projected crown area occupies a large share of the visual field at close range, producing the strongest per-unit uplift in GVI.

scope.description: SIG_ADD_VEG_FG_SIZ encodes a single-layer intervention that increases the amount (VAR_SIZ) of vegetation (OBJ_VEG) by introducing (ACT_ADD) canopy trees in the foreground tier (TER_FG) of pedestrian routes. Linked evidence SVCs_P_Ki2021_4 demonstrates that higher Green View Index (IND_GVI) values along neighbourhood streets are positively associated (DIR_POS) with leisure walking time within the Spatial use and physical activity performance dimension (PRF_USE / PRS_WAL_WALK), with a substantial effect size of 4.241. By planting street trees that enlarge the visible green coverage in the pedestrian's immediate field of view, the intervention raises GVI and is therefore expected to increase leisure walking duration.
scope_validation_notes: Scope structure is internally consistent with signature_count and phase_count.

performance_effect.expected_change_note: Increase Leisure Walking Time

operation.description: Increase the size and quantity of vegetation in the foreground viewing area to enhance Tree View Factor (TVF) from street-level perspective.

pathway.mechanism_description: Higher Tree View Factor (more canopy in the foreground) is linearly associated with higher Perceived Beauty scores; greater natural visual dominance may underlie this via biophilic preference, but this mediator is hypothesized rather than tested.

signatures.mechanism: Adding canopy trees and increasing their size within the immediate foreground zone (0–10 m) raises the proportion of tree pixels captured in street-level panoramic imagery, thereby directly increasing the Tree View Factor (TVF). A higher TVF enriches the green visual content of the streetscape, which the linked evidence shows positively correlates with perceived beauty.

scope.description: A single-signature intervention (SIG_ADD_VEG_FG_SIZ) that increases the size and quantity of foreground vegetation to raise the Tree View Factor (IND_TVF). Linked evidence SVCs_P_Lei2024_2 reports a positive relationship (DIR_POS, β = 0.321) between TVF and the Perceived Beautiful Score (0–5) within the aesthetic preference subdimension (PRF_AES / PRS_AES_PREF). By introducing additional canopy trees and enlarging existing tree crowns in the 0–10 m foreground band, the intervention expands the tree-pixel share of the street-view image, pushing TVF upward and thereby improving the perceived beauty of the streetscape.
scope_validation_notes: Scope structure is internally consistent with signature_count and phase_count.

performance_effect.expected_change_note: Increase Perceived Beautiful Score (0-5)

source.citation: Lei, Y., Zhou, H., Xue, L., Yuan, L., Liu, Y., Wang, M., & Wang, C. (2024). Evaluating and Comparing Human Perceptions of Streets in Two Megacities by Integrating Street-View Images, Deep Learning, and Space Syntax. Buildings, 14(6), 1847.

operation.description: Reduce the size/coverage of tree view factor in the foreground visual field to minimize vegetation dominance in the immediate viewing zone

pathway.mechanism_description: Higher TVF in the foreground is linearly associated with lower Perceived Beauty scores; excessive foreground vegetation may reduce sight lines and openness, but these perceptual mediators are inferred from the negative association rather than empirically tested.

signatures.mechanism: Reducing the size and areal coverage of foreground tree canopies directly lowers the Tree View Factor (the proportion of the visual field occupied by tree pixels). By pruning or thinning canopy extent within the 0–10 m zone, the visual dominance of vegetation is minimised, decreasing the IND_TVF value.

scope.description: A single-signature operation (SIG_MOD_VEG_FG_SIZ) that modifies the size/coverage of canopy trees in the foreground tier. According to linked evidence SVCs_P_Lei2024_4, Tree View Factor (IND_TVF) exhibits a negative relationship (DIR_NEG, β = −0.095) with Perceived Beautiful Score (0–5) on the aesthetic preference subdimension (PRS_AES_PREF) of PRF_AES. Reducing IND_TVF by decreasing the spatial extent and canopy coverage of foreground trees is therefore expected to improve the perceived beauty rating, alleviating the depressive effect that excessive immediate-zone vegetation dominance has on aesthetic preference.
scope_validation_notes: Scope structure is internally consistent with signature_count and phase_count.

performance_effect.expected_change_note: Increase Perceived Beautiful Score (0-5)

source.citation: Lei, Y., Zhou, H., Xue, L., Yuan, L., Liu, Y., Wang, M., & Wang, C. (2024). Evaluating and Comparing Human Perceptions of Streets in Two Megacities by Integrating Street-View Images, Deep Learning, and Space Syntax. Buildings, 14(6), 1847.

operation.description: Increase the visibility and coverage of greenery in the foreground of urban streetscapes to enhance the proportion of green elements visible from eye-level perspective

pathway.mechanism_description: Higher image contrast in street-view imagery is positively correlated with perceived safety, plausibly because visual contrast enhances legibility and signals environmental upkeep; the mechanism should reference image-contrast features rather than vegetation.

signatures.mechanism: Adding canopy trees in the immediate foreground of urban streetscapes increases the proportion of green pixels visible at eye-level, particularly vegetation above the horizon line (>2.5 m), which the study identifies as a stronger predictor of perceived safety than low vegetation. Greater green coverage raises Image Contrast between vegetated and non-vegetated scene components, shifting the visual ratio toward greenery.

scope.description: A single-signature intervention (SIG_ADD_VEG_FG_SIZ) that increases the amount of foreground greenery along urban streets. Linked evidence SVCs_P_Li2015_2 shows that higher visibility of green vegetation—measured through Image Contrast (IND_IMG_CON) between vegetated and built/paved scene elements—has a positive relationship (DIR_POS, β = 0.304) with Perceived Safety Score within the Social Outcomes dimension (PRF_SOC / PRS_SOC_SAFE). The paper specifically demonstrates that vegetation above the horizon (canopy-level, >2.5 m) is the strongest predictor of perceived safety across residential, commercial and public/institutional land uses, while low vegetation (<2.5 m) shows limited significance. By adding canopy trees in the foreground tier, the intervention directly raises the green-vegetation fraction in street-level imagery, thereby increasing image contrast between vegetated and non-vegetated zones and improving perceived safety.
scope_validation_notes: Scope structure is internally consistent with signature_count and phase_count.

performance_effect.expected_change_note: Increase Perceived Safety Score

source.citation: Li, X., Zhang, C., & Li, W. (2015). Does the Visibility of Greenery Increase Perceived Safety in Urban Areas? Evidence from the Place Pulse 1.0 Dataset. ISPRS International Journal of Geo-Information, 4(3), 1166–1183.

operation.description: Add street trees with sufficient canopy size along urban streets to increase shade coverage and reduce sky view factor

pathway.mechanism_description: Larger tree canopies intercept direct solar radiation and create shadow zones on street surfaces. The vertical and horizontal extent of tree crowns blocks the view of open sky from ground level, thereby decreasing Sky View Factor (SVF). This reduction in SVF indicates enhanced shade provision, which contributes to thermal comfort improvement in urban street environments

signatures.mechanism: Adding street trees with sufficient canopy size along urban streets introduces overhead canopy coverage that intercepts direct solar radiation before it reaches the street surface, thereby reducing the Sky View Factor (SVF) and increasing shade provision within the street canyon.

scope.description: A single-signature intervention (SIG_ADD_VEG_MG_SIZ) that introduces canopy trees of sufficient size along urban streets. As documented in linked evidence SVCs_P_Li2018_6, this operation targets the indicator Sky View Factor Decrease (IND_SVF_DEC) within the performance dimension PRF_THR (Thermal Comfort), subdimension PRS_THR_COOL. The addition of large-canopied street trees obstructs the visible sky hemisphere from street level, decreasing SVF by an effect size of 18.52 percentage points. The relationship direction is DIR_POS, meaning that adding trees with larger canopies positively contributes to SVF reduction and therefore enhanced shade provision, improving pedestrian thermal comfort in street canyons. The study confirms that canopy coverage and canopy height have significant positive correlations with shade provision, and that large trees are particularly effective in areas where building heights are moderate to low.
scope_validation_notes: Scope structure is internally consistent with signature_count and phase_count.

performance_effect.expected_change_note: May increase Decrease in Sky View Factor attributed to street trees (Shade Provision)

source.citation: Li, X., Ratti, C., & Seiferling, I. (2018). Quantifying the shade provision of street trees in urban landscape: A case study in Boston, USA, using Google Street View. Landscape and Urban Planning, 169, 81–91.

operation.description: Modify the shape and spatial configuration of vegetation elements to create diverse natural forms and patterns in the middleground landscape, enhancing visual complexity and organic composition.

pathway.mechanism_description: Varied vegetation shapes and natural forms are positively correlated with landscape aesthetic preference scores via the Naturalness Index; preferences for natural-looking forms may underlie this, but the causal contribution is not directly tested.

signatures.mechanism: Modifying the shape/outline of vegetation elements introduces diverse natural crown forms and organic silhouettes that raise the Naturalness Index (IND_NAT) by increasing the perceived variety and biomorphic quality of plant forms in the middleground.

Adjusting the spatial configuration and arrangement of vegetation clusters produces irregular, nature-like distribution patterns rather than rigid geometric layouts, further elevating IND_NAT by reinforcing perceived ecological authenticity and visual complexity in the middleground scene.

scope.description: This S2 coordinated intervention superimposes two simultaneous modifications on middleground vegetation. SIG_MOD_VEG_MG_SHP (primary) reshapes vegetation forms to create diverse, organic silhouettes, while SIG_MOD_VEG_MG_POS (secondary) reconfigures their spatial arrangement into naturalistic, non-uniform patterns. Together they target the Naturalness Index (IND_NAT) referenced in evidence SVCs_P_Li2024_2, which reports a strong positive relationship (β = 0.886, DIR_POS) with landscape aesthetic preference score under the performance dimension PRF_AES / PRS_AES_PREF. By jointly increasing both morphological diversity and spatial heterogeneity of vegetation, the intervention amplifies perceived naturalness, thereby improving aesthetic preference outcomes.
scope_validation_notes: Scope structure is internally consistent with signature_count and phase_count.

performance_effect.expected_change_note: Increase Landscape aesthetic preference score

source.citation: Li, X., Shang, W.-L., Liu, Q., Liu, X., Lyu, Z., & Ochieng, W. (2024). Towards a sustainable city: Deciphering the determinants of restorative park and spatial patterns. Sustainable Cities and Society, 104, 105292.

operation.description: Modify vegetation texture and composition in the middleground to optimize visual complexity and layering as measured by Binocular Vision Index (BVI), creating enhanced depth perception and visual interest.

pathway.mechanism_description: Higher Water View Index (visible water elements in the mid-ground) is positively correlated with landscape aesthetic preference scores; the presence of water features may enhance perceived naturalness and aesthetic value, but the causal mechanism is not directly tested.

signatures.mechanism: Adjusting vegetation texture and compositional layering in the middleground optimizes visual complexity and depth perception (BVI). By modifying planting density and foliage texture, visual penetration is calibrated—avoiding the sense of entrapment noted in dense vegetation (Excerpt 6)—while maintaining richly layered plant coverage that enhances perceptual recovery (Excerpt 5). This recomposition simultaneously modulates sight-lines toward water features, influencing the Water View Index (IND_WAT) by selectively framing or revealing water elements through vegetation gaps, which the linked evidence associates with increased aesthetic preference.

scope.description: SIG_MOD_VEG_MG_TEX encodes a single-signature intervention that modifies middleground vegetation texture and composition to enhance visual complexity and layering. Per linked evidence SVCs_P_Li2024_3, the operative indicator is the Water View Index (IND_WAT) within the performance dimension PRF_AES (Aesthetic Preference, subdimension PRS_AES_PREF). The mechanism operates through recalibrating vegetation texture so that layered planting simultaneously enriches visual depth and modulates the visibility of water elements within the scene. The expected outcome direction is DIR_POS: as vegetation texture is adjusted to create optimal layering, water views become more perceptible and aesthetically framed, increasing landscape aesthetic preference scores. Excerpts 5, 6, 11, and 13 collectively support that balanced vegetation layering (avoiding over-density) combined with enhanced water visibility yields positive restorative and aesthetic outcomes.
scope_validation_notes: Scope structure is internally consistent with signature_count and phase_count.

translation_signature_schema.signature_order: Operation × Semantic Layer × Spatial Layer × Morphological Layer

performance_effect.expected_change_note: Increase Landscape aesthetic preference score

source.citation: Li, X., Shang, W.-L., Liu, Q., Liu, X., Lyu, Z., & Ochieng, W. (2024). Towards a sustainable city: Deciphering the determinants of restorative park and spatial patterns. Sustainable Cities and Society, 104, 105292.

operation.description: Increase street-level visible vegetation across neighborhood streetscapes by adding and enlarging foreground and midground plantings (trees, shrubs, hedges) along road corridors and at intersections, thereby raising the Green View Index. Denser, well-positioned greenery masks physical disorder cues (abandoned cars, trash, damaged facilities, graffiti) and signals active maintenance, reducing perceived physical incivilities.

signatures.mechanism: Adding vegetation mass in the 0-10 m foreground zone directly increases green pixels captured in street-view imagery, raising GVI. Foreground foliage has the largest per-unit effect on pixel ratio because of angular size.

Additional canopy and vertical greening in the 10-50 m midground fills the upper-centre portions of street-view panoramas with green pixels, boosting GVI beyond what foreground planting alone achieves.

Repositioning or distributing vegetation along primary sightlines at street intersections (where GVI images are captured and pedestrians naturally look) ensures green coverage occupies the most perceptually salient portions of the visual field, maximising GVI per unit of planting.

Using dense, multi-layered foliage (canopy + understory + ground cover) increases continuous green pixel clusters and visually screens disorder elements (trash, damaged infrastructure, graffiti) that define perceived physical incivilities, strengthening the negative pathway from GVI to incivility perception.

Removing or replacing built-environment elements associated with physical incivilities (abandoned vehicles, damaged street furniture) simultaneously reduces non-green pixels and frees space for additional vegetation, amplifying the GVI increase and breaking the incivility signal.

scope.description: SCP_S2 neighborhood-street scale: signatures SIG_ADD_VEG_FG_SIZ, SIG_ADD_VEG_MG_SIZ, SIG_MOD_VEG_FG_POS, SIG_MOD_VEG_FG_TEX, and SIG_REM_BLT_FG_SIZ are applied across the streetscape network within residential neighbourhoods (mean GVI 0.27, range 0.12–0.44) in Guangzhou, targeting IND_GVI under PRF_SOC / PRS_SOC_SAFE as reported in SVCs_P_Jing2021_2.
scope_validation_notes: Scope structure is internally consistent with signature_count and phase_count.

pathway.mechanism_description: Adding layered vegetation (street trees, mid-level shrubs) along neighbourhood streets increases the visible green proportion in pedestrian-level imagery, raising the Green View Index. Higher street-view greenness is hypothesised to reduce residents’ fear of neighbourhood crime through two mediating pathways: reducing perceived physical and social incivilities, and enhancing social integration and informal surveillance in the neighbourhood.

translation_signature_schema.signature_order: Operation × Semantic Layer × Spatial Layer × Morphological Layer

performance_effect.expected_change_note: Increase Perceived physical incivilities (abandoned cars, trash, damaged facilities, graffiti, noise) as mediator

source.citation: Jing, F., Liu, L., Zhou, S., Song, J., Wang, L., Zhou, H., Wang, Y., & Ma, R. (2021). Assessing the Impact of Street-View Greenery on Fear of Neighborhood Crime in Guangzhou, China. International Journal of Environmental Research and Public Health, 18(1), 311.

operation.description: Selectively modifying the crown shape and canopy size of foreground and midground vegetation, and adjusting tree spacing, to optimise the sky view factor (SVF) as experienced from ground-level street perspectives. By crown-raising, thinning, or replacing dense-canopy species with open-form species, the weighted sky-pixel ratio increases, enhancing perceived openness and visual comfort in urban green-space corridors.

signatures.mechanism: Crown-raising and lateral pruning reshape the foreground tree canopy silhouette, directly reducing the solid angle subtended by vegetation in the hemisphere above the observer, thereby increasing the sky-pixel proportion in the SVF computation.

Reducing canopy spread or selecting smaller-crowned species in the midground lowers cumulative sky occlusion across the 10-50 m depth range, which contributes substantially to the weighted sky(i) summation in the SVF formula.

Increasing inter-tree spacing or staggering planting rows creates sky-view corridors along the street axis; because SVF integrates across azimuth directions, opening even a few directional channels raises the aggregate weighted sky ratio perceived at ground level.

scope.description: SCP_S2 intervention combining SIG_MOD_VEG_FG_SHP (primary crown reshaping), SIG_MOD_VEG_MG_SIZ (midground canopy extent management), and SIG_MOD_VEG_FG_POS (spacing optimisation) to shift IND_SVF upward, linked to SVCs_P_Meng2024_2 with DIR_POS effect (β = 1.011) on PRF_AES / PRS_AES_CMFT visual-comfort perception.
scope_validation_notes: Scope structure is internally consistent with signature_count and phase_count.

pathway.mechanism_description: Sky View Factor is a positive predictor of Positive Perception Scores in this analysis; greater visible sky may enhance perceived openness and brightness, although other co-varying streetscape features may also contribute to the predicted score.

translation_signature_schema.signature_order: Operation × Semantic Layer × Spatial Layer × Morphological Layer

performance_effect.expected_change_note: Increase Positive Perception Score

source.citation: Meng, Y., Shi, J., Lyu, M., Sun, D., & Fukuda, H. (2024). Research into the Influence Mechanisms of Visual-Comfort and Landscape Indicators of Urban Green Spaces. Land, 13(10), 1688.

operation.description: Increase the Sky View Factor along streetscapes by reducing the height and massing of sky-blocking built elements in the midground while selectively shaping foreground tree canopies to maintain greenery presence without occluding the sky. This configurational adjustment enhances the proportion of visible sky pixels, which the ℓ1-regularized sparse model identifies as a significant positive predictor (weight 1.95) of Class 2 (Calm/Greenery) subjective perceptions.

signatures.mechanism: Reducing building height or increasing setback lowers the solid angle subtended by built surfaces in the hemispherical view, directly increasing the sky pixel ratio in the SVF integral (∑ω·sky(i)/∑ω). Table 4 confirms building weight for Class 2 is near-zero (−0.03), so diminishing built mass carries minimal perceptual cost while liberating sky area.

Reshaping foreground tree canopies (e.g., crown-raising, selective thinning) converts dense opaque canopy into a semi-transparent layer that permits sky pixels to register while retaining vegetation pixels. This balances SVF gain with the strong positive vegetation weight (3.34) for Class 2, preserving the calm/greenery perception channel.

Removing minor overhead built obstructions (awnings, utility cables, large signage) in the foreground eliminates small but cumulative sky-blocking elements, incrementally raising SVF without altering the dominant streetscape character. The negative car weight (−2.02) for Class 2 also supports de-cluttering the visual field of non-natural objects.

scope.description: SCP_S2 street-corridor scale: SIG_MOD_BLT_MG_SIZ + SIG_MOD_VEG_FG_SHP + SIG_REM_BLT_FG_SIZ collectively reshape the cross-sectional profile of the streetscape as captured in street-view imagery. Evidence SVCs_P_Ogawa2024_2 links IND_SVF to PRF_AES via PRS_AES_NATU for the Calm/Greenery perception class across residential and commercial streetscapes in Tokyo.
scope_validation_notes: Scope structure is internally consistent with signature_count and phase_count.

pathway.mechanism_description: Modifying the balance of built-environment and vegetation elements visible in streetscapes alters the subjective perception scores predicted by deep-learning models trained on pairwise human comparisons. Specifically, reducing the visual dominance of monotonous built surfaces while increasing vegetation presence shifts the predicted perception toward higher scores on dimensions such as openness, attractiveness, and pleasantness.

translation_signature_schema.signature_order: Operation × Semantic Layer × Spatial Layer × Morphological Layer

performance_effect.expected_change_note: Increase Subjective perception score for Class 2 (Calm/Greenery)

source.citation: Ogawa, Y., Oki, T., Zhao, C., Sekimoto, Y., & Shimizu, C. (2024). Evaluating the subjective perceptions of streetscapes using street-view images. Landscape and Urban Planning, 247, 105073.

operation.description: Increase street-level visible greenery through addition and enhancement of vegetation along streets and slopes in hilly neighborhoods, targeting eye-level canopy coverage within an 800 m residential buffer. The intervention maximises the green pixel proportion captured in panoramic street-view imagery (GVI), which in steep-slope contexts was positively associated with vigorous physical activity.

signatures.mechanism: Adding new street trees and shrubs within 0-10 m of the pedestrian viewpoint directly increases green pixel count in panoramic street-view images, raising GVI.

Expanding the canopy volume of existing foreground vegetation (pruning for spread, species replacement for denser foliage) enlarges the green pixel area per tree, increasing GVI without new planting sites.

Positioning vegetation at eye-level (1.2-2.5 m height bands, e.g., hedges, green walls, raised planters) ensures green pixels occupy the central visual field of street-view captures, disproportionately boosting GVI relative to canopy-only strategies.

In hilly terrain, exposed slopes at 10-50 m distance are highly visible in street-view panoramas. Adding slope vegetation (ground-cover, terraced planting) converts bare or built pixels to green, contributing to GVI across steeper contexts specific to Group 3.
Reducing foreground impervious or built elements (walls, fences, parking surfaces) lowers the non-green pixel denominator and frees planting space, indirectly increasing the GVI ratio.

scope.description: SCP_S2 neighbourhood scale (800 m residential buffer) based on SVCs_P_Sun2022_1; signatures SIG_ADD_VEG_FG_SIZ through SIG_REM_BLT_FG_SIZ collectively target IND_GVI within PRF_USE / PRS_PHY_ACTV dimension.
scope_validation_notes: Scope structure is internally consistent with signature_count and phase_count.

pathway.mechanism_description: Higher eye-level GVI in steep-slope neighbourhoods is positively associated with vigorous physical activity in the 800 m residential buffer; visible greenery may make slopes more attractive for walking and exercise, but the broader mediation to self-reported health is not directly tested in this evidence.

translation_signature_schema.signature_order: Operation × Semantic Layer × Spatial Layer × Morphological Layer

performance_effect.expected_change_note: Increase Vigorous Physical Activity (VPA) in Steep Slope Group (Group 3, 800m buffer)

source.citation: Sun, P., Song, Y., & Lu, W. (2022). Effect of Urban Green Space in the Hilly Environment on Physical Activity and Health Outcomes: Mediation Analysis on Multiple Greenery Measures. Land, 11(5), 612.

operation.description: Increase eye-level street greenery by adding and enlarging vegetation elements along neighborhood streets in the foreground and midground tiers. The intervention targets the green pixel ratio captured by street-level imagery, directly raising the Green View Index to promote walking time among older adults.

signatures.mechanism: Introducing new vegetation elements (street trees, tall shrubs, hedge rows) within 0-10 m of the pedestrian viewpoint directly increases the count of green pixels in eye-level panoramic imagery, raising GVI.

Positioning new and existing vegetation so that canopy and foliage occupy the eye-level visual band (1.2-2.5 m height) maximises green pixel capture in the four cardinal street-view images used to compute GVI.
Enlarging the canopy spread and foliage volume of existing foreground vegetation increases the spatial extent of green pixels per viewpoint, amplifying GVI without requiring new planting sites.

Adding tree rows and green buffers in the 10-50 m midground fills background gaps between foreground elements, contributing additional green pixels and raising the cumulative GVI across the neighbourhood buffer zone.

Selecting species with denser, year-round foliage and layered leaf textures increases the proportion of pixels classified as green within each vegetation element, improving GVI especially in subtropical contexts like Hong Kong.

scope.description: SCP_S2 street/corridor scale — SIG_ADD_VEG_FG_SIZ and SIG_MOD_VEG_FG_POS applied along neighbourhood street segments within residential buffer zones, as referenced in SVCs_P_Zang2020_1 where IND_GVI was averaged across all sample points in each community buffer to predict PRF_USE walking outcomes.
scope_validation_notes: Scope structure is internally consistent with signature_count and phase_count.

pathway.mechanism_description: Increasing street-level vegetation coverage (trees and shrubs) along neighbourhood streets enhances the proportion of green pixels captured in street-view imagery within residential buffers, thereby raising the Green View Index. In the context of compact high-density urban environments, higher eye-level greenness along walking routes is associated with increased total walking time among older adults, as greener streetscapes provide more pleasant and stimulating pedestrian environments.

translation_signature_schema.signature_order: Operation × Semantic Layer × Spatial Layer × Morphological Layer

performance_effect.expected_change_note: Increase Total walking time in the past 7 days (minutes)

source.citation: Zang, P., Liu, X., Zhao, Y., Guo, H., Lu, Y., & Xue, C. Q. L. (2020). Eye-Level Street Greenery and Walking Behaviors of Older Adults. International Journal of Environmental Research and Public Health, 17(17), 6130.

operation.description: Reduce Sky View Factor along road corridors by introducing large-canopy street trees and strategically positioned overhead vegetation structures in the foreground tier, thereby screening direct and diffuse solar radiation from reaching road surfaces. The intervention targets the weighted sky-pixel ratio in the SVF formula by replacing visible sky portions with vegetative canopy, lowering road surface temperatures in hot-humid contexts.

signatures.subtype: large-canopy street trees (e.g., broad-crowned deciduous or evergreen species)
signatures.mechanism: Tree canopy occupies sky-hemisphere solid angles, directly replacing sky(i) pixels with vegetation pixels in the SVF summation, reducing the weighted sky ratio. Larger canopy spread → greater angular obstruction → lower SVF → reduced shortwave irradiance on road surface → lower RST.

signatures.subtype: horizontal canopy spread / umbrella-form crown architecture
signatures.mechanism: Wider, flatter canopy shapes maximize the horizontal projection of sky obstruction per unit biomass, increasing the angular coverage ω·sky(i) displacement at zenith and near-zenith angles where solar irradiance contribution to RST is greatest in low-latitude hot-humid cities.

signatures.subtype: bilateral / staggered tree rows along road edges
signatures.mechanism: Strategic positioning of trees on both sides of the road corridor creates overlapping screening cones that block solar access across the full road width, reducing sunlit hours on pavement. Bilateral placement converts the road cross-section from open-sky to quasi-canyon geometry, lowering SVF symmetrically across measurement points.

signatures.subtype: secondary tree canopy layer / tall understory vegetation buffer
signatures.mechanism: Mid-ground vegetation contributes to SVF reduction at lower elevation angles of the sky hemisphere, complementing foreground canopy by filling angular gaps between FG tree crowns. This is especially relevant at oblique viewing angles where the fish-eye projection captures distant canopy mass, further depressing the cumulative sky ratio.

While not directly altering SVF, modifying ground surface texture and material properties synergistically reduces RST under the remaining (non-screened) sky exposure. The paper notes water-retaining pavements can reduce RST by ~10°C (Higashiyama et al., 2016), complementing the SVF-mediated radiative pathway with an evaporative cooling co-benefit.

scope.description: SCP_S2 street-corridor scale intervention combining SIG_ADD_VEG_FG_SIZ, SIG_MOD_VEG_FG_SHP, SIG_ADD_VEG_FG_POS, SIG_ADD_VEG_MG_SIZ, and SIG_MOD_GRD_FG_TEX to reduce IND_SVF (r = 0.92 with RST per SVCs_P_Zeng2023_1) in the PRF_THR thermal comfort dimension, targeting PRS_THR_TEMP road surface temperature reduction in hot-humid megacity contexts.
scope_validation_notes: Scope structure is internally consistent with signature_count and phase_count.

pathway.mechanism_description: Higher Sky View Factor along road corridors is positively correlated with Road Surface Temperature, because greater sky exposure admits more direct and diffuse solar radiation; canopy interventions that reduce SVF (i.e., increase canopy view) are therefore associated with cooler road surfaces, especially in east-west street canyons during summer.

translation_signature_schema.signature_order: Operation × Semantic Layer × Spatial Layer × Morphological Layer

performance_effect.expected_change_note: Decrease Road Surface Temperature (RST)

source.citation: Zeng, L., Lindberg, F., Zhang, X., Pan, H., & Lu, J. (2023). Road surface temperature evaluated with streetview-derived parameters in a hot and humid megacity. Urban Climate, 51, 101585.

operation.description: Strategically add and position multi-layered vegetation in foreground and midground tiers within park settings to increase the Green View Index (green pixel ratio), while incorporating visible water features and screening built elements, to enhance perceived psychological restoration. Vegetation form and placement should balance greenery coverage with visual penetration to avoid excessive visual enclosure, as cautioned by the study's finding that excessive greenery can reduce spatial legibility.

signatures.mechanism: Adding foreground vegetation directly increases green pixel count in the panoramic visual field, raising GVI; greenery validated as significant global predictor of restoration (Model-2: β_std=0.044, p=0.003)

Midground vegetation extends green pixel coverage across depth of field, increasing cumulative GVI and reinforcing spatial continuity of greenery perceived from viewpoints; high spatial autocorrelation of greenery (Moran's I=0.382) confirms clustered distribution supports coherent green coverage

Repositioning vegetation to maximize green pixel coverage along key viewsheds (e.g., entrance square axis noted in excerpt [4]) while preserving visual penetration; optimizes GVI distribution without suppressing spatial openness referenced in excerpt [7]

Water visibility validated as significant positive predictor of restoration (Model-2: β_std=0.032, p=0.025); adding or revealing water in midground complements greenery by engaging innate affective responses aligned with Stress Recovery Theory (SRT, Ulrich 1983)

Building pixel share negatively correlates with restoration (r=−0.217, p<0.01); reducing built-element visibility via vegetative screening simultaneously raises GVI green pixel ratio and removes restorative detractors from the visual field

Modifying vegetation canopy shape and density balances green pixel coverage with visual penetration; prevents excessive greenery from reducing spatial legibility and ensures GVI gains do not compromise the non-significant but design-relevant sky openness (Sky: p=0.275 in Model-1)

scope.description: Site-scale (SCP_S2) park intervention linking SIG_ADD_VEG_FG_SIZ, SIG_ADD_VEG_MG_SIZ, SIG_MOD_VEG_FG_POS, SIG_ADD_WAT_MG_SIZ, SIG_REM_BLT_FG_SIZ, and SIG_MOD_VEG_FG_SHP to evidence SVCs_P_Li2024_1; targets IND_GVI through increased vegetation and water pixel share in panoramic imagery to support PRF_EMO via PRS_MNT_REST
scope_validation_notes: Scope structure is internally consistent with signature_count and phase_count.

pathway.mechanism_description: Higher GVI in streetscapes is positively correlated with Visual Openness (Sky View Index) and Color Richness across the study area; greater visible vegetation tends to co-occur with more open, chromatically richer streetscapes, but the directional causal pathway is not tested.

translation_signature_schema.signature_order: Operation × Semantic Layer × Spatial Layer × Morphological Layer

performance_effect.expected_change_note: Increase Visual openness (Sky View Index) and Color Richness

source.citation: Li, X., Shang, W.-L., Liu, Q., Liu, X., Lyu, Z., & Ochieng, W. (2024). Towards a sustainable city: Deciphering the determinants of restorative park and spatial patterns. Sustainable Cities and Society, 104, 105292.

operation.description: Increase the proportion of green pixels captured in eye-level street-view imagery by adding or enlarging visible vegetation within the camera's field of view. This primarily involves introducing canopy and shrub mass in the foreground and mid-ground tiers so that green pixels displace non-green pixels (built surfaces, ground, sky) in the segmented image, thereby raising the GVI ratio.

signatures.mechanism: Adding new vegetation mass (tree canopy, hedges, climbing plants) within 0-10 m of the viewpoint maximises green-pixel coverage due to angular proximity; each added unit of leaf area occupies a disproportionately large share of the image frame, directly increasing Sum(Green_Pixels)/Sum(Total_Pixels).

Vegetation added at 10-50 m (the interval at which Mapillary sample points are spaced, per excerpt [25]) fills the visual depth of the streetscape, contributing additional green pixels in the upper and lateral portions of the image where sky or façades would otherwise dominate.

Modifying crown shape (e.g., selecting broad-canopy species or allowing lateral growth instead of pollarding) widens the silhouette of existing vegetation against the image frame, converting more pixels from sky/built to green without necessarily adding new planting locations.

Repositioning vegetation vertically (e.g., adding understory planting, green walls, or lowering branch clearance) shifts green mass into the central band of the street-view image where the segmentation algorithm registers eye-level greenness, improving GVI even if total biomass is unchanged.

Increasing foliage density (species with denser leaf packing, evergreen rather than deciduous selection) reduces see-through gaps in the canopy, converting mixed sky-green pixels to solid green pixels and raising the green-pixel count within the same crown envelope.

Removing or reducing built elements (walls, signage, overhead structures) that occlude vegetation in the camera's line of sight reveals existing green mass that was previously hidden, increasing green-pixel share without new planting.

scope.description: Coordinated (SCP_S2) intervention scope: each GVI value is computed at a discrete street-view sampling point (50 m intervals, excerpt [25]) from a single eye-level camera perspective. Signatures SIG_ADD_VEG_FG_SIZ and SIG_ADD_VEG_MG_SIZ capture the dominant physical changes that shift green-pixel ratio (IND_GVI) toward higher aesthetic naturalness (PRS_AES_NATU) as recorded in SVCs_P_Sanchez2024_1.
scope_validation_notes: Scope structure is internally consistent with signature_count and phase_count.

pathway.mechanism_description: At discrete street-view sampling points, increasing the proportion of visible vegetation (trees, shrubs, grass) in the surrounding streetscape raises the Green View Index computed from semantically segmented open-source street-view imagery. The methodology uses Mapillary images with satellite NDVI supplementation at locations lacking street-view coverage, enabling scalable citywide assessment of eye-level greenness visibility.

translation_signature_schema.signature_order: Operation × Semantic Layer × Spatial Layer × Morphological Layer

performance_effect.expected_change_note: Descriptive evidence only; directional effect on Green View Index derived from Mapillary images is not established.
