operation.description: Operation paired with evidence SVCs_P_Li2018_1: modulate IND_SVF to influence Mean reduction in sky view factor attributable to street trees, computed as SVF_diff = SVF_S (building-height-model simulation, buildings only) - SVF_P (GSV-based photographic SVF, all obstructions)

pathway.mechanism_description: Lower Sky View Factor at GSV panorama sites in downtown Boston corresponds to greater obstruction by street trees: replacing the building-only simulated SVF (SVF_S) with the photographic SVF (SVF_P) yields a mean reduction of ~18.52%, attributable specifically to tree canopy interception of sky-pixels. This SVF difference quantifies the shade provision of street trees.

operation.description: Operation paired with evidence SVCs_P_Li2018_NEW_2: modulate IND_TVF to influence SVF difference (SVF_S - SVF_P) at site level, used as a proxy for shade provision by street trees

pathway.mechanism_description: Higher Tree View Factor at a site is positively correlated with the SVF difference (SVF_S - SVF_P) across 20-100 m buffers, because greater canopy presence in the photographic image translates into more sky-pixels occluded by trees; this difference serves as a proxy for shade provision by street trees.

operation.description: Operation paired with evidence SVCs_P_Li2018_NEW_3: modulate IND_TVF to influence SVF difference (SVF_S - SVF_P), proxy for shade provision by street trees

pathway.mechanism_description: Taller average canopy heights (ACH from LiDAR-derived nDSM) in the surrounding buffer increase the cross-sectional area of sky-pixels they can occlude, raising the SVF difference (SVF_S - SVF_P) and thereby providing more potential shade in the street canyon.

operation.description: Operation paired with evidence SVCs_P_Li2018_NEW_4: modulate IND_BVI to influence SVF difference (SVF_S - SVF_P), proxy for shade provision by street trees

pathway.mechanism_description: Higher Building View Index / average building height in the surrounding buffer reduces the SVF difference attributable to trees, because tall buildings already occlude much of the sky that street trees would otherwise shade; this negative association reflects shadow competition between buildings and trees.

operation.description: Operation paired with evidence SVCs_P_Li2018_NEW_5: modulate IND_SVF to influence Agreement between SVF_P (GSV photographic) and SVF_S (building-height simulation) at sites with no tree canopy within 30 m, used to validate the GSV-based method

pathway.mechanism_description: At sites with no surrounding tree canopy within 30 m, the photographic SVF (SVF_P) and the building-only simulated SVF (SVF_S) are tightly correlated, validating the GSV-based photographic method as a measurement of SVF in the absence of canopy obstruction; this provides the methodological basis for attributing any SVF_S-SVF_P difference at treed sites to street trees.

operation.description: Operation paired with evidence SVCs_P_Zhao2024_NEW1: modulate IND_GVI to influence Place Pulse 2.0 'beautiful' perception score (0-9 ten-class) predicted by SegFormer-B5 + ConvNeXt-B + RF

pathway.mechanism_description: Higher Green View Index / vegetation proportion in a street view image makes a positive predictive contribution (via SHAP) to the SegFormer-B5 + ConvNeXt-B + RF model's predicted Place Pulse 2.0 'beautiful' score; this reflects the learned association in human-perception training data between visible vegetation and aesthetic ratings, not a tested causal effect.

operation.description: Increase the proportion and coverage of visible vegetation elements in urban streetscapes to enhance Green View Index (GVI) across different spatial contexts.

pathway.mechanism_description: Higher GVI improves visual environmental quality by increasing natural elements in the field of view, which positively correlates with perceived safety (visibility and natural surveillance), wealth (environmental quality and maintenance), liveliness (biodiversity and seasonal variation), and beauty (aesthetic preference for nature) in urban spaces. The spatio-temporal evolution of GVI directly reflects changes in composite perceptual scores.

signatures.mechanism: Introducing vegetation elements (predominantly canopy trees) into the streetscape increases the proportion of green pixels captured in pedestrian-level street view imagery, directly raising the Green View Index (GVI). Higher GVI corresponds to a greater share of the visual field occupied by greenery, which the evidence links to improved perceived safety, wealth, liveliness, and beauty of the urban scene.

scope.description: SCP_S1 encodes a single operation: SIG_ADD_VEG_MG_SIZ. Vegetation (canopy trees) is added to the streetscape to increase the amount (VAR_SIZ) of visible greenery at the midground social-field tier. According to linked evidence SVCs_P_Liang2023_3, this raises the Green View Index (IND_GVI), which exhibits a positive relationship (DIR_POS) with the aesthetic-preference performance dimension (PRF_AES / PRS_AES_QUAL). Descriptive effect sizes for composite perception scores are Safety (0.28), Wealthy (0.32), Lively (0.23), and Beautiful (0.30), confirming that greater vegetation coverage consistently improves subjective streetscape quality across all four measures.
scope_validation_notes: Scope structure is internally consistent with signature_count and phase_count.

translation_signature_schema.signature_order: Operation × Semantic Layer × Spatial Layer × Morphological Layer

performance_effect.expected_change_note: May increase Composite scores: Safety, Wealthy, Lively, Beautiful

operation.description: Operation paired with evidence SVCs_P_Zhao2024_NEW5: modulate IND_HPS to influence Average prediction ACC across six Place Pulse 2.0 perceptions (beautiful, boring, depressing, lively, safe, wealthy)

pathway.mechanism_description: The ML-predicted Human Perception Score across the six Place Pulse 2.0 dimensions attains an average prediction accuracy of 78.56% on the Place Pulse 2.0 dataset; this descriptive performance metric characterizes the predictive utility of the SegFormer-B5 + ConvNeXt-B + RF pipeline and does not imply a directional or causal relationship.

operation.description: Increase the proportion of vegetation pixels visible in street-level views by adding and enlarging multi-layer planting (canopy trees, understory shrubs, ground cover) along street corridors in both foreground and midground tiers. Strategic positioning and dense canopy form maximize the vegetation pixel ratio captured in street view imagery, thereby improving perceived visual comfort.

signatures.mechanism: Adding new vegetation elements (street trees, hedge rows, planter beds) within 0-10 m of the viewpoint directly increases the count of green pixels in the street view image, raising VEG = (Pixels_Veg / Total_Pixels) × 100. Foreground vegetation occupies the largest angular subtense and therefore contributes the most pixels per unit of real canopy area.

Adding large-canopy trees and continuous green corridors in the 10-50 m midground fills the upper and middle portions of the street view image with vegetation pixels, complementing foreground planting and boosting the overall vegetation index. Midground vegetation also partially occludes building facades, reducing the negative 'building' pixel fraction identified in the study.

Repositioning vegetation into multi-layer arrangements (canopy + understory + ground cover) and staggering planting along both sides of the street maximises the overlap of green surfaces across vertical bands of the panoramic image, ensuring vegetation pixels span low, middle, and upper zones of the visual field and increasing the total VEG ratio beyond what single-layer planting achieves.

Selecting and pruning tree species for broad, spreading canopy forms rather than narrow columnar forms increases the projected pixel footprint of each individual tree in the street view image. A wider canopy shape converts more of the sky and building pixel area into vegetation pixels, raising the VEG index per planted unit.

Increasing foliage density and selecting species with fine, continuous leaf texture reduces sky-through gaps in the canopy. The semantic segmentation algorithm classifies dense foliage patches more reliably as 'vegetation' pixels; sparse canopies with visible sky gaps are partially classified as sky, lowering the effective VEG ratio.

scope.description: Street-corridor scale (SCP_S2) intervention inferred from SVCs_P_Shao2023_1. Signatures SIG_ADD_VEG_FG_SIZ and SIG_ADD_VEG_MG_SIZ act as primary drivers of IND_GVI within PRF_AES / PRS_AES_CMFT, supported by positional layering (SIG_MOD_VEG_FG_POS), canopy form optimisation (SIG_MOD_VEG_FG_SHP), and foliage density enhancement (SIG_MOD_VEG_FG_TEX). Evidence reports consistent positive correlations (R = 0.723–0.782, p < 0.01) across Beijing, Shanghai, and Shenzhen.
scope_validation_notes: Scope structure is internally consistent with signature_count and phase_count.

pathway.mechanism_description: Increasing the proportion of visible vegetation in urban streetscapes, as measured through semantic segmentation of street-view images, directly raises the Green View Index. Across four Chinese megacities, streets with higher GVI and balanced spatial enclosure receive higher visual comfort ratings from survey respondents, with the relationship varying by street-type cluster and city context.

translation_signature_schema.signature_order: Operation × Semantic Layer × Spatial Layer × Morphological Layer

performance_effect.expected_change_note: Increase Perceived Visual Comfort Score (1-5 Likert)

source.citation: Shao, Y., Yin, Y., Xue, Z., & Ma, D. (2023). Assessing and Comparing the Visual Comfort of Streets across Four Chinese Megacities Using AI-Based Image Analysis and the Perceptive Evaluation Method. Land, 12(4), 834.

operation.description: Increase the visible proportion of ground-level terrain surfaces in the streetscape by expanding and improving ground-plane areas (sidewalks, unpaved surfaces, open ground) in the foreground and midground, while moderating the visual dominance of buildings that occlude terrain. This compositional shift raises the Terrain Ratio, which is positively correlated with perceived visual comfort across multiple Chinese megacities.

signatures.mechanism: Widening or extending ground-level surfaces (sidewalks, plazas, unpaved terrain) increases terrain pixel count in the foreground, directly raising IND_TER_RAT.
Adding visible ground-plane area in the midground (e.g., setback plazas, exposed terrain strips) extends the terrain depth in the image, contributing additional terrain pixels to the total frame.

Modifying ground surface materiality (e.g., permeable paving, natural stone, exposed earth) ensures segmentation algorithms consistently classify these pixels as 'terrain' rather than 'road', reinforcing the ratio while improving perceived surface quality.
Setting back or stepping down building facades reduces building pixel dominance in the lower-to-mid frame, revealing more ground surface behind and increasing the terrain-to-total pixel ratio.

pathway.mechanism_description: The visible Terrain Ratio (ground-plane surfaces) is descriptively associated with Perceived Visual Comfort Scores; balanced ground-plane composition combined with appropriate enclosure may relate to comfort perception, but no directional or causal claim is established.

This compositional shift raises the Terrain Ratio, which is positively correlated with perceived visual comfort across multiple Chinese megacities.

operation.description: Increase the coverage area and density of vegetation (trees and grass) in public open spaces through strategic planting to enhance visual greenness in street-level views

pathway.mechanism_description: Higher Total Vegetation Index is positively associated with Perceived Environment Score in predictive models, consistent with biophilic hypotheses; the mechanism is hypothesized rather than directly tested.

signatures.mechanism: Strategic planting of trees increases the canopy-layer pixel share in street-level views, directly raising the green-coverage component of the Total Vegetation Index (ss_tree = 1.797)
Strategic planting of grass increases the ground-layer green pixel share in street-level views, contributing an additional vegetation-index uplift (ss_grass = 1.596)

scope.description: SIG_ADD_VEG_MG_SIZ (canopy_tree) and SIG_ADD_VEG_MG_SIZ (grass) form a vertical superposition: canopy trees are layered above a ground-cover grass plane, together maximising the pixel-ratio green coverage captured in street-level panoramic imagery. Linked evidence SVCs_P_Chen2023_1 reports the indicator Total Vegetation Index (IND_GVI), composed of SVI-derived green coverage and NDVI, within the performance dimension PRF_AES / PRS_AES_NATU (Aesthetic Preference – Naturalness). Both vegetation layers show a positive relationship (DIR_POS) with the Perceived Environment Score, with canopy trees contributing a slightly larger standardised effect (1.797) than grass (1.596). Coordinating the two subtypes addresses both the upper and lower portions of the visual field, amplifying overall visual greenness beyond what either layer alone would achieve.
scope_validation_notes: Scope structure is internally consistent with signature_count and phase_count.

translation_signature_schema.signature_order: Operation × Semantic Layer × Spatial Layer × Morphological Layer

performance_effect.expected_change_note: Increase Perceived Environment Score

operation.description: Modify the texture and visual complexity of vegetation in the middleground of urban street environments to enhance spatial quality perception through balanced greenery distribution and visual richness.

pathway.mechanism_description: Mid-ground vegetation texture and visual complexity may contribute to the composite Street Spatial Quality Score through a positive weighting, but the linked evidence only specifies weight assignments and does not establish a causal pathway.

signatures.mechanism: Modifying the textural richness and visual complexity of middleground vegetation raises Visual Entropy (VEI, weight 7.798) — the dominant quality determinant — and supports Green Visual Index (GVI, weight 1.507), together lifting the composite Street Quality Index through diversified foliage patterns and balanced greenery distribution.

scope.description: SIG_MOD_VEG_MG_TEX represents a single-signature intervention that adjusts the texture and pattern complexity of middleground vegetation. According to linked evidence SVCs_P_Jin2023_2, the Street Quality Index (IND_SQI) is most strongly driven by the Visual Entropy Index (VEI = 7.798), which directly captures the visual richness and diversity of scene patterns, alongside the Green Visual Index (GVI = 1.507), which registers the proportion of visible tree canopy. Both indicators respond positively when vegetative texture is enriched through species variety, layered foliage, and canopy articulation. The negative Motorization Visual Index weight (MVI = −2.412) further contextualises the operation: enhancing vegetative textural presence counterbalances hard-surface monotony. The performance dimension is Aesthetic Preference (PRF_AES, subdimension PRS_AES_QUAL) with an expected positive direction (DIR_POS), meaning that increasing the textural complexity of street vegetation measurably improves perceived spatial quality scores.
scope_validation_notes: Scope structure is internally consistent with signature_count and phase_count.

translation_signature_schema.signature_order: Operation × Semantic Layer × Spatial Layer × Morphological Layer

performance_effect.expected_change_note: May increase Calculated Street Spatial Quality Score

operation.description: Increase the amount and coverage of visible vegetation in the middleground view to enhance Green View Index in neighborhoods across different income groups.

pathway.mechanism_description: GVI levels are descriptively compared across high- and low-income neighbourhoods; differences in eye-level greenery may relate to socioeconomic patterns of green-space provision, but no directional or causal claim about restoration outcomes is asserted.

signatures.mechanism: Adding vegetation volume and canopy coverage in the middleground (10–50 m) increases the proportion of green pixels captured in street-level imagery, directly raising the Green View Index (GVI). Greater visible green coverage at neighborhood scale addresses the GVI disparity observed between high-income (mean GVI = 12.97) and low-income (mean GVI = 11.71) apartment neighborhoods.

scope.description: SCP_S1 comprises a single signature SIG_ADD_VEG_MG_SIZ. The intervention increases the amount and spatial coverage of middleground vegetation to enhance the Green View Index (IND_GVI) as documented in evidence SVCs_P_Ki2021_2. The linked evidence reports a descriptive gap in GVI between high-income (mean = 12.97) and low-income (mean = 11.71) apartment neighborhoods, indicating that lower-income areas have less visible street-level greenery. By adding canopy trees in the 10–50 m viewing zone of underserved neighborhoods, the intervention raises GVI toward parity. The performance dimension is PRF_SOC (Social Outcomes), subdimension PRS_SOC_DEMO (demographic equity). The expected outcome direction is an upward shift in GVI, particularly in low-income neighborhoods, narrowing the income-based greenery gap. The relationship direction is DIR_NA (descriptive comparison), so the signature targets equalising the observed disparity rather than following a confirmed causal pathway.
scope_validation_notes: Scope structure is internally consistent with signature_count and phase_count.

translation_signature_schema.signature_order: Operation × Semantic Layer × Spatial Layer × Morphological Layer

performance_effect.expected_change_note: Descriptive evidence only; directional effect on Income Group (High vs Low) is not established.

operation.description: Modify walkway shape and spatial configuration to influence pedestrian trajectory patterns and movement behavior in urban green spaces

pathway.mechanism_description: Walkway shape modifications may alter pedestrian trajectory patterns; the linked descriptive evidence notes only that trajectory features support gender prediction in association with Visual Beauty Scores, without a directional or causal claim.

signatures.mechanism: Modifying the walkway's planimetric shape and routing geometry (e.g., curvature, branching, width transitions) reconfigures pedestrian trajectory patterns, altering spatial coverage, walking speed, stop frequency, and viewing distances as users navigate the path network through the green space.

scope.description: SIG_MOD_GRD_MG_SHP encodes the modification of walkway shape and spatial configuration within urban green spaces. Evidence SVCs_P_Zhu2024_2 links this ground-surface manipulation to the Visual Beauty Score indicator (IND_BEA_VIS) under the Aesthetic Preference dimension (PRF_AES / PRS_AES_PREF). The study demonstrates that streetscape spatial characteristics—including walkway layout—shape divergent trajectory behaviours: males exhibited higher walking speeds (1.2 m/s), larger spatial coverage, and preference for central, open areas, while females showed more frequent stops, shorter viewing distances, and southeast-biased enclosure-oriented routes (gender prediction accuracy 78.3%). Because the relationship direction is DIR_NA and the outcome is descriptive rather than directional, the intervention does not predict a monotonic improvement in the aesthetic indicator but rather reveals that walkway shape mediates differentiated perceptual-behavioural responses. Designers can leverage SIG_MOD_GRD_MG_SHP to calibrate path geometry toward inclusive movement patterns that support varied aesthetic engagement with the streetscape.
scope_validation_notes: Scope structure is internally consistent with signature_count and phase_count.

translation_signature_schema.signature_order: Operation × Semantic Layer × Spatial Layer × Morphological Layer

performance_effect.expected_change_note: Descriptive evidence only; directional effect on Gender prediction accuracy from trajectory is not established.

operation.description: Increase streetside vegetation canopy volume and lateral spread along roadways to raise the proportion of green pixels captured in pedestrian-level street view imagery. This primarily entails adding or enlarging tree and shrub plantings in the foreground and midground tiers of the streetscape, thereby directly increasing the Green View Index across different road types.

signatures.mechanism: Adding foreground vegetation (0-10 m from viewpoint) directly increases the count of green pixels relative to total pixels in panoramic street view imagery, raising GVI. Street trees and hedgerows at the roadside dominate the near-field visual proportion.

Adding midground vegetation (10-50 m) along medians, setbacks, and secondary planting strips contributes additional green pixels in the middle depth plane of the street view panorama, supplementing the foreground canopy contribution to GVI.

Narrower road cross-sections reduce the proportion of non-green (paved/built) pixels and shorten the distance to roadside vegetation, enlarging the apparent angular size of tree canopies in the view. The negative correlation between road width and GVI (e.g., T1 r = −0.332**, T4 r = −0.207**) confirms this mediating role.
Repositioning vegetation closer to the pedestrian viewpoint increases its projected pixel area in the hemispherical/panoramic image, boosting GVI without requiring additional planting volume.

scope.description: This SCP_S2 superposition record coordinates 4 signatures linked to evidence SVCs_P_Dong2018_2. The original entry had no explicit phase assignments, so it is normalized as a concurrent multi-signature intervention rather than a phased SCP_S3 programme.
scope_validation_notes: Scope structure is internally consistent with signature_count and phase_count.

pathway.mechanism_description: Planting additional street trees along major urban roads increases the proportion of green vegetation pixels visible in street-view images sampled at regular intervals along the road network. By aggregating the Green View Index across road segments and administrative districts, the intervention produces a measurable citywide increase in pedestrian-level green visibility, with the effect varying by road type and district-level urbanisation intensity.

translation_signature_schema.signature_order: Operation × Semantic Layer × Spatial Layer × Morphological Layer

performance_effect.expected_change_note: Descriptive evidence only; directional effect on Comparison of GVI across road types is not established.

operation.description: Increase the size and quantity of street-level vegetation elements in urban streetscapes through systematic addition of trees, shrubs, and vertical greenery to enhance foreground green visibility from pedestrian eye-level perspective.

pathway.mechanism_description: Addition of foreground vegetation directly increases the proportion of green elements visible in the immediate viewshed, thereby improving Green Visibility Index (GVI) measurements captured at street level. The temporal increase in overall GVI observed in Wuhan's central urban area reflects cumulative effects of expanding vegetation coverage in pedestrian sight lines.

signatures.mechanism: Planting street trees along roads forms continuous tree-lined avenues whose canopies dominate the green-pixel proportion in street-view imagery, producing the largest single contribution to GVI increase.
Adding understory shrubs fills the lower visual band of pedestrian eye-level imagery, supplementing canopy coverage and increasing the cumulative green-pixel share captured by GVI.

Introducing vertical greenery on walls and facades converts otherwise hard-surface pixels into green pixels in street-view imagery, extending green visibility into portions of the visual field that canopy trees and shrubs cannot reach.

scope.description: A coordinated S2 superposition operation in which three vegetation layers—SIG_ADD_VEG_FG_SIZ (canopy_tree), SIG_ADD_VEG_FG_SIZ (shrub), and SIG_ADD_VEG_FG_SIZ (climbing_plant)—are simultaneously introduced within the foreground streetscape zone to collectively raise the Green View Index (IND_GVI) referenced in evidence SVCs_P_Guo2025_1. The linked evidence documents a GVI increase from 17.49 % to 21.67 % (+4.18 percentage points) over the 2013–2019 period in Wuhan's central urban area. Each signature contributes additively to the green-pixel proportion captured in eye-level street-view imagery: canopy trees supply the dominant overhead and lateral green mass, shrubs fill the lower visual band, and climbing plants convert vertical hard surfaces into green coverage. The combined intervention addresses the performance dimension PRF_SOC (Social Outcomes), sub-dimension PRS_SOC_EQTY, by raising baseline green visibility across previously under-served residential neighbourhoods and thereby narrowing spatial inequity in residents' exposure to street-level greenery.
scope_validation_notes: Scope structure is internally consistent with signature_count and phase_count.

source.citation: Guo, X., Liu, C., Bi, S., & Tang, Y. (2025). Identification of Inequities in Green Visibility and Ways to Increase Greenery in Neighborhoods: A Case Study of Wuhan, China. Applied Sciences, 15(2), 742.

operation.description: Increase the size and quantity of street-level vegetation elements in urban streetscapes through systematic addition of trees, shrubs, and vertical greenery to enhance foreground green visibility from pedestrian eye-level perspective.

pathway.mechanism_description: Addition of foreground vegetation directly increases the proportion of green elements visible in the immediate viewshed, thereby improving Green Visibility Index (GVI) measurements captured at street level. The temporal increase in overall GVI observed in Wuhan's central urban area reflects cumulative effects of expanding vegetation coverage in pedestrian sight lines.

signatures.mechanism: Planting street trees along roads forms continuous tree-lined avenues whose canopies dominate the green-pixel proportion in street-view imagery, producing the largest single contribution to GVI increase.
Adding understory shrubs fills the lower visual band of pedestrian eye-level imagery, supplementing canopy coverage and increasing the cumulative green-pixel share captured by GVI.

Introducing vertical greenery on walls and facades converts otherwise hard-surface pixels into green pixels in street-view imagery, extending green visibility into portions of the visual field that canopy trees and shrubs cannot reach.

scope.description: A coordinated S2 superposition operation in which three vegetation layers—SIG_ADD_VEG_FG_SIZ (canopy_tree), SIG_ADD_VEG_FG_SIZ (shrub), and SIG_ADD_VEG_FG_SIZ (climbing_plant)—are simultaneously introduced within the foreground streetscape zone to collectively raise the Green View Index (IND_GVI) referenced in evidence SVCs_P_Guo2025_1. The linked evidence documents a GVI increase from 17.49 % to 21.67 % (+4.18 percentage points) over the 2013–2019 period in Wuhan's central urban area. Each signature contributes additively to the green-pixel proportion captured in eye-level street-view imagery: canopy trees supply the dominant overhead and lateral green mass, shrubs fill the lower visual band, and climbing plants convert vertical hard surfaces into green coverage. The combined intervention addresses the performance dimension PRF_SOC (Social Outcomes), sub-dimension PRS_SOC_EQTY, by raising baseline green visibility across previously under-served residential neighbourhoods and thereby narrowing spatial inequity in residents' exposure to street-level greenery.
scope_validation_notes: Scope structure is internally consistent with signature_count and phase_count.

operation.description: In high GVI streets (>0.6), optimize vegetation design by diversifying plant forms (e.g., columnar, spreading, weeping habits), varying species composition to create layered canopy structures, and introducing seasonal color variations through flowering species and foliage changes, rather than simply increasing vegetation quantity or coverage.

pathway.mechanism_description: In high-GVI streets, monotonous vegetation has been associated with reduced visual interest. Diversifying plant morphology (columnar, spreading, weeping habits), structural layering, and chromatic variation may enhance visual richness and break uniform green dominance. Diverse forms can create rhythmic visual sequences, multi-layered textures add depth, and color accents establish focal points, plausibly supporting visual comfort. Direct evidence for this pathway remains descriptive.

signatures.mechanism: Diversifying plant growth habits (columnar, spreading, weeping) within already high-GVI streets breaks up the homogeneous silhouette of the green mass, introducing formal variety that enriches the visual field and raises perceived visual comfort without increasing the GVI value itself.

Varying species composition to create vertically layered canopy structures (canopy trees over understory shrubs and ground cover) converts a flat, dense green plane into a textured, permeable vegetation profile along the street corridor, counteracting the visual monotony that depresses VICO at high GVI levels.

Introducing flowering species and foliage with seasonal chromatic variation injects colour contrast into the dominant green matrix, disrupting perceptual saturation and providing temporal visual interest that improves visual comfort scores even when green coverage remains constant.

scope.description: This S2 superposition operation coordinates three simultaneous attribute modifications on the same object class (OBJ_VEG) to qualitatively optimize streets that already exceed a GVI of 0.6. SIG_MOD_VEG_FG_SHP diversifies individual plant forms at close range; SIG_MOD_VEG_MG_TEX restructures the vegetation profile into a layered pattern visible along the street corridor; and SIG_MOD_VEG_FG_COL introduces chromatic variety through flowering and foliage seasonality. Linked evidence SVCs_P_Sun2023_3 demonstrates that high-GVI streets (mean VICO = 0.390) perform substantially worse on Visual Comfort (PRF_AES / PRS_AES_CMFT) than low-GVI streets (mean VICO = 0.717), indicating that beyond a threshold, additional green quantity yields diminishing or negative returns. The combined signatures therefore aim to raise the GVI-to-VICO conversion efficiency: the Green View Index (IND_GVI) is held approximately constant while the intervention redirects its composition toward greater formal, textural, and chromatic complexity, thereby improving the aesthetic-comfort outcome that the evidence shows is currently suppressed at high GVI values.
scope_validation_notes: Scope structure is internally consistent with signature_count and phase_count.

operation.description: Counteracting spatio-temporal perceptual decline by increasing street-level vegetation coverage through foreground and midground planting interventions, thereby raising the Green View Index and shifting visual cluster membership away from the perceptually negative 'Urban Jungle' (Cluster 1) profile toward greener, higher-quality perception clusters (Clusters 2 and 4). This is consistent with the study's observation that post-construction greening improvements successfully is associated with changes in visually degraded clusters into perceptually positive ones over successive time periods.

signatures.mechanism: Adding foreground vegetation volume directly increases the green pixel count in panoramic street-level imagery, raising the GVI numerator and shifting pixel-ratio composition away from the built/paved dominance characteristic of Cluster 1 ('Urban Jungle': highest building view factor, lowest vegetation)

Adding midground vegetation extends green pixel coverage into the depth of the visual field, further boosting GVI and approximating the high green-view-factor profile of perceptually positive Cluster 2 ('Flanked by Nature'), while producing the layered greenery balance associated with Cluster 4 ('Balanced Living')

Repositioning vegetation to maximise visual coverage along dominant street-view sightlines increases effective green pixel capture per panoramic image, optimising GVI gains per unit of planting and ensuring seasonal visibility across multi-temporal image acquisition

Modifying visible built-element surfaces with green walls or climbing vegetation reclassifies building-segmented pixels as green-segmented pixels, directly reducing the high building view factor that defines Cluster 1 while simultaneously increasing GVI, compounding both vegetation gain and built-element attenuation in pixel-ratio terms

Removing paved ground surfaces and replacing them with vegetated ground cover reduces the visual pavement component that co-characterises perceptually declining clusters, while converting road/pavement-classified pixels to green-classified pixels, increasing GVI and supporting the compositional shift toward Cluster 4's balanced urban-natural profile

scope.description: SCP_S2 encompasses SIG_ADD_VEG_FG_SIZ (primary), SIG_ADD_VEG_MG_SIZ, SIG_MOD_VEG_FG_POS, SIG_MOD_BLT_FG_SIZ, and SIG_REM_GRD_FG_SIZ (supporting), linked to evidence SVCs_P_Liang2023_4; multi-tier vegetation intervention targeting IND_GVI under PRF_AES / PRS_AES_QUAL to reverse the descriptive cluster-shift-based perceptual decline identified across spatio-temporal periods
scope_validation_notes: Scope structure is internally consistent with signature_count and phase_count.

pathway.mechanism_description: Adding vegetation to street-level environments increases the proportion of green pixels in multi-temporal street-view imagery aggregated to hexagonal spatial units. Over time, increased vegetation presence in the visual environment contributes to improved visual environment quality scores, reflecting the role of greenery as a stable positive component in the urban visual landscape that enhances perceived naturalness and visual diversity.

performance_effect.expected_change_note: Descriptive evidence only; directional effect on Cluster Shift (Perceptual Decline) is not established.

operation.description: Moderate building visual dominance along riverfront by integrating vegetation and open sky views to balance the visual composition of built elements

pathway.mechanism_description: Building view index (BVI) contributes to the comprehensive visual perception score and six perceptual dimensions (safe, lively, beautiful, boring, wealthy, depressing). The visible volume of built elements influences subjective perceptions predicted by deep-learning models. Moderating BVI while increasing GVI and maintaining WVI can shift the perceptual profile toward higher scores on positive dimensions (beautiful, lively, safe) and lower scores on negative dimensions (boring, depressing), consistent with the descriptive evidence about BVI's contribution to riverfront perception.

signatures.mechanism: Adding canopy trees along approach streets increases the Green View Index (GVI) as perceived in the pedestrian's forward-looking visual field. Higher GVI enhances visual comfort, shade provision, and spatial enclosure, which collectively raise the Street Safety Index (Visual) by signalling a well-maintained, walkable environment. This positive shift in perceived safety and visual appeal encourages pedestrian activity and willingness to visit, supporting social vitality along riverfront roads.

scope.description: A single-signature intervention (SIG_ADD_VEG_MG_SIZ) that increases the amount of street-tree canopy along approach routes to urban parks. Linked evidence SVCs_P_Zhang2024_9 is descriptive (DIR_NA): it reports that the Building View Index (IND_BVI) contributes to subjective perception scores (safe, lively, beautiful, boring, wealthy, depressing) predicted by deep learning for riverfront road landscape in Guangzhou, within the performance dimension PRF_AES (Aesthetic Preference, subdimension PRS_AES_QUAL). Because the evidence is descriptive, no directional effect is established. By adding canopy trees at the mid-ground tier, the intervention may reduce visible building dominance (IND_BVI) in the pedestrian's visual field, potentially altering the overall visual quality of riverfront streets.
scope_validation_notes: Scope structure is internally consistent with signature_count and phase_count.

translation_signature_schema.signature_order: Operation × Semantic Layer × Spatial Layer × Morphological Layer

signatures.mechanism: Adding canopy trees along approach streets increases the Green View Index (GVI) as perceived in the pedestrian's forward-looking visual field. Higher GVI enhances visual comfort, shade provision, and spatial enclosure, which collectively raise the Street Safety Index (Visual) by signalling a well-maintained, walkable environment. This positive shift in perceived safety and visual appeal encourages pedestrian activity and willingness to visit, supporting social vitality along riverfront roads.

scope.description: A single-signature intervention (SIG_ADD_VEG_MG_SIZ) that increases the amount of street-tree canopy along approach routes to urban parks. Linked evidence SVCs_P_Zhang2024_9 is descriptive (DIR_NA): it reports that the Building View Index (IND_BVI) contributes to subjective perception scores (safe, lively, beautiful, boring, wealthy, depressing) predicted by deep learning for riverfront road landscape in Guangzhou, within the performance dimension PRF_AES (Aesthetic Preference, subdimension PRS_AES_QUAL). Because the evidence is descriptive, no directional effect is established. By adding canopy trees at the mid-ground tier, the intervention may reduce visible building dominance (IND_BVI) in the pedestrian's visual field, potentially altering the overall visual quality of riverfront streets.

operation.description: Operation paired with evidence SVCs_P_Zhao2024_NEW2: modulate IND_SVF to influence Place Pulse 2.0 six-dimension perceptions (beautiful/boring/depressing/lively/safe/wealthy) predicted by SegFormer-B5+ConvNeXt-B+RF

pathway.mechanism_description: Higher Sky View Factor makes a negative SHAP contribution to overall positive Place Pulse 2.0 perceptions (beautiful/lively/safe/wealthy) and a positive contribution to boring/depressing, suggesting the model has learned that very open, sky-dominated scenes are rated less favorably; this is a predictive association rather than a tested causal effect.

operation.description: Add diverse vegetation types with varied structural forms (trees, shrubs, groundcovers) in different vertical layers to create heterogeneous street greenery composition in the middleground zone

pathway.mechanism_description: Incorporating multiple vegetation layers with distinct shapes and heights increases structural complexity and diversity indices by creating varied spatial arrangements, enhancing vertical stratification and habitat heterogeneity along street corridors

signatures.mechanism: Introduces the upper canopy layer, contributing the dominant vertical stratum that increases Vegetation Structural Diversity (VSD) by raising the number of distinct vegetation types (N) and altering the proportional composition (pi) in the Shannon-based VSD formula.

Introduces the middle understory layer beneath the tree canopy, adding a second vegetation type that increases N in the VSD calculation and creates the multi-layered 'trees over shrubs' combination identified in the evidence as aesthetically preferred.

Introduces the ground-plane herbaceous layer (lawns/groundcovers), maximising N to 3 and thereby pushing VSD toward its theoretical maximum, completing the three-tier vertical heterogeneity visible at eye-level in street-view imagery.

scope.description: SCP_S2 coordinates three superimposed signatures—SIG_ADD_VEG_MG_SIZ (canopy_tree), SIG_ADD_VEG_MG_SIZ (shrub), and SIG_ADD_VEG_MG_SIZ (ground_cover)—that together vertically layer upper, middle, and ground-plane vegetation in the middleground zone. Evidence SVCs_P_Tong2020_3 links this composite intervention to the Vegetation Structural Diversity indicator (IND_VSD), a Shannon-based index computed from the proportional presence of trees, shrubs, and lawns (N up to 3). The study reports descriptive means of VSD = 0.6154 (New City) versus 0.5513 (Old City), showing that streets with more complete multi-layer planting achieve higher structural diversity. In the performance dimension PRF_AES / PRS_AES_RICH, higher VSD is associated with richer, more complex streetscape compositions that residents aesthetically prefer over single-layer plantings (Excerpt 17). Single-type streets (e.g., trees alone on branch roads, Excerpt 10) score lower on VSD; the coordinated addition of all three strata is therefore required to shift VSD upward. The superposition pattern captures the vertical stacking logic: each signature occupies a distinct height band yet shares the same plan-view tier, jointly producing the heterogeneous greenery composition described in the IOM.
scope_validation_notes: Scope structure is internally consistent with signature_count and phase_count.

operation.description: Implement vertical greening systems (green walls, climbing plants on facades) and modular box planting (elevated planters, container gardens) along branch roads in high-density old city districts where horizontal planting space is constrained by narrow sidewalks and utility infrastructure.

pathway.mechanism_description: Addition of vertical and containerized vegetation in the foreground tier increases the visible green surface area within the street canyon's visual field, directly elevating Green View Index (GVI) measurements captured from pedestrian eye-level perspectives. This strategy compensates for limited ground-level planting space by expanding greenery into the vertical dimension of building facades and deploying portable planting systems that maximize greenery density per unit of available horizontal space.

signatures.mechanism: Adding climbing plants on building facades introduces vertical green surface at and above eye level on narrow branch roads, directly increasing the proportion of vegetation pixels captured in street-level imagery and thus raising the Green View Index (GVI).

Adding shrubs and herbs in modular box planters supplements lower-level greenery (as recommended in Excerpt [3]) that contributes additional green-pixel area to the GVI calculation, compensating for the absence of ground-level planting beds on constrained sidewalks.

Elevated planters and container garden structures provide the physical substrate enabling vegetation establishment where in-ground planting is precluded by narrow sidewalks and subsurface utility infrastructure; they do not directly alter GVI but are a necessary enabling component.

scope.description: This is an SCP_S2 coordinated intervention using a superposition pattern: two layers of newly introduced vegetation (SIG_ADD_VEG_FG_SIZ as climbing_plant and as shrub) are superimposed within the same foreground zone, supported by added built infrastructure (SIG_ADD_BLT_FG_SIZ as furniture/planters). Evidence SVCs_P_Tong2020_4 documents that the Green View Index (IND_GVI) on old-city branch roads currently averages only 0.152, substantially below the new-city branch-road mean of 0.196. The intervention targets the performance dimension PRF_AES, subdimension PRS_AES_NATU (perceived naturalness). By simultaneously introducing facade-mounted climbing plants (vertical greening) and elevated modular box planting (lower-level shrubs and herbs), the combined green-pixel area in pedestrian-level views is expected to increase, raising GVI toward or beyond the new-city benchmark. The outcome direction is positive (increased GVI → enhanced street greenery perception), though the evidence itself is descriptive-diagnostic (DIR_NA) rather than experimentally causal.
scope_validation_notes: Scope structure is internally consistent with signature_count and phase_count.

performance_effect.expected_change_note: Descriptive evidence only; directional effect on Street Greenery Level on Branch Roads is not established.

operation.description: Increase foreground tree coverage and density to elevate Green View Index (GVI) levels, transforming low-vegetation urban corridors ('Urban Jungle' typology) into greener streetscapes ('Flanked by Nature' or 'Boulevard' typologies) through strategic planting of street trees along pedestrian sight lines.

pathway.mechanism_description: Higher foreground tree presence is positively correlated with Visual Beauty Score (Place Pulse), plausibly because trees dominate the immediate pedestrian visual cone; the relationship is correlational and other co-varying factors may contribute.

signatures.mechanism: Planting street trees along pedestrian sight lines increases the proportion of vegetation pixels captured in street-level imagery, directly elevating the Green View Index (GVI). Higher canopy coverage transforms the visual composition from built-dominated ('Urban Jungle', Cluster 1 — highest construction view factor, lowest vegetation) toward vegetation-rich typologies ('Flanked by Nature', Cluster 2 — highest green view factor), thereby raising perceived visual beauty.

scope.description: A single-signature intervention SIG_ADD_VEG_FG_SIZ that adds canopy trees in the foreground tier to increase vegetative coverage and density within pedestrian sight lines. Linked evidence SVCs_P_Liang2023_1 demonstrates that elevating the Green View Index (IND_GVI) has a positive relationship (DIR_POS, r = 0.66) with the performance dimension PRF_AES (Aesthetic Preference, subdimension PRS_AES_PREF), measured via the Visual Beauty Score (Place Pulse). The intervention shifts streetscape classification from low-greenery clusters (Cluster 1: 'Urban Jungle') toward high-greenery clusters (Cluster 2: 'Flanked by Nature' or Cluster 6: 'Boulevard') by increasing the vegetation view factor at the expense of built-environment dominance, directly improving perceived visual quality.
scope_validation_notes: Scope structure is internally consistent with signature_count and phase_count.

performance_effect.expected_change_note: May increase Visual Beauty Score (Place Pulse)
